column2: The key skills for a modern digital economy are AI literacy, practical digital skills, and the ability to adapt as technology changes. Canada needs workers who can both build AI and use it in everyday business. That means programs that teach Canadians how to actually use the technology, not just academic courses. Short applied training, reskilling for mid-career workers, and incentives for companies to train staff on the job are needed.
column3: AI literacy has to reach all ages. Kids, adults, and elderly people all need to understand what AI is, how it can be used, and how it can be used against them. This means integrating AI education into schools, creating simple workplace training for employees, and public campaigns that explain both the benefits and the risks in plain language. Awareness of bias and limitations should be part of every program so people do not see AI as a black box. The goal is not just training experts but making
column4: Canada can ensure equitable access to AI literacy by making programs available both virtually and physically across the country. These programs should provide certificates on AI knowledge so participants can show practical skills to employers. column3: Enhancing AI literacy means embedding digital and AI education in schools, colleges, and workplaces, with focus on how AI works, its benefits, limits, and biases. column4: Equitable access requires meeting people where they are. column2: Canada can best support  by investing in accessible, inclusive training programs, especially for adult learners, that integrate both AI-focused careers and broader workforce readiness. Adult literacy support is essential in this process as it forms the base for digital skill acquisition and meaningful participation in the digital economy.
column3: To enhance AI literacy in Canada, a multi-faceted approach is needed including national public awareness campaigns, workplace training programs, and accessible credentials that emphasize AI's limitations, ethical use, and biases. Adult literacy is foundational in this process, as strong literacy skills enable individuals to critically evaluate and engage responsibly with AI technology. Programs should be designed to reach marginalized communities and integrate culturally relevant content.
column4: Canada can ensure equitable access to AI literacy by coordinating national and provincial strategies, partnering with industry to fund training hubs, developing plain-language materials, and embedding AI literacy in adult education programs. Collaboration with all government levels, accessible infrastructure, and culturally relevant content are essential to reaching marginalized communities. Adult literacy is core, empowering Canadians to confidently and critically use AI tools.

column2: Beyond technical AI/ML skills, Canada's workforce needs strong data literacy, critical thinking to evaluate AI outputs, and human-AI collaboration skills to work effectively alongside automated systems. Expand micro-credential programs through colleges and polytechnics offering 3-6 month certificates in practical AI applications for specific sectors - healthcare workers learning diagnostic AI tools, manufacturers understanding predictive maintenance, or accountants using AI audit systems. Create tax-deductible "Learning Accounts" where individuals and employers can contribute funds for continuous reskilling, recognizing that AI will require career-long adaptation rather than one-time training. Partner with major employers to identify emerging skill gaps and co-develop training programs that guarantee job interviews for graduates, ensuring education aligns with real labor market needs. Mandate that all post-secondary programs include AI literacy components relevant to their field, preparing teachers to use educational AI, lawyers to understand algorithmic evidence, and tradespeople to work with AI-assisted equipment.
column3: Develop a national AI competency framework similar to language proficiency levels (basic, intermediate, advanced) with standardized assessments that employers can recognize and individuals can progressively achieve. Create workplace AI ambassador programs where tech-savvy employees receive training to become internal AI literacy champions, cascading knowledge through peer-to-peer learning rather than top-down training. Launch public awareness campaigns that demonstrate AI's actual capabilities versus science fiction myths, using concrete examples like "AI can help detect cancer but can't read your thoughts." Integrate AI literacy into existing digital skills programs at Service Canada centers, public libraries, and Indigenous friendship centers, meeting people where they already seek services. Partner with professional associations to develop sector-specific AI ethics training - engineers learning about algorithmic bias, healthcare workers understanding AI decision limitations, and educators recognizing AI-generated content.
column4: Provide free high-speed internet and devices to remote and Indigenous communities through expanded Universal Broadband Fund programs, recognizing digital infrastructure as prerequisite for AI literacy. Create mobile AI literacy labs that travel to rural communities, elder care facilities, and underserved neighborhoods with hands-on demonstrations and locally relevant training. Develop AI education materials in French, Indigenous languages, and major immigrant languages, ensuring newcomers and linguistic minorities aren't excluded from AI opportunities. Partner with community organizations already serving vulnerable populations - food banks, employment centers, settlement agencies - to embed AI literacy into existing support programs. Establish AI literacy grants specifically for groups facing systemic barriers, covering not just training costs but childcare, transportation, and lost wages during education, removing practical barriers to participation.

column2: Computer literacy, building-block skills like math, creativity (arts) and physical health. It may seem odd to include the latter but everything is interwoven. Teach people to work with those of all abilities, backgrounds and ideas before teaching them about AI and we'll be ready to evolve when AI does beyond how you see it now.
column3: Put the same way of training across all disciplines - if I learn how to build a car the same way I build AI I'll much easier understand AI from my own unique way of thinking. This is how football players learn physics.

Don't think top-down, put literacy in existing structures, which will build trust by showing you have a desire to learn about your own customers/citizens at their level.

column2: Fully fund universities and colleges. Create a K12 & post-secondary media literacy curriculum. Use existing government facilities, including Canada Post, Service Canada, and under-used federal buildings for "test and try" new tech, education for SME, maker centres, and similar. Fund post-secondary courses that draw together students, researchers, SME to discuss and address real-world problems. Allow SME to participate in "startup" incubators with small amounts. Expand CBC locally. Canada is currently exporting a huge portion of its tech graduates - get them to stay in the country by offering jobs. At or near US rates. But the "modern, digital economy" actually needs more arts & social sciences - ethics, content, social impact, child development, privacy, equity. And since industry isn't about to regulate itself, or consider social impacts, that's what the government should be specializing in. Another reason to bring in the CBC and certified journalists. Also medical ethics is a good grounding. Canada can really differentiate its "startup" economy by focusing on creating businesses that plan for, and remain for the long-term. The US "startup" economy is all about making money quickly - the investors want fast profits and to sell off quickly any business that succeeds. Canada should concentrate on "long-term" investments, 5-50 years, small amounts invested, government investment, investments from crown corporations and pension funds, while avoiding and de-incentivizing the millionaire class (Weston, McCain, Bill Gates foundation, etc) from being involved. They created the big tech bubble of the early 2000s, and AI is a bubble about to burst - at great cost to everyone except the millionaires.
column3:
Canada should 
- seriously educate public about media literacy, disinformation & fact-checking, as Finland does: https://finland.fi/life-society/educated-decisions-finnish-media-literacy-deters-disinformation/
- pass legislation to prevent politicians from lying and against using bots, trolls, or other automated amplification methods
- actively pursue & prevent weaponized disinfo, by foreign and domestic entities (see Poilievre who seems to be using bots to push disinfo, leaving the public seriously misinformed about critical topics, like the safety of vaccines)
- legislate Canadian ownership of news media
- update the mandate under-lying CSIS (and the EA act), as they have requested for years to cover cyber-crime, internet, social media, disinformation, domestic terrorism / extremism (e.g., Proud Boys) and attacks on our government and people that are not small militias with arms ("threats or the use of serious violence to persons or property") - our current practice of relying on largely volunteer organizations and independent news media (The Trillium, The Narwhal, Anti-Hate.ca) to track IMVE (Ideologically Motivated Violent Extremism) and Russian financing / disinfo (e.g., Tenet media) leaves Canada vulnerable to more bridge closures / city sieges, and legally and illegally financed "influencers" and algorithmic attacks and amplification (Twitter / X, Canada Proud, Rebel media, etc.) - all of which AI exasperates
REFERENCES
- https://epaper.ottawacitizen.com/article/281852942573986 (Outdated rules hamper CSIS Brief, Ottawa Citizen "e-paper", 
- http://www.sirc-csars.gc.ca/csiscr/amdmod-eng.html (Amending the CSIS Act, SIRC, gc.ca)
- https://journals.lib.sfu.ca/index.php/jicw/article/view/5101 (Russia's Role in the Far-Right Truck Convoy: An analysis of Russian state media activity related to the 2022 Freedom Convoy, 2023-01-31, Caroline Orr Bueno
column4:
Canada is undergoing a serious mis/disinformation campaign by foreign (and domestic) actors, exacerbated by AI. Polling shows that belief in disinformation is highly correlated with right-wing leanings, in part because the right-wing is actively pushing mis/disinformation through its social media, newsletters, email, and paid PR firms (masquerading as news, such as Western Standard, Canada Proud, Rebel, etc., not to mention the entire news media empire of Post Media), such as lack of trust in vaccines, demonization of teachers / public education, etc  . No "education" about AI, or tech in general, will be effective, so long as these mis/disinformation channels remain in place. News media must be regulated - Canadian ownership, truth in news, journalist credentialing, and, like restaurants, visible scores on trustworthiness / factual-basis. Along with requiring politicians to not lie, and enforcing Elections Canada and other bodies to prevent use of bots and algorithms to push viewpoints. It's impossible to discuss education without addressing the current right-wing push to destroy public education in Canada - there's no use relying on universities and colleges, if they no longer exist. See Alberta and Ontario especially - where K12 and post-secondary education is massively under-funded, Ford has put education under political control
- See Finland media literacy: https://finland.fi/life-society/educated-decisions-finnish-media-literacy-deters-disinformation/
- Ensure provinces fully fund healthcare and education - demand transparency, have consequences 
- https://www150.statcan.gc.ca/n1/pub/75-006-x/2025001/article/00008-eng.htm (Insights on Canadian Society:
Concern about misinformation: Connections to trust in media, confidence in institutions, civic engagement, and hopefulness, StatsCan, June 2025)
- https://mediapolicy.ca/2025/01/01/how-much-online-hate-and-misinformation-do-canadians-see-do-your-politics-matter/

column2:
Canada needs a skills continuum: universal digital fluency (devices, data hygiene, privacy, basic analytics), “hybrid” business skills (communication, teamwork, change), and advanced tracks in software, data, cloud, cybersecurity, MLOps and AI safety/evaluation. The public sector should convene employers, colleges/universities and institutes (Vector/Mila/Amii) to co-design micro-credentials and work-integrated learning, expand re-skilling grants and apprenticeships, and align immigration and credential recognition to priority roles. Funding should favour programs that place graduates in Canadian firms and public agencies using Canadian-controlled data/compute.

AI literacy should be civic, workplace and technical. Federally led, bilingual resources (K-12 modules, college updates, public-service training, library/community workshops) must explain what AI can and cannot do, including bias, privacy, hallucinations and security risks, with simple ways to verify outputs and escalate concerns. Employers should adopt short, stackable credentials in responsible AI, data protection and model oversight; regulators can recognise them in compliance guidance. Public pilots in health, transport and benefits should publish plain-language notices, evaluations and outcomes so Canadians see safeguards and benefits in practice.

Trust and uptake depend on access. Ottawa, provinces and Indigenous partners should pair broadband/device programs with local delivery through schools, libraries, settlement agencies and Friendship Centres, offering materials in French and, where appropriate, Indigenous languages. Targeted supports—travel/childcare stipends, paid micro-internships, and loaner laptops/connectivity—can include rural, low-income, newcomer, older and disabled learners. Data governance must respect OCAP/CARE where Indigenous data is involved. A single federal-provincial dashboard should track participation, completion, job placement and regional coverage to steer funding to gaps.
column3:
Canada should treat AI literacy as a civic, workplace, and technical capability. Civic literacy equips people to recognize where AI is used, what rights they have, and how to seek recourse. Workplace literacy enables safe, effective use of AI tools in daily tasks. Technical literacy develops stewards who can evaluate models, detect bias, and manage risk. Across all three, Canadians must understand AI’s limits: hallucinations, bias in data and outputs, privacy/security risks, provenance of content, and the difference between assistance and automated decision-making.

The government can lead with a national, bilingual AI literacy framework that provinces, territories, and school boards can adopt and adapt. Core elements should include age-appropriate K–12 modules, TVET and college updates, and mandatory public-service training for high-impact uses. For employers—especially SMEs—Ottawa should fund ready-to-use toolkits (policies, checklists, prompt and data-handling guides) and promote stackable micro-credentials in responsible AI, model oversight, privacy, and cybersecurity, recognized by regulators in guidance and procurement. Public-sector deployments should model best practice: plain-language notices, risk/benefit summaries, and links to human review and complaints, so Canadians see safeguards working in real services.

Public engagement should be continuous and inclusive. Libraries, settlement agencies, Friendship Centres, and community colleges can host free workshops and clinics; materials should be available in French and Indigenous languages. Targeted campaigns should pair media literacy with myth-busting on AI’s capabilities and limits, using real Canadian examples from health, transport, and benefits delivery. Finally, an open national dashboard should track reach and outcomes (participation, credentials earned, complaints resolved), allowing governments to redirect funding to underserved regions and groups and to keep programs current as technology evolves.
column4:
Equity starts with access. The federal government should pair universal, affordable connectivity and devices with locally delivered learning. Broadband build-outs and low-cost plans must be matched by device programs and secure public access points in schools, libraries, Friendship Centres and community hubs, so rural, remote and low-income learners can actually participate. Materials should be bilingual and, where appropriate, available in Indigenous languages and accessible formats (captioned video, screen-reader friendly, plain-language summaries).

Delivery must be community-led. Provinces, territories, municipalities and Indigenous governments should co-design curricula and workshops that reflect local needs, with funding that enables trusted partners—schools, colleges, libraries, settlement agencies, disability organizations and youth groups—to host free, recurring sessions. Programs should remove practical barriers through stipends for childcare and travel, paid micro-internships, and loaner laptops and hotspots. Content must cover both “how to use AI” and “how to question it,” including bias, privacy, security, provenance and routes to human review.

Employers and the public sector can widen the funnel by recognizing stackable micro-credentials in hiring and procurement, and by offering paid release time for workers to upskill. Crown corporations and major departments should model best practice by publishing plain-language notices and evaluations in public services, turning real deployments into learning opportunities. Finally, a national equity dashboard—co-managed with provinces and Indigenous partners—should track participation, completion, job placement and regional coverage, so funding moves to where gaps persist and programs evolve with community feedback.

This approach treats AI literacy as essential public infrastructure—delivered locally, inclusive by design, and backed by measurable accountability.

column2: Workers need skills in data literacy, AI use, prompt engineering, and critical thinking, alongside resilience and lifelong learning. Canada should expand retraining programs, create certification pathways, and offer grants or tax credits so small businesses and one-person corporations can access training. Just as important, AI awareness and digital skills should start early in elementary and high school to prepare the next generation for a transformed economy.
column3: AI literacy should start in schools and continue into workplaces. Students and workers need to understand not only how to use AI, but also its risks, limitations, and biases. Training must include the ability to detect provenance using standards like C2PA so citizens and businesses can distinguish authentic from manipulated content. Grants and tax credits should make this training affordable for small businesses and sole proprietors.
column4: AI literacy should be treated as a baseline skill, delivered through schools, public libraries, and community centres across all regions. Programs must be bilingual, accessible, and designed for lower-income and rural communities. Training should also cover content provenance standards like C2PA so everyone, not just experts, can verify authenticity. Grants and tax credits for small firms and one-person corporations will ensure the majority of Canadian businesses are not left behind.

column2: Modern digital economy skills include AI literacy, data analysis, digital collaboration, and adaptable problem-solving. Canada can support development by scaling micro-credentials, bridging programs for internationally trained professionals, co-op placements, and continuous upskilling through pre-arrival and settlement agencies, ensuring workers rapidly adapt to technology while meeting both AI-focused and broader workforce needs.
column3: AI literacy can be enhanced through workplace training, micro-credentials, and pre-arrival or settlement programs that teach capabilities, limitations, and biases. Public campaigns, community workshops, and school curricula increase awareness, while sharing international best practices ensures ethical, inclusive adoption. Success is measured by broader informed usage, reduced bias incidents, and higher trust in AI systems across workplaces and communities.
column4: Canada can ensure equitable AI literacy by partnering with provincial and municipal governments, industry, and non-profits to deliver accessible training and resources. Programs should include online micro-credentials, community workshops, and pre-arrival settlement support, targeting newcomers, underserved regions, and lower-income populations. Success is measured by participation rates, skill attainment, and increased confidence in using AI tools across diverse communities.

column2: Key skills for a modern digital economy include data literacy, digital problem-solving, AI and coding basics, critical thinking, and adaptability. Canada should support development by expanding access to digital training, micro-credentials, and lifelong learning programs ensuring both AI specialists and the broader workforce can adapt quickly to change. Close industry-education partnerships, co-op placements, and support for reskilling will help workers at all stages keep pace with evolving tech
column3: Enhance AI literacy with workplace training, public awareness campaigns, and easy-to-access credentials that include AI’s limits and biases. Use global best practices and tailor outreach for diverse communities.
column4: Canada should offer AI literacy programs nationwide through schools, libraries, and community centres, partnering with provincial governments, industry, and non-profits. Subsidize digital tools and training in underserved areas, and deliver materials in multiple languages and formats. Involve private sector experts in hands-on learning and mentorship to reach all regions and backgrounds.

column2:
Technical skills, yes. Also: critical thinking about AI limitations, recognizing bias, knowing when AI shouldn't be used.
Missing: workers being consulted. "Enabling rapid adaptation" - adaptation to what? Changes imposed without their input?
Programs need: worker transition support, retraining with income protection, union involvement in workplace AI, worker rights education.
Can't "support workforce readiness" without asking the workforce.
column3:
Can't teach about limitations and biases without experts on your task force. You have zero AI ethicists.
AI literacy should include: recognizing bias, challenging AI decisions, rights and protections, system limitations, demanding transparency. Not just "how to use AI tools."
Best practices: involve ethics experts in literacy programs. Otherwise it's marketing.
column4:
Equitable literacy is meaningless if AI systems aren't equitable. Teaching people about AI that discriminates against them isn't equity.
Needed: affected communities deciding what literacy means for them, not top-down.
Private sector: fund programs without controlling curriculum. No industry-written "education" that's really marketing.
Community-based programs, Indigenous-led initiatives, multilingual resources.
First question: are you building equitable AI systems?

column3: -create national learning modules that can be built into courses in K-12 and post-secondary as well as workplaces

This needs to be worked into curriculum from an early age - teaching the pros and cons of AI in schools simultaneously is imperative to create understanding from the next generation.

Workplace and public awareness campaigns would also be effective. I would suggest a modern take on the 'House Hippo' campaign of 1999 - shown as PSA's on children's and adult programming - as well as social media platforms
Prioritize awareness of AI mis/disinformation on social media. Target these ads to young people (sub-30) and those 50+.

column2:
These are less about skill and more about personal attributes that will allow people to flourish in the age of AI integration:
Courage, resilience, willingness to learn new things, ability to create, common sense perspective that questions when things seem wrong, critical thinking, intuition, creativity and imagination, openness to collaborating with an electronic being

To support an AI-enabled workforce I believe it is important that people get a "look under the hood", and conceptually understand how AI works, and a little about the history of how AI started and has evolved since the 1950s.

It is also important for people to understand what AI can DO, and where progress on the technology is headed so they can reimagine their future workforce role.
column3:
- Educate K-12 students and teachers, principals, Board members - our kids are our future tech gurus, their eyes will see more AI advancements than our eyes.

- public awareness campaign
- regional targeted information sessions
- executive training for leaders in all organizations 
- establish regular cadence of conferences organized regionally and/or by industry - not the flashy conferences organized by big tech, ones in which there are credible speakers that are there to promote AI literacy and adoption with an awareness of limitations/biases

column2: Canada needs skills in AI literacy, ethics, and digital creativity, alongside technical expertise. That should be part of our schools' curriculum. By funding lifelong learning and practical programs, Canada can build a workforce ready for both opportunity and change.
column3: Canada can enhance AI literacy by funding public education campaigns, workplace training, and school programs that explain both AI’s uses and its limits. People need to understand how bias, misinformation, and misuse can creep in, and how to question AI outputs critically. If Canadians learn early and often that AI is a tool  --not necessarily a truth machine, we’ll build informed confidence and responsible use.
column4: Canada should ensure equitable AI literacy by making programs accessible across regions and income levels, not just in major cities. Public broadcasters like CBC and TVO can play key roles in delivering trustworthy, easy-to-understand education on AI’s benefits and risks. Partnering government, schools, industry, and media ensures no group is left behind in building AI awareness.

column2:
Media literacy should be a class taught as early as elementary school. Knowing how to discern truth from misinformation and knowing to check the source of something being said to ensure it's not all coming from one person's bald assertions are skills essential to today's digital life and far too uncommon. 

Basic computer programing should be taught early and often as well.
column3: Ads on services like Instagram, Tik Tok, and Youtube to inform people on what to trust and what not to trust would hit wide audiences. As mentioned above this should also be part of media literacy classes in our schools, and should also be offered to companies as workplace training.
column4: Go directly to groups that are traditionally underserved in such things and ask them what they need, and what would work best for them, and how best they would engage. A website with the course materials on it would be a good start, but in some more remote communities with poorer internet access, physical documents may better serve. Group classes made available at community and recreation centers would be one idea to spread awareness, but again, those may not work for everyone.

column3: There needs to be massive training and oversight regarding how biased and unreliable a lot of accessible AI is.

column2: This is very hard to know right now. It is very likely that the ways in which we interface with AI will change rapidly over the coming years; "chatGPT-like" interfaces may be replaced just as quickly as they appeared. It's possible that education and experience will become less important, and that significant amounts of white-collar office and creative work will disappear as it cannot compete on cost with AI. The most important skills going forward may be childcare and skilled manual labour.
column3: In the '90s and 2000s, the Canadian government put out very effective advertising campaigns (eg. heritage minutes). I'm confident that it can do so on this issue as well. Run ads where Canadians are watching them -- on YouTube and TikTok. Publicize examples in which AI hallucinates false answers or cites nonexistent sources. And discuss public policy about the potential for harms & existential risks posed by AI. Interview prominent experts on their opinions; run recurring discussions on the CBC.
column4: This will need the participation of the provinces, because it needs to become part of the high school curriculum, or even elementary school. AI literacy is going to become as fundamental of a skill as computer literacy or typing. Be careful with leaning on the private sector too much here; AI literacy should not focus on how to use any particular company's products. Build educational examples around open-source tools and models wherever possible.

column2: A modern digital economy needs skills in AI, data, cybersecurity, cloud, adaptability, and ethics. Canada can scale STEM and digital training, expand reskilling, and embed AI literacy across education. Partnerships with academia, industry, and government should align programs with business needs, while fast-track training, lifelong learning incentives, and inclusive access ensure workers adapt and thrive.
column3: AI literacy in Canada must cover both skills and awareness of limits and biases. Workplace training and micro-credentials can prepare employees to use AI responsibly, while schools and universities embed literacy across disciplines. Public campaigns should make risks and safeguards clear, and Canada can adopt global best practices like EU transparency rules. Combining education, training, and outreach will foster informed, responsible AI adoption nationwide.
column4: Canada can ensure equitable AI literacy by embedding digital education in schools, offering subsidized training for underrepresented groups, and expanding access in rural and remote areas through online platforms. Collaboration with provinces, Indigenous communities, and municipalities is key, while industry contributes resources, mentorship, and tools. Public campaigns and inclusive curricula can make AI literacy accessible nationwide, ensuring no group is left behind.

Building equitable access to AI literacy means giving Canadians coast to coast the chance to learn about and use AI tools in practical ways. Hackathons like the aforementioned NASA International Space Apps challenge are extremely inclusive and support participation from under represented groups. They can be performed in person or virtually, therefore are available to anyone in the country. This is why I recommend increased federal government support for this event to accelerate AI adoption.

column2:
Canada should invest in training that includes digital, creative, and ethical practice skills, with a
focus on how AI works. AI should be rolled out with Consent, Compensation, and Control at the 
core of its intent. We need funding for union-led training hubs, fast re-skilling programs, and co-ops
that connect technology, arts, and trades, so that everyone can adapt in a fair and respectful way.
column3:
To enhance AI literacy in Canada, governments should engage unions and communities as well as businesses in
identifying risks and needs of workers. Invest in workplace training, short courses (e.g., microcredentials), and public campaigns that explain how AI works, where it can go wrong, and its
benefits. Literacy empowers all workers to use AI confidently, effectively, and challenge AI when it’s
used in ways that undermine fairness
column4:
To ensure fair access to AI literacy, Canada must listen to underserved groups about barriers.
Invest in libraries, schools, and town halls as learning hubs. Protect access by requiring industry
co-funding, and enshrine Consent, Compensation, and Control in all programs. Equitable access
means that rural, racialized, Indigenous, and low-income communities have equal opportunities to
learn and lead in the AI era.

column2: The federal government should regulate all provincial departments of education since they have been massively underfunded for the last several decades, and should be focusing on stem skills.

Critical thinking, systems thinking, life-long learning, media literacy, etc. Hard skills are useful, but they are fleeting. To keep pace, we will need to develop the skills necessary to remain curious, learn quickly and seek out the correct information. We need to develop a generation of strong problem-solvers, considerate collaborators and curious learners.
column3: An emphasis on media literacy in schools. AI literacy is important for understanding AI technologies specifically, but media literacy will help address the wider impact of AI technologies. We need to teach people how to ask the right questions and seek out information from well-informed sources. AI has the potential to blur the line between truth and misinformation. That is its greatest threat. Don't forget that. And don't take it lightly.

Public education has the added benefit of bringing digital technologies into conversations at home. Time to end the XBox digital literacy I see as a teacher and start making digital about tools, not toys.
column3: By teaching the nuts and bolts. Digital isn't magic, but most people are oblivious to how it works even as they spend most of their time on these devices. Clear the ignorance around the ICT, networking, cloud and big data that enables AI and Canadians will grab it like the tool it is and use it.

column2: The current modern digital economy requires skills in using a multitude of apps, and being able to scale up knowledge on new apps quickly. But that’s not what AI needs. I’ve used AI. It requires very little skill. It can answer any question you throw at it within seconds. I’ve created PowerPoint presentations using AI in a mere fraction of the time it would take me without it. AI transcribes and summarizes all of my meetings and notes. It will require fewer skills. And this is bad.
column3: Honestly, don’t bother. It’s a losing battle. It’s like asking, how can we educate people about the bots and trolls and far right extremism that’s promoted by social media algorithms like X. Or, how can we educate people that the so-called news they rely on Facebook to feed them is misleading at best and usually worse, intentional lies and propaganda?  You can’t. If you could, you would have solved both those issues by now. Embracing AI, something infinitely more powerful, will be a disaster.

column3:
Increase transparency and accountability
In the absence of transparency, Canadians' trust in AI diminishes. People must be able to understand when and how AI is used, and who is responsible for its outcomes. 
Mandate disclosure of high-impact AI systems: Require both public and private sector organizations to clearly disclose when they are using high-impact AI systems that affect people's lives. This applies to areas like screening for jobs, evaluating credit applications, or making decisions in healthcare.
Ensure human oversight: A human-in-the-loop approach for high-impact decisions should be a standard. Studies show public comfort with AI-driven decisions increases significantly when human supervision is guaranteed.

column2:
Critical Skills for AI-Era Economy

Technical Tier:
- AI/ML engineering (build systems)
- Data engineering (pipelines, infrastructure)
- AI security/safety (adversarial robustness)
- Domain + AI hybrid (healthcare AI, climate AI)

Translator Tier
- Business analysts who understand AI capabilities
- Product managers who can design AI experiences
- Domain experts who can specify AI requirements
- Ethics/policy specialists who can govern AI

Foundational Tier
- AI literacy (what it can/can't do)
- Critical thinking (evaluate AI outputs)
- Prompt engineering (work with AI tools)
- Data interpretation (understand AI-generated insights)

Human-Advantage Tier
- Complex communication/negotiation
- Creative problem-solving
- Ethical judgment
- Cross-cultural collaboration

How Canada Develops These

K-12
- AI literacy mandatory (like digital literacy)
- Computational thinking (not just coding)
- Critical evaluation of AI content

Post-Secondary
- AI minor available in ALL programs (not just CS)
- "Translator" certificates (6-12 months, domain expert → AI capable)
- Industry co-op placements with AI companies

Workforce Retraining
- $5K/person training vouchers for AI upskilling
- 6-month "AI translator" bootcamps for mid-career professionals
- Employer tax credits for training (50% of training costs)
- Paid leave for retraining (EI equivalent during learning)

Industry Collaboration
- Apprenticeships at AI companies (learn while earning)
- Micro-credentials recognized by employers
- Company training programs subsidized 50%

Immigration
- Fast-track for AI specialists 
- Recognize foreign credentials rapidly
- Retain international students (auto-PR for AI graduates)

Critical Innovation - AI Application Engineers
- 500 hybrid roles: 50% university, 50% industry
- Bridge research to deployment
- Train next generation of translators
- Government funds $120-150K salaries

Key Insight: Shortage isn't just technical AI experts - it's people who can apply AI to real problems.
column3:
Multi-Layer AI Literacy Strategy
K-12 Foundation
Mandatory AI literacy curriculum (Grades 7-12)
Hands-on: Use AI tools, observe failures, understand limitations
Critical thinking: Identify bias, hallucinations, inappropriate use cases
Ethics scenarios: When should/shouldn't AI be used?

Public Education Campaign:
"Know Your AI" - like nutritional labels
TV/social media: Debunk myths, show limitations
Community workshops at libraries
Celebrity ambassadors (trusted voices, not just techies)

Professional Development:
Sector-specific training (healthcare workers, teachers, lawyers)
Focus on: Capabilities, limitations, bias detection, appropriate use
Mandatory for roles using AI systems
CEU credits for licensed professionals

Media/Journalism:
Train journalists on AI (responsible reporting)
Fact-checking AI claims (debunk hype)
Investigative reporting on AI harms/biases
Fund "AI beat" reporters at major outlets

Higher Education:
AI ethics mandatory in ALL programs (not just CS)
Case studies of AI failures/biases
Guest lectures from affected communities
Research-practice partnerships

Workplace Training:
Employer obligation: Train workers using AI systems
Understanding outputs, knowing when to override
Bias detection protocols
Whistleblower protections for raising concerns

Vulnerable Populations:
Seniors: Scam detection, deepfake awareness
Indigenous communities: Data sovereignty, cultural bias
Low-income: Access to AI literacy (not just elite)
Newcomers: Language-appropriate materials

Key Principles:
Show, don't tell (use AI, see it fail)
Context-specific (healthcare AI ≠ shopping AI)
Continuous (AI evolves, literacy must too)
Accessible (all languages, education levels)

Measurement:
Annual national AI literacy survey
Track understanding of: capabilities, limitations, bias, appropriate use
Adjust programs based on gaps

Success Metric: 80% of Canadians can identify AI use, understand basic limitations, recognize potential bias by 2030.

column2:
For a modern digital economy, essential skills include:
- Advanced digital literacy (data analysis, AI, cybersecurity, cloud computing)
- Critical thinking, problem-solving, creativity, adaptability, and emotional intelligence
- Leadership, collaboration, and ethical awareness for technology’s societal impact

Canada can best support these skills by:
- Embedding practical digital skills and AI training into post-secondary education and continuous learning programs tied to industry needs
- Expanding flexible pathways including on-the-job training, co-op programs, and lifelong learning
- Coordinating efforts between employers, government, and education providers to address skills gaps and ensure workforce readiness
- Offering specialized programs for AI-focused careers (e.g., AI research internships, accelerators) alongside broader workforce digital literacy initiatives
column3:
To enhance AI literacy in Canada, including understanding AI’s limitations and biases, several strategies are key:

- Workplace Training and Credentials: Develop targeted training programs and certifications focused on AI fundamentals, ethical use, and bias mitigation for workers across sectors. Encourage employer-led continuous learning and integration of AI literacy into professional development.
- Public Awareness Campaigns: Launch accessible government-backed campaigns to demystify AI, explain its benefits and risks, and raise awareness of ethical concerns and biases. Use multi-channel outreach including media, public workshops, and community engagement to reach diverse audiences.
- Inclusive and Targeted Engagements: Focus on underserved communities and sectors, ensuring AI literacy efforts address digital divides and equip users with critical thinking skills to navigate AI decisions responsibly.
- International Best Practices: Learn from countries with national AI literacy initiatives (e.g., UK’s AI Council education efforts, Singapore’s AI workforce readiness programs) by adapting their frameworks for local relevance.
- Collaboration Among Stakeholders: Foster partnerships among government, academia, industry, and civil society to co-create curricula, share resources, and sustain AI literacy momentum nationwide.

This multi-pronged approach builds a well-informed society capable of leveraging AI responsibly while recognizing its limits and potential biases.
column4:
Canada can ensure equitable access to AI literacy across regions, demographics, and socioeconomic groups through the following actions:
- Collaboration with All Government Levels: Foster partnerships between federal, provincial, and municipal governments to tailor AI literacy initiatives to local community needs, especially in rural, Indigenous, and underserved areas, ensuring resources reach diverse populations.
- Industry and Private Sector Involvement: Encourage businesses and tech companies to invest in inclusive AI training programs and digital infrastructure upgrades, offering accessible learning opportunities and apprenticeships to historically marginalized groups.
- Targeted Educational Strategies: Develop culturally relevant curricula and use multiple delivery modes (online, in-person, bilingual) to accommodate varied learning styles and language proficiency, while emphasizing foundational digital skills, critical thinking, and awareness of AI biases.
- Community Engagement and Support: Empower local organizations and community leaders to act as AI literacy champions, bridging trust gaps and delivering peer-led workshops to build informed confidence.
- Monitoring and Feedback: Establish metrics and ongoing consultation to assess access and effectiveness, allowing adjustments that close gaps and improve inclusion over time.

These coordinated, inclusive efforts ensure no Canadian is left behind in understanding and benefiting from AI technologies.

column2: As it relates to AI LLMs, the focus should be on prompting. There is an abundance of excellent knowledge present and available. There is also nothing wrong with governments pointing people to a YouTube video. This will enable a faster ramp-up of the whole of society. AI cannot be a tool just for the “next generation” if Canada is to be a leader. Canada can really shine if we open things up beyond LLMs by pushing many of the open models, source and freely accessible models that already exist.
column3: In schools, AI will need to be an ongoing part of the curriculum because it is constantly evolving. Today, AI is an LLM as an app on our phones and computers. Tomorrow? It needs to be used, not omitted. The AI LLMs alone have pushed us past the age of information into the age of intelligence. Examples to consider include developments in Estonia and Singapore, where their education AI initiatives are scheduled to launch in 2025 and 2026. For the masses, to ensure we maintain their dignity, if society moves past what are essentially documents on screens (web pages), to an interactive AI model (and who knows what after), we need to prevent a repeat of the transition from paper to computers. How many high-level CEO's didn't know basic typing skills at the time? The analogy might happen again. To deploy rapidly, we can again look to what's happening in Finland, Singapore, Germany, and even the USA.
column4: As an officially bilingual nation, with many other languages also calling our country home, Canada can be a leader in helping to push AI beyond its current English-dominated systems. Languages throughout the world are being lost due to a lack of use, and that is also the case in Canada. AI can be an opportunity not only to save our indigenous languages but also to expand them. One must recall that it's not just the words we're saving, but also the meaning and context behind them. For often the way of speaking is the way of thinking, and to deny our vast multitude of other languages and cultures is to deny ourselves. For this, Government need not to be - and should not be - the solution providor, but can be the one that sets the initiatives and benchmars that must be met. The government, meanwhile, should focus on solidifying all the datasets and expanding them to be multilingual. Where government financial support can be beneficial is through language lab funding, as seen in Finland. Those, though, would have the dual advantages of not only being sources of action but also serving as think tank knowledge hubs that provide benefits back to the local community.

Le Canada doit développer un ensemble de compétences hybrides : techniques (IA, données, calcul distribué), mais aussi critiques et culturelles, afin de comprendre les impacts, biais et usages sociaux de l’IA. Les formations doivent articuler science, art et société : intégration de modules sur la gouvernance des données, la propriété intellectuelle, les biais et la durabilité.
 Le gouvernement peut soutenir ce virage en finançant des ressources d’apprentissage critiques co-construites avec des organismes culturels et scientifiques, en valorisant les micro-certifications, les communautés de pratique et les laboratoires d’expérimentation.
 Ces dispositifs permettraient de renforcer la capacité d’adaptation de la main-d’œuvre face aux innovations rapides tout en ancrant l’IA dans une culture de responsabilité et d’inclusion.
column3:
Il faut développer une littératie élargie de l’IA, combinant compréhension technique et sensibilisation critique. La recherche-création joue ici un rôle clé : les artistes rendent visibles les biais, limites et implications des technologies, facilitant une compréhension publique concrète.
Le Canada devrait financer des programmes de formation en milieu de travail intégrant l’éthique, la gouvernance et les impacts environnementaux de l’IA, ainsi que des campagnes publiques s’appuyant sur des récits culturels accessibles.
Ces initiatives contribueraient à une appropriation citoyenne et professionnelle informée, condition essentielle d’une adoption responsable et durable de l’IA.
column4:
Assurer un accès équitable signifie rendre l’IA accessible partout et pour tous : régions, langues, milieux socioéconomiques. Le Canada doit reconnaître les centres culturels, universités régionales et OBNL comme acteurs légitimes du développement de compétences en IA.
Cela passe par un accès partagé à la puissance de calcul souveraine, la création de corpus culturels plurilingues (fr, en, autochtones) et des programmes de formation hybrides (ateliers, badges, ressources ouvertes).
Une gouvernance par les pairs — inspirée des communs numériques — garantirait une distribution équitable des ressources et des savoirs, tout en favorisant la participation citoyenne et la souveraineté culturelle.

column2: Skills for a modern digital economy: Canada needs two stacks: (1) AI-core skills—data engineering, MLOps, model evaluation/safety, privacy/security, human-factors design—and (2) AI-enabled skills—domain expertise (health, energy, finance, public services), product management, change management, and data-driven decision-making. The play is rapid upskilling and re-skilling: micro-credentials that stack into diplomas, paid co-ops/apprenticeships in SMEs and the public sector, national compute/data sandboxes for hands-on learning, and incentives for employers to train mid-career workers. Fund translational fellowships that place researchers inside hospitals, utilities, and municipalities, and tie grants to measurable adoption outcomes so learning turns into deployment.
column3: Boosting AI literacy (including limits and bias): Make literacy practical and scenario-based, not abstract. Every worker should know what AI can/can’t do, how to spot bias, when to escalate to a human, and how to handle data responsibly. Support this with short, role-specific modules (clinician, teacher, inspector, line manager), portable credentials recognized by provinces, and open “AI product labels” that explain model purpose, data, risks, and known failure modes. Borrow international best practice—NIST AI Risk Framework–style playbooks, ISO/IEC 42001 management systems—and localize with Canadian case studies (including Indigenous data sovereignty). Require public agencies and large vendors to run periodic red-team drills and publish lessons learned; make those materials reusable by SMEs.
column4: Equitable access across regions and demographics: Treat AI literacy like basic infrastructure. Co-fund programs with provinces, school boards, colleges, and libraries; deliver hybrid (online + community hub) training in multiple languages; and provide devices/connectivity stipends where needed. Partner with industry to donate credits, tools, and mentors to rural/northern communities and underrepresented groups; require grantees to report participation and outcomes by region and demographic. Build Indigenous-led curricula that respect data governance principles and local priorities. Use outcome-based funding—pay for placement, certification, and on-the-job usage—not just course completion. If every community has a place to learn, the tools to practice, and a pathway to a real job using AI, confidence and capability rise together.

column2: Canada’s modern digital economy demands a blend of foundational and advanced skills including data literacy, cybersecurity, cloud computing, AI, and digital collaboration, paired with soft skills like communication, adaptability, and teamwork. To support their development and deployment, Canada must scale inclusive, hands-on training programs, embed digital competencies across all levels of education, and foster public-private partnerships that align workforce development with industry needs.
column3: Canada can enhance AI literacy by implementing a national strategy that embeds foundational and ethical AI education across K–12 and post-secondary curricula, empowering citizens to understand how AI works, its limitations, and potential biases. Key efforts should include supporting teachers with training, engaging the public through inclusive dialogue, and promoting frameworks that emphasize critical thinking, ethical use, and human-centric values.
column4: To ensure equitable access to AI literacy across regions, demographics, and socioeconomic groups, Canada must implement a national AI literacy strategy that embeds inclusive education in K–12 and post-secondary systems, supports marginalized communities, and provides regionally tailored resources. Leadership from federal and provincial governments, in partnership with schools, Indigenous organizations, and community groups, is essential to fund pilot programs, train educators, and ensure consistent access to AI tools and training nationwide.

Data that has been observed should be treated as unreliable. Accuracy, interpretation and bias should be expected until disproven.
While AI is not designed to be unethical, data can be broken and AI can be trained on broken data. This leads to false positives and false falses. Both of these are bad for everybody but depending on the case, one can be worse than the other.

Show the process of how raw data goes into structured data goes into vectorized data.

Show the process of how data gets sanitized to protect privacy.

Show how the process of machine learning occurs. Showing what's behind the curtain goes a long way to dispelling myths and fears.

Equity vs efficiency question.  Economics 101 question.  If you want more of something, you subsidize it.  It you want rapid gains, you subsidize those that show the most promise.

Hard skills include coding, data engineering, advanced data analytics.

Soft skills are problem solving and storytelling.

Government must decide if it wants rapid gains (in which case it will subsidize most promising) or is willing to sacrifice rapid gains for spreading out opportunity (in which case it will focus on equity of subsidies).

In the book Abundance, author Ezra Klein argues that one of the biggest failures of government, despite its noble intentions, is setting a single policy that tries to solve more than one problem at the same time.  It very often ends solving next to nothing when it does that.

The industry is still in its infancy so it's difficult to predict what will fall along the wayside and what will be widely adopted.  

As it stands now, programming or coding can be assisted with AI.  But as the industry is showing us, this AI-assisted coding, or 'vibe coding' does a terrible, terrible job at producing efficient code.  It might work, but it won't work as well as code created by a proper coder who knows what they're doing and is trying to make things efficiently.  

Is it a good idea to consider funding in 'AI-focused' careers if all we're doing is teaching people to 'vibe code'?  This could create downstream problems as vibe coders don't know the fundamentals and can't fix problems or trouble shoot without AI-assistance.  In essence, this is teaching skill degradation and reliance on a tool.  If that tool goes away, becomes obsolete, or the world decides vibe-coding doesn't make code good enough for actual use, then we've trained up a generation of people who have no worthwhile skills.
column3:
It should become a part of media literacy training, which should be part of our education system (but it isn't).  

The biggest misunderstanding is the term 'Artificial Intelligence' itself, and we should probably have regulations around what AI actually is.  While several models can pass a Turing test, none of them are truly 'intelligent' by any definition of the word.  They can't reason, or think, or conclude.  It's a pattern recognition system that is very good at predicting the next word or thing in the pattern, based on its training data. 

This means if the training data includes erroneous information, then the model will be erroneous.  But because it doesn't think, reason or conclude, it doesn't know when it doesn't know something.  The result is that it can give VERY wrong answers VERY confidently. 

The public needs to understand that AI isn't actually self-aware, or sentient, it doesn't think or reason, and is not intelligent.  In fact, we should regulate the use of the moniker 'AI' and enforce use of other terms such as 'LLM' or "machine learning'

column2:
The most important skill for a modern digital economy isn’t coding—it’s data stewardship: the ability to publish, verify, and govern structured information.
AI is reshaping every sector, but the real divide won’t be between those who build AI and those who use it—it will be between those who can shape the knowledge layer AI depends on and those who can’t.

Core skills:
- Metadata literacy: describing information in ways machines can process—as basic as clear writing, not coding.
- Data governance: verifying sources, maintaining provenance, correcting errors.
- Verification and audit: checking AI outputs against authoritative data.
- Infrastructure participation: contributing to and benefiting from shared systems rather than relying on proprietary platforms.
These skills adapt across technologies—metadata literacy learned today works for whatever AI comes next.

How Canada supports development:
- Embed data stewardship into existing workforce programs: small business support, nonprofit capacity building, and sector-specific training.
- For AI-focused careers: expand applied research chairs in verifiable AI and metadata engineering, fund fellowships in data ethics, and create career pathways from research to infrastructure development.
- Use public metadata infrastructure (like Artsdata.ca and Footlight CMS) as live training environments.
- Offer micro-credentials and apprenticeships in data stewardship, verification, and infrastructure maintenance.

Deployment:
Partner with libraries, colleges, and regional nonprofits to deliver hands-on training.
Create data-stewardship roles across sectors—maintaining verified data, coordinating regional partners, ensuring quality.

Result:
Workforce readiness becomes infrastructure readiness. When publishing verified information is as easy as posting online, every worker helps build the knowledge layer that keeps AI accurate. That’s not just literacy—it’s shared economic power.
column3:
AI literacy isn’t just understanding what AI does—it’s understanding how it knows, and where it fails. Teaching AI’s limitations means showing people real errors: where systems get things wrong, what they can’t do, and how bias enters through missing or unverified data.

Reframe:
Awareness of AI’s limitations and biases grows when people can trace where AI gets its information, test outputs against reality, and correct errors themselves. Literacy comes from participation, not passive explanation.

Strategy:
- Use public metadata infrastructure as a national learning environment. When citizens and organizations publish verified information, they see how AI interprets it—and where it misreads.
- Develop workplace training programs across sectors—especially health, education, and public service—with modules on data provenance, verification, and audit.
- Offer micro-credentials in AI interpretability and data ethics through colleges, professional associations, and online platforms.
- Launch public awareness campaigns focused on AI’s dependencies—how trust depends on verified data, not promises of accuracy. Target high-impact sectors and underserved communities.
- Create citizen audit projects where community groups, students, and journalists test AI systems for accuracy using open data and report findings publicly.
- Integrate data stewardship into K–12 curricula—teaching students to publish, verify, and govern information as civic literacy for the digital age.

International alignment:
Learn from Estonia’s digital identity infrastructure and Singapore’s Smart Nation model, but extend them further by grounding literacy in infrastructure people can use and improve.

Result:
Canada’s AI literacy grows through agency. The more people contribute verified knowledge and test systems against truth, the more trustworthy—and self-correcting—AI becomes for everyone.
column4:
Equitable access to AI literacy begins with access to participation. When communities can’t publish or verify their own information, they’re excluded from how AI systems learn and represent them. Programs should emphasize both capability (how to contribute and audit) and critical awareness (how to spot errors and understand limitations).

Reframe:
AI literacy isn’t just learning about technology—it’s learning through authorship. Free, accessible tools for publishing verified data are the foundation of digital equity.

Strategy:
	•	Distribute simple, no-cost publishing tools nationwide, enabling local organizations—from Indigenous councils to rural nonprofits—to create and verify metadata without technical skills or platform fees.
	•	Partner with libraries, community centers, and regional nonprofits to deliver hands-on workshops and ongoing support. These institutions already serve marginalized and rural populations—they are the access network.
	•	Fund translation, multilingual interfaces, and offline-first design, and prioritize low-income communities through free programs, equipment lending, and partnerships with employment centers and social services.
	•	Collaborate with provinces and municipalities: federal government funds tools and standards, provinces integrate literacy into education systems and workforce programs, municipalities deploy through community services and local development initiatives.
	•	Industry role: companies benefiting from public data infrastructure contribute to community training programs, sponsor data stewardship fellowships, and provide technical mentorship through regional partnerships.

Result:
Equity in AI isn’t about equal exposure—it’s about equal authorship. When every community can contribute verified knowledge and audit the systems that use it, literacy becomes lived, not taught—and Canada’s digital future becomes truly inclusive.

column2:
Skills for a modern, digital economy — and how to build them

Two tracks: (A) AI makers (data, MLOps, security, assurance); (B) AI users (process redesign, prompt craft, data literacy, change management).

Learn by shipping: Fund 6–12-month rotations/apprenticeships across universities, startups, primes, and departments; completion = deployed use case, not a certificate.

Modular micro-credentials: Short, stackable badges in cloud, data, security, and AI assurance delivered via colleges + employer academies; tax credits only when skills are applied in production.

Manager upskilling: Train line leaders on AI ROI, risk, and ATO patterns so teams can move from pilots to production.
column3:
Boost AI literacy (including limits and bias)

Workplace first: Mandatory AI 101 + “failure modes” (bias, hallucination, privacy, safety) for all knowledge workers; advanced tracks for reviewers/red-teamers.

Use standard playbooks: Publish open prompting, eval, and incident-response guides; require model cards and plain-language risk notices at point of use.

Public offerings that scale: Free, bilingual intro courses + sandbox demos; partner media and libraries for “how it works / how it fails” sessions with live examples.
column4:
Equitable access across regions and groups

Bring the tools to people: Regional AI labs-in-a-box (accredited cloud access, GPUs via vouchers, evaluation credits) run through colleges, libraries, and Indigenous innovation centres.

Device + connectivity: Means-tested device/compute vouchers tied to course completion and project delivery.

Local mentors, real projects: Pay practitioners to coach cohorts solving local problems (health access, transit, resource ops); success measured by deployed solutions and job placement.

One national playbook: Reusable curricula, security/ethics modules, and data/IP templates so provinces/municipalities can adopt without reinventing.

column2:
Key Skills for a Modern, Digital Economy:


Digital Literacy and Critical Thinking

Understanding how digital systems work, how data is used, and how to question algorithmic decisions.
Ability to opt out or challenge AI systems when necessary.



Ethical and Privacy-Aware AI Competence

Skills in privacy-preserving technologies, bias detection, and responsible AI design.
Awareness of data rights, consent, and digital sovereignty.



Human-AI Collaboration

Training in how to work alongside AI tools to enhance productivity without replacing human judgment.
Emphasis on augmenting human skills, not automating them away.



Open-Source and Decentralized Tech Skills

Competence in open-source development, distributed systems, and non-centralized platforms.
Encouraging innovation that is transparent, auditable, and community-driven.



Resilience and Adaptability

Lifelong learning, adaptability to new tools, and the ability to navigate change without coercion.
Emphasis on human agency in shaping one’s own digital path.




How Canada Can Support Development and Deployment:


Publicly Funded, Open-Access Training

Provide free or low-cost digital skills programs accessible to all Canadians, regardless of background or location.
Focus on ethical AI, privacy, and digital rights as core curriculum components.



Support for Workers, Not Just Startups

Invest in reskilling and upskilling programs for workers in sectors most affected by automation.
Ensure that AI adoption does not lead to displacement, but to empowerment.



Decentralized Learning Hubs

Establish community-based digital learning centers across Canada, especially in rural and underserved areas.
Encourage peer-to-peer learning, open-source collaboration, and local innovation.



Ethical Standards in Education and Employment

Embed digital ethics and privacy literacy into K–12 and post-secondary education.
Encourage employers to adopt ethical AI practices and provide transparent upskilling pathways.
column3:
Center Ethics, Bias, and Limitations in Education

Integrate AI ethics, bias awareness, and limitations into K–12 and post-secondary curricula—not just coding or data science.
Teach students how AI systems can amplify discrimination, misrepresent reality, or erode privacy if not properly designed and governed.



Promote Public Awareness Campaigns

Launch national campaigns to explain what AI is, what it isn’t, and how it affects daily life—in plain language.
Focus on rights-based messaging, including the right to refuse AI systems and still fully participate in society.



Support Community-Based Learning

Fund local workshops, libraries, and community centers to host AI literacy programs tailored to diverse populations.
Ensure materials are multilingual, culturally relevant, and accessible to people with disabilities.



Empower Workers and Professionals

Provide sector-specific training on how AI is used in healthcare, law, education, and other fields—highlighting both benefits and risks.
Include bias detection, data ethics, and human-AI collaboration in professional development programs.



Encourage Critical Media and Civic Engagement

Support journalism and civic tech initiatives that investigate AI systems, expose harms, and explain complex technologies to the public.
Promote public deliberation on AI policy through citizen assemblies, town halls, and participatory design processes.



Normalize Transparency and Accountability

Require that AI systems used in public services include plain-language explanations of how they work and what data they use.
Make bias audits and impact assessments publicly available and understandable.



Protect the Right to Non-Participation

Ensure that people who choose not to engage with AI systems are not marginalized or excluded from essential services.
Maintain human-based alternatives in all critical sectors—healthcare, education, employment, and government services.
column4:
Fund Community-Based Learning Across All Regions

Establish AI literacy hubs in rural, remote, and underserved communities, including Indigenous territories.
Partner with local organizations, libraries, and schools to deliver culturally relevant, multilingual, and accessible programs.



Ensure Accessibility for All Abilities and Backgrounds

Design AI literacy materials that are inclusive of people with disabilities, newcomers, seniors, and low-income Canadians.
Provide free or low-cost access to devices, internet, and learning platforms.



Respect the Right to Opt Out

Guarantee that individuals who choose not to engage with AI are not excluded from education, employment, or public services.
Maintain non-AI alternatives in all essential systems to uphold freedom of participation.



Support Educators and Local Leaders

Train teachers, community leaders, and public servants to deliver AI literacy in contextually relevant ways.
Provide open-source toolkits and train-the-trainer programs to scale impact locally.



Involve Marginalized Communities in Co-Design

Engage Indigenous, racialized, and low-income communities in the design of AI literacy initiatives.
Ensure programs reflect local values, lived experiences, and community priorities.



Embed AI Literacy in Public Institutions

Integrate AI awareness and critical thinking into public school curricula, employment programs, and civic education.
Make AI literacy a core part of digital citizenship.



Measure and Address Gaps

Collect disaggregated data (with consent) to identify and address disparities in AI literacy access.
Use this data to target funding and support where it’s most needed.

column2:
"AI" is a dead-end technology at best, and the world's greatest technology hoax at worst. Generative models and their creators have repeatedly shown that they have no regard for the rules of law, copyright, ethics, creativity, or the environment.

AI researchers have mostly all agreed that the current approach to large language models has reached its limit. Without a significant lateral breakthrough, this technology will not improve beyond confidently lying whenever asked a question.

Banks and economists worldwide are quickly coming to the realization that the AI industry as a whole is a bubble. It has not delivered on any of its promises, and only grows as a consequence of circular investment between the leading AI companies.

The US especially has heavily bet on AI, with 40% of their GDP being tied up in AI companies who, so far, have shown no benefit to society and generated no return on investment. When the bubble bursts, Canada should be economically self-sufficient to weather that storm, not tie us further to the sinking ship.

column2:
In an AI-driven economy, the most essential skill is critical thinking. While technical proficiency and AI literacy are important, it is human judgment that determines whether AI is used responsibly or misused. AI is a tool; its impact depends entirely on the discernment of the person using it.

Recent research from Microsoft and Carnegie Mellon University shows that users with higher confidence in AI tend to engage in less critical thinking, while those with higher self-confidence in their own abilities apply more scrutiny and judgment to AI outputs. This highlights the urgent need to cultivate critical thinking as a core competency in the workforce. [www.microsoft.com]

The World Economic Forum also identifies critical thinking as one of the top skills for the future, especially as AI automates lower-level cognitive tasks. Forbes emphasizes that no clever prompt can replace human reasoning, especially when AI outputs may be biased, inaccurate, or ethically questionable. [www.weforum.org] [www.forbes.com]

How Canada Can Support Skill Development:
- Integrate critical thinking into all levels of education, from K–12 to post-secondary and professional training.
- Promote AI literacy alongside ethical reasoning, helping workers understand both the capabilities and limitations of AI.
- Support lifelong learning through micro-credentials, reskilling programs, and interdisciplinary training.
- Encourage reflective use of AI in workplaces, emphasizing human oversight and accountability.
To thrive in the digital economy, Canadians must not only learn how to use AI but also how to think critically about it.
column3:
To build a digitally resilient society, Canada must prioritize AI literacy, not just technical skills, but a deep understanding of AI’s limitations, biases, and ethical implications.
Best Practices from Global Leaders:
- EU AI Act – Article 4 mandates organizations to ensure sufficient AI literacy among staff and stakeholders. This includes tailored training based on roles, technical knowledge, and the context in which AI is used. [www.forbes.com], [www.trail-ml.com]
- UNESCO’s AI Ethics Framework promotes public education on AI risks, including bias, discrimination, and misuse. It emphasizes inclusive, rights-based approaches to AI governance. [www.unesco.org]
- Digital Promise’s AI Literacy Framework recommends integrating AI education across K–12 and higher education, focusing on understanding, evaluating, and using AI responsibly. It highlights the need for hands-on learning and ethical reflection. [files.eric.ed.gov]
My recommendations for Canada:
- National AI Literacy Campaign: Launch a public-private initiative to educate citizens on how AI works, its limitations, and how to critically assess outputs.
- Curriculum Integration: Embed AI ethics and bias awareness into school and university programs, not just computer science but across disciplines.
- Community Engagement: Host citizen panels, workshops, and public hearings to discuss AI’s role in society and gather feedback on its use.
- Professional Development: Offer ongoing training for workers in all sectors, tailored to their roles and the AI tools they use.
- Inclusive Design: Ensure AI literacy efforts are accessible to marginalized communities, including Indigenous, neurodivergent, and rural populations.
Building AI literacy is not just about understanding technology, it’s about empowering Canadians to use it wisely, question it critically, and shape its future responsibly.
column4:
To build a truly inclusive digital economy, Canada must ensure that AI literacy reaches all regions, demographics, and socioeconomic groups, not just urban centers or tech-savvy populations.
Best Practices from Global Leaders:
- Singapore’s AI for Everyone (AI4E) program offers free, accessible workshops to citizens of all ages, focusing on AI basics, ethics, and real-world applications. It’s delivered in multiple languages and tailored to different learning levels.
- Finland’s Elements of AI course, developed with the University of Helsinki, is free, online, and designed for non-technical audiences. It has reached over 1 million learners globally and is available in multiple languages.
- UNESCO’s AI Competency Framework emphasizes inclusive education strategies, especially for underserved communities, including Indigenous populations, women, and rural learners.
My recommendations for Canada:
- National AI Literacy Strategy: Launch a coordinated effort across provinces to deliver free, multilingual, and accessible AI education, online and in-person.
- Community-Based Learning: Partner with libraries, nonprofits, and local organizations to host workshops and training sessions in rural and underserved areas.
- School and College Integration: Embed AI literacy into K–12 and post-secondary curricula, with special support for neurodivergent and marginalized students.
- Public-Private Collaboration: Encourage tech companies to co-develop inclusive learning platforms and sponsor outreach programs.
- Mobile and Offline Access: Ensure materials are available on mobile devices and in offline formats for communities with limited internet access.
Equitable AI literacy is not just a social goal; it’s an economic imperative. Canada must ensure every citizen has the opportunity to understand, question, and benefit from AI.

column2: Canada’s digital economy demands hybrid skills—technical, analytical, and human-centered—and can support their development through inclusive training programs, employer partnerships, and regional innovation hubs.To thrive in a modern digital economy, workers need more than just coding or data analysis. They must combine digital fluency with soft skills like communication, adaptability, and teamwork. Canada’s workforce strategy is evolving to meet this challenge.
column3: Canada can enhance AI literacy by embedding it in education, launching public awareness campaigns, and offering inclusive training that emphasizes AI’s limitations, biases, and ethical implications.Despite Canada’s leadership in AI research, it ranks 44th out of 47 countries in AI literacy and 42nd in trust according to a 2025 KPMG–University of Melbourne study. This gap threatens responsible adoption and public confidence.By making AI literacy a national priority-and focusing on its risks as well as its potential-Canada can build a digitally empowered, ethically aware society.
column4: Canada can ensure equitable access to AI literacy by embedding inclusive education in schools, funding regional programs, partnering with community organizations, and tailoring outreach to marginalized groups.Despite its AI research leadership, Canada ranks 44th out of 47 countries in AI literacy. Bridging this gap requires targeted efforts that reach all Canadians-regardless of geography, income, or background.By making AI literacy inclusive, accessible, and locally relevant, Canada can empower all citizens to engage with AI confidently and responsibly.

column2:
Right now, AI skills are not required for a modern economy. But if you incentivize AI education over all else, you risk creating a generation of workers whose only marketable skills are plugging in commands to an offshore AI model their cheapskate bosses selected. This represents a threat to Canadian resilience and economic independence.

So, rather than making the labour skills of the next generation of Canadians dependent on the cost-cutting whimsy of their employers, we should build a diversified labour base that possesses the skills to perform tasks independent of AI. Then if everyone else in the world starts building dependencies on error-prone AI, they can come looking for Canadian technology that performs at a higher standard.
column3: Targeted engagements and public awareness campaigns are essential to reach members of the public who don't want to go back to school. Right now, the culture surrounding AI is in its infancy, and we have the power to set or influence people's first impression of the technology. We have a responsibility to convey the truth-- that AI has both incredible potential and incredible risk.

column3:
Ideally through well-funded and thoroughly-reviewed public education initiatives, in the same way that Finland combats bias in the media by teaching media literacy as a core component of its language and social studies curricula. From primary school, learning the difference between "true and false" versus "reality and fiction", to writing stories and learning literary devices in middle school, to developing campaigns and initiatives in high school and beyond.

Obviously not an apples-to-apples comparison but the general idea has merit, I feel.
