column2: Honestly, generative AI leads STRONGLY to a reduction in skills. I know a number of programmers from around the world (Canada, UK, US, a few others) who have all noticed a sharp drop in skills of new programmers as they rely on AI tools. This is preventing them from learning the core skills of their job, as they throw everything into a generative model, and don't actually know how to fix things when it doesn't work. I would stay a hard barrier on the use of AI in the education system, like how we don't allow calculators until you know how to do arithmetic by hand. Core fundamentals of each field don't change over time; the most popular programming languages were all created in the 70s and 80s, and even more popular one heavily draw on those languages. If you know the fundamentals it is much easier to transition to changing technologies.
column3: Very strict regulation and education. Limit advertising in the same way we do for prescription drugs. column2: Basic literacy, to start, but far more importantly: Bayesian updating, which is the skill of *changing what you believe to be most plausible in the face of new evidence*. No "AI-specific" skills exist, and training people to today's market is transparently silly. Ten years ago everyone was supposed to learn how to code. Today nobody is. Two years ago "prompt engineering" was all the rage. Today it is nowhere. Investment in strong, independent, research-and-teaching-oriented post-secondary institutions that have a focus on the foundations of Western civilization--art, music, mathematics, the basic sciences, philosophy--is by far the best thing for developing a citizenry capable of taking care of each other and dealing with whatever the future, and the world, is likely to throw at us.
column3: "AI literacy" is a meaningless marketing buzzword, but insofar as it has meaning, making it illegal for an AI to present itself as a human being or human-like being is what is required. column2:
Essential Skills for the Digital Economy
Core Digital Literacy: Proficiency in basic digital tools (e.g., productivity and collaboration software), navigating cloud platforms, and using online services is now essential in almost every occupation.
AI and Data Proficiency: Skills in AI tool use, data analysis, automation (e.g., coding, scripting), and the ability to integrate, interpret, and manage technological solutions are highly valued—not just in tech, but across all sectors.
Cybersecurity & Cloud Computing: Knowledge in digital safety, privacy, and ethical handling of digital assets—plus familiarity with new platforms and AI-powered solutions—are in growing demand, especially as cyber risks escalate.
Adaptive and Human Skills: Skills like critical thinking, collaboration, project management, and the ability to rapidly learn new tools underpin resilience in a tech-driven workforce.
Digital Equity & Inclusion: A commitment to equitable access ensures that all Canadians—including those in rural, Indigenous, and underrepresented communities—benefit from digital transformation. 
Strategic Support for Skill Development and Deployment: 
Integrated Education & Training: Embed digital and AI skills in K-12, post-secondary, and adult education curricula, with hands-on, practical applications tied to workplace needs. Expand programs like the Digital Skills for Youth Program and similar public-private initiatives to reach more Canadians.
Upskilling & Reskilling: Develop targeted micro-credentials, short courses, and AI-skilling bootcamps that quickly transition workers into tech-enabled and AI-adjacent roles, leveraging collaborations between industry, government, and academic institutions.
Work-Integrated Learning: Encourage experiential learning, internships, and co-ops focusing on digital and AI skills, ensuring learners gain real-world practice on the latest technologies.
Immigration and Talent Attraction.
column3:
Workplace Training and Credentials
Sector-specific Workshops: Programs like the AI Skills Training Series for Canadian Public Servants offer certificate-based, foundational and application-focused workshops tailored for the public sector, building both understanding and practical skills for AI use and its risks, including bias and security.
Professional Micro-credentials: Continuing education, such as Concordia’s Certificate in AI Proficiency, equips learners not just with technical knowledge, but with critical thinking around explainability, bias, and responsible use—credentials that are increasingly valued across sectors.
AI Literacy for Entrepreneurs and HR: Targeted training such as 8-week AI literacy courses for business owners or self-paced online learning for HR professionals, helps different professional groups adapt AI tools responsibly, with explicit modules on limitations, ethical risks, and bias mitigation.
Public Awareness Campaigns and Targeted Engagement
Government-Led Campaigns: The federal Public Awareness Working Group has recommended a national AI Community of Practice, public information campaigns, and free, online AI literacy modules to engage all Canadians, especially marginalized and underrepresented groups, in dialogues on trustworthy and responsible AI.
Inclusion and Deliberative Dialogue: Pan-Canadian consultation processes, deliberate workshops, and collaborations with community leaders ensure AI literacy and trust-building reach diverse, multilingual, and geographically dispersed communities.
Education and International Best Practices
K–12 and Postsecondary Integration: Embedding AI fundamentals, data literacy, and ethical considerations into Canadian school curricula mirrors best-in-class strategies seen in Finland and Estonia, which begin AI education early to foster lifelong critical engagement with the topic.
Teacher and Facilitator Training, and Continuous Adaptive Learning.
column4:
Multi-Level Government Collaboration
Federal Leadership with Local Delivery: National programs, such as the Digital Literacy Exchange and Digital Skills for Youth, set standards and funding priorities, but empower provinces, territories, and municipalities to tailor delivery for local language, culture, connectivity gaps, and employment needs.
Community-Based Partnerships: Municipal governments and Indigenous authorities collaborate with federal agencies to co-design AI literacy campaigns and training initiatives—ensuring culturally relevant, accessible materials.
Industry and Private Sector’s Role
Corporate Support for Upskilling: Tech leaders and large employers invest in regional workforce training, mentorship, and community programs, such as Google’s AI Opportunity Fund, targeting underserved and remote communities for scholarships or free access to AI courses.
Public-Private Joint Ventures: Shared funding and program development between government and industry support scalable, local initiatives—like specialized bootcamps or digital equity hubs in rural and diverse urban areas.
Inclusive Educational and Literacy Strategies
Flexible, Modular Learning: Online and blended formats, accessible micro-credentials, and low-cost community workshops break barriers for remote learners, busy families, and working adults. Programs like Palette Skills and ATHABASCA’s Introduction to AI Literacy ensure adaptability to different skill levels and backgrounds.
Targeted Outreach and Multilingual Resources: AI literacy programs must offer materials and instruction in multiple languages, including Indigenous languages, and adapt for learners with disabilities—partnering with local organizations to reach youth, newcomers, and equity-deserving groups.
Mentorship and Local Champions: Recruit and train community leaders, educators, and tech mentors from within target demographics to deliver programs, build trust, and foster a sense of ownership and relevance in local AI adoption.

column2: Canada can lead by example through enacting policies supported by technologies that strengthen and protect health, safety, and infrastructure for all. As it stands, AI is far too unstable and unreliable to be responsibly integrated across all industries without significant detriments and potentially far-reaching consequences. It would be a gross misappropriation of funding to blindly invest into and apply technologies that misrepresent information, necessitate rampant personal data theft, and pose a substantial ecological threat to all Canadians. Much must be done about the rampant data breaches, algorithmic bias, social manipulation through AI algorithms, job losses due to AI automation, widening socioeconomic inequality sparked by AI-driven job losses, a lack of transparency for applications of AI, invasive social surveillance, violation of creative voices due to generative AI, and the weakening of ethics and goodwill because of AI. It is only when safeguards have been introduced can we move to responsibly establish industry around this emerging technology. Otherwise, we will all be left vulnerable to unchecked application of a volatile technology with already palpable consequences.

column2: Generative AI is a danger to the Canadian populations both mentally and financially. There is no ethical use for generative AI as it leads to loss of cognitive function and no gains in productivity, and generative AI takes in copyrighted material and spews out infringement after infringement on both copyrighted material that it scrapes for its databases and the creativity the human psyche has as a latent skill. Tech companies specializing in generative AI research have explicitly said that they know the generative AI bubble will burst and they'll keep pushing it until it does, as it makes them money. There is no feasible way to maintain this generative AI growth either as the sheer speed in which they want it to grow will destroy everything in our environment with carbon emissions, destruction of natural land, strain on our power grids, and as already mentioned, damage to the cognitive functioning of Canadians across the board as it continues to destroy jobs for artists, graphic designers, actors, and many other creatives while throwing out useless slop that can barely be called "concepts", let alone art. Please do not push for generative AI, and lead the world in stopping this economic train wreck before it happens.

column2:
We are sorely lacking in our education of how computer systems work. The argument has been made that we don't need to know how our cars work. The difference here is that cars are not running our lives.

We need to put computer programming focus back in elementary school. It doesn't need to be advanced. It could even be BASIC again. (Although, the modern version of that would appear to be Python.) Just simple stuff that lets people see how software is put together, so they can learn the strengths and weaknesses of it.

Obviously, some AI training will be needed. Some theory of how it works, and some exercises that show the strength and weaknesses of it. People need to understand what the machine is actually doing so they understand how to work with the information it provides. This could be as simple as a week in class, if even that.

University computer programming courses right now are overrun with students cheating and using AI to create assignments, completely unable to explain how the program works. THESE ARE THE GRADUATES GOING INTO COMPUTER ENGINEERING JOBS. That should scare the daylights out of you - it's scares the heck out of me. Software quality has been steadily degrading for decades as it gets more complex and more focused on 'magic bullets'. It's about to get a lot worse, very rapidly, as the people building the software lose the ability to support it.

The professors I know are helpless to fix this trend, blocked by resistance to change. Computer science degrees need to move away from the mechanics of creating the code, as that will change, and focus on the understanding and troubleshooting side. As AI does more and more of the building (of software), we will more and more need to be the mechanics.
column3: Needs to start in the elementary schools. I guess this is mostly provincial.. it's been a while since I was in school. But it's really important - as important as reading. The kids today are savvy. They can spot AI-generated images much faster than an older person. But they don't have the understanding of WHY the AI does what it does. And it's not that complicated a subject to teach well enough to enlighten.

column2:
25 years ago, we were teaching people how to Google: how to pick the right keywords and use boolean operators. We stopped when Google said "you can just ask questions", but knowing "Google-Fu", as it came to be known, still yielded better results. This is a skill that is still sorely needed and can help with the fabled "prompt engineering".
The Art of Letter Writing is so forgotten, a lot of people swear they're unable to answer emails without cracking open an LLM. Programs to re-teach workers this skill so that they waste less time in the LLM (if all you need to know is whether to use "sincerely" or "yours", just ask that one question!) could prove vital for productivity.
Sounding Board techniques such as Rubber Duck Debugging could also save the time and resources of asking an LLM.
Cash education (knowing when paying with cash is, in fact, faster than tapping your card or phone) could help people save time outside of work and these kind of campaigns could be exported ...remember I said Canada is a leader in educational media?
Finally, for all the people decrying that they "need" their image generators, basic photoshopping skills (not necessarily with the Adobe version, in fact I personally discourage the Adobe version) should be taught.
Are we noticing a pattern here?

column4:
If each AI model is limited to a specific use, limiting acces to each might actually be more productive. For instance, if someone goes to a library there might be a librarian AI that all it does is recommend books with a mix of a graphical menu and a "chat box" to refine that menu, but it would never tell you "actually don't get that first aid book, just do this one weird trick instead! Wanna ERP now?" Such models should also know when to refer you to a human or when the sum of your questions might signal you need help (e.g. "Books on repairing clocks. Books on making clock alarms do things. Books on explosive materials." should immediately trigger something like "you might want to talk to a mental health professional, please talk to the librarian for a free referral" but never a "this conversation has been forwarded to the police.")
Once again, the current lack of confidence is not a consequence of people not being informed, in fact those who are less informed tend to blindly trust the LLM more. The legitimate concerns at the heart of the new generation of AI needs to be addressed head-on before any such campaigns can begin.

column2:
(I prepared and shared this text in a briefing in May 2025. It was shared with government, industry, and NGO leaders. I have included it in the following boxes under Education and Skills) Canada urgently requires a national AI literacy strategy to ensure our all Canadians (from youth to seniors) are not left behind in a rapidly transforming world. Crucially, this strategy must include children from the earliest years, as it is during this foundational period that core values, cognitive habits, and learning frameworks are established. Embedding AI literacy early supports the development of responsible, critical, and ethically grounded engagement with technology later in life.
An inclusive, values-driven approach to AI literacy also ensures the development of a diverse workforce, capable of bringing varied perspectives, experiences, and innovations to the design and deployment of AI technologies. Equity in access to AI education is critical to preventing systemic biases from being replicated or deepened by the very technologies shaping our future.
This document outlines the case for strategic investment by government, industry, and philanthropy, and policy intervention in AI literacy, calling for collaboration across civil society, government, and industry. It also recommends strong partnerships with other like-minded countries that share values and have already started to implement AI literacy strategies.
In parallel with Canada’s efforts to reduce interprovincial trade barriers, this strategy can also serve as a foundation for greater collaboration among provincial and territorial ministries of education. By aligning efforts across jurisdictions, Canada can ensure that all citizens—regardless of region—are adequately educated and prepared for the opportunities and challenges of an AI-driven future.
column3:
To be an international leader in responsible AI, Canada must:
Develop and fund a National AI Literacy Strategy that can support the provinces and territories (especially Ministries and Departments of Education), and align with a renewed Canadian AI Strategy, and leverages work being done by international partners
Create an AI Literacy advisory board that reflects Canada’s diversity in population, segments of society (academia, industry, government, civil society), and immediate Indigenous involvement 
Align and streamline Canada-funded training opportunities and remove duplication of efforts and resources
Align Canada's superclusters to streamline AI literacy training opportunities and remove duplication of efforts and resources
Invest in AI public awareness and lifelong learning initiatives, including training through community-led initiatives
Support and align with provincial and territorial Departments and Ministries of Education 
Align all digital innovation funding for youth with federal strategy
Build international partnerships, including with the OECD, WEF, UNESCO, UNICEF, and other global organisations, and put Canada in a leadership position in AI literacy.
AI literacy training must begin in primary school (values, ethics etc) and build on year to year to post secondary. Youth have to be prioritised in the AI strategy. Not just data protection, but also the impacts on their future work, their mental health. I would highly recommend there being a person within the government'a AI team who is responsible for youth and can help navigate between departments to make sure that kids interests don't get bounced around.
column4:
Education Pathways for AI Literacy
AI literacy must be embedded across a lifelong continuum of learning, encompassing three educational levels and three delivery modes:

K–12 Education
Integrate AI concepts (ethics, algorithms, digital citizenship) across subjects.
Provide equitable access to AI tools and teacher training in all communities.
Ensure inclusive curriculum design reflecting diverse cultural contexts.

Post-secondary Education
Embed interdisciplinary AI literacy across all faculties (not just STEM).
Support programs focused on ethics, philosophy, human-computer interaction, and social justice.
Partner with industry to align curriculum with workforce needs.

Workforce Skills Training
Provide reskilling and upskilling opportunities for all age groups.
Support workers in adapting to AI tools, automation, and changing job roles.
Prioritise vulnerable sectors and communities at risk of exclusion

Community-led Training
Provide AI literacy training through organisations such as libraries, YMCA
Prioritise vulnerable sectors, including seniors, and communities at risk of exclusion and AI manipulation

Policy Leaders:
Prioritise AI literacy in federal and provincial funding agendas
Legislate ethical AI use in workforce training
Include AI literacy strategy in the Pan-Canadian AI strategy
Ensure public-private collaborations include equity mandates
Include AI literacy in international partnerships in overall AI strategies
Legislate AI literacy for all employees (both internal government public servants, and any Canadian company with employees who engage with AI systems)

Philanthropic & Industry Funders:
Invest in inclusive AI education programs 
Support grassroots organisations to develop culturally responsive AI literacy programming
Fund research on the social impacts of AI literacy and access gaps
Fund research on AI education and collaboration with universities

column2:
To thrive in a modern digital economy, Canada must prioritize both technical and adaptive skills—building a workforce that can evolve with technology while staying grounded in ethical, civic, and economic realities.Core Skills for a Digital Economy: 
1. Digital Literacy
Basic proficiency in navigating digital tools, platforms, and communication systems. This includes cybersecurity awareness, data handling, and online collaboration.
2. AI and Data Competency
Understanding how AI systems work, how to interpret data, and how to apply machine learning tools responsibly. Includes coding, analytics, and model evaluation.
3. Hybrid Skill Sets
Combining technical knowledge with soft skills like communication, teamwork, and ethical reasoning. Employers increasingly seek people who can bridge tech and human contexts.
4. Adaptability and Lifelong Learning
Ability to learn new tools, shift roles, and respond to emerging technologies. Includes critical thinking, problem-solving, and resilience.
5. Sector-Specific Digital Skills
Tailored competencies for healthcare, agriculture, construction, finance, and public services—where AI and automation are reshaping workflows.

How Canada Can Support Development and Deployment

• National Upskilling Strategy
Fund micro-credentials, bootcamps, and continuing education programs that blend AI literacy with domain expertise. Prioritize accessibility and regional equity.
• Public-Private Training Partnerships
Co-develop training programs with industry, academia, and government. Ensure they include civic ethics, open science, and public-interest applications.
• Workforce Transition Support
Offer retraining and placement programs for workers displaced by automation. Focus on transferable skills and community-based employment pathways.
• AI-Focused Career Pipelines
Create fellowships, internships, and mentorships in AI safety, civic tech, and public-sector innovation. Tie funding to ethical benchmarks and Canadian IP retention.
column3:
Canada ranks near the bottom globally in AI literacy and trust, which directly impacts adoption, oversight, and civic empowerment. To enhance AI literacy, especially around limitations and biases, here’s what needs to happen:
1. Launch National AI Literacy Campaigns
Use plain-language messaging across schools, libraries, media, and public services. Focus on how AI works, where it fails, and what rights citizens have. Include real-world examples of bias, misuse, and civic impact.

2. Embed AI Training in Workplaces and Credentials
Create sector-specific training for healthcare, education, law, and public administration. Offer micro-credentials that cover ethical use, bias detection, and responsible deployment. Make this part of continuing education and professional standards.

3. Support Community-Led Engagements
Fund grassroots workshops, civic tech labs, and Indigenous-led programs that explore AI’s risks and opportunities. Prioritize underserved regions and multilingual access. Trust grows through participation.

4. Integrate AI Ethics into K–12 and Higher Education
Follow models like British Columbia’s framework for ethical AI in schools B. Teach students to question systems, understand data bias, and design responsibly. Include critical thinking, not just coding.

5. Adopt International Best Practices
Learn from countries with strong public engagement—like Finland’s Elements of AI course or Singapore’s national AI ethics curriculum. Adapt these to Canadian contexts with local case studies and civic relevance.

6. Build Feedback Channels and Oversight Mechanisms
Let citizens report concerns, ask questions, and shape how AI is used in their communities. Combine education with accountability.

AI literacy isn’t just technical, it’s civic. Canada must treat it as essential infrastructure for democracy, not just innovation.
column4:
To ensure equitable access to AI literacy across Canada, we need coordinated action that reaches beyond urban tech hubs and privileged institutions. Here’s what must be put in place:
1. National Strategy with Local Delivery
Develop a federal AI literacy framework, then fund provinces, territories, and municipalities to adapt and deliver it locally. This ensures consistency in standards while respecting regional needs and languages.

2. Public-Private Partnerships for Inclusive Training
Industry must co-invest in community-based programs—not just corporate upskilling. Offer volume discounts, free licenses, and mentorship for schools, libraries, and nonprofits. Platforms like Amii’s “AI Literacy for Everyone” are a strong start. 
3. Embed AI Literacy in Public Services
Train frontline workers—teachers, healthcare staff, municipal employees—to understand and explain AI systems they use. Make literacy part of service delivery, not just tech education.

4. Fund Community-Led and Indigenous-Led Programs
Support grassroots organizations to run workshops tailored to local contexts. Prioritize Indigenous data sovereignty and culturally relevant approaches. Equity means letting communities lead.

5. Offer Accessible, Multilingual Resources
Ensure materials are available in plain language, multiple formats (video, audio, print), and languages spoken across Canada. Accessibility must include disability inclusion and digital access.

6. Create Credential Pathways for All Skill Levels
Develop micro-credentials and certifications for students, workers, and lifelong learners. Tie them to employment pathways and civic engagement—not just technical roles.

7. Monitor and Report on Equity Outcomes
Track participation, access, and impact across regions and demographics. Use this data to refine programs and close gaps.
Canada’s AI future must be built with everyone, not just for them. Literacy is the foundation of agency, and equitable access is the measure of democratic integrity.

column2:
AI Implementation Science - the systematic methodology for translating AI capabilities into value by addressing the behavioral, organizational, and operational factors that determine adoption success. Implementation Science is the discipline of making AI actually work in real organizations and the real world. While computer science focuses on building AI models and data science on analyzing information, Implementation Science solves the 95% failure rate by systematically addressing how humans and organizations adapt to, adopt, and extract value from AI technologies. It's not about the technology—it's about the transformation.

Skills Framework:
Technical Foundation (20%)
• Basic AI/ML understanding
• Data literacy
• System thinking

Implementation Core (50%)
• Process mapping and optimization
• Change management
• Behavioral psychology
• Project management
• ROI measurement

Human Skills (30%)
• Cross-functional collaboration
• Ethical reasoning
• Communication across technical divides
• Continuous learning mindset

Delivery: Micro-credentials, not degrees. 3-month certificates, not 4-year programs.
Scale: 1 million Canadians with implementation skills by 2030
column3:
Implementation Science in Education - not everyone needs to code AI, but everyone needs to work with AI.

AI Implementation-Focused Literacy:
1. K-12 Program - "AI and Me" curriculum focusing on how AI helps humans, not how to code. Include bias recognition and limitation understanding. 
K-12: Building Implementation Mindsets
 Elementary (K-5):
o	AI as learning partner, not answer machine
o	Simple automation projects
o	Data quality basics
o	Ethical thinking about AI decisions
 Middle School (6-8):
o	Progressive automation projects
o	Peer learning and collaboration
o	Basic implementation planning
o	AI limitation understanding
 High School (9-12):
o	 Full implementation projects
o	 Business case development
o	 Change management basics
o	 Implementation portfolio building
2.	Workplace Implementation Training - Sector-specific programs showing how AI enhances current jobs. 2-day workshops, not months of training.
•	Target Audiences: Current managers and executives HR and organizational development professionals Consultants and advisors Government policy makers
•	Delivery Mechanisms: Micro-credentials through universities Industry association programs Online learning platforms Peer learning cohorts
3.	Public Implementation Education - Community college courses on "Living with AI" - practical skills for citizens as users and decision-makers.
o	Business Schools: Implementation Science as required course AI-enhanced case study analysis Live implementation projects with local businesses 
o	Engineering/Computer Science: Implementation considerations in system design Human factors in AI deployment Organizational change management 
o	Liberal Arts: Critical analysis of AI implementation impacts Ethical frameworks for deployment Social implications of automation
Key Message: AI literacy means understanding how to work WITH AI, not how to build it.

Principle: No Canadian left behind in the implementation revolution.

column2:
Core skill domains
	•	Data and AI
Data literacy, prompt engineering, applied ML, analytics, MLOps, safety and governance, privacy and security.
	•	Digital operations
Process automation, ERP and CRM fluency, API thinking, basic scripting, workflow design, QA and testing.
	•	Human skills
Problem framing, change management, stakeholder communication, ethics and risk judgement, critical thinking.
	•	Build and run
Cloud basics, version control, CICD, monitoring, documentation, incident response.

How Canada can support development and deployment
	•	Micro-credentials and stackable certificates
Short, competency based programs delivered by colleges and universities, recognized by employers and tied to specific roles.
	•	Work-integrated learning at scale
Co-ops, paid internships, and 6 to 12 month apprenticeships that place learners into clinics, plants, construction sites, and municipalities.
	•	Outcome tied funding
Grants and wage subsidies that pay when learners are placed and retained, and when employers report verified productivity gains.
	•	National role profiles and rubrics
Publish standard role maps for data analyst, AI product manager, prompt engineer, MLOps engineer, AI safety lead, automation analyst.
	•	SMB enablement
Vouchers that let small firms buy part time experts for MLOps, data engineering, or governance while upskilling their staff.
	•	Public sector as training ground
Pilot to production projects inside hospitals, ports, utilities, and municipalities with clear learning outcomes and job pathways.
column3:
What good literacy covers
	•	What AI is useful for, and where it fails
	•	Data quality, bias, and representativeness
	•	Privacy, consent, and data minimization
	•	Safety risks, red teaming, and abuse patterns
	•	Human in the loop and accountability
	•	How to measure outcomes and detect drift

Practical delivery models
	•	Workplace learning paths
Three tiers: awareness for all staff, practitioner for daily users, steward for owners of higher risk workflows.
	•	System cards and job aids
One page per AI tool that explains purpose, inputs, limits, escalation, and how to appeal decisions.
	•	Micro simulations
Short, role specific exercises for triage nurses, schedulers, plant supervisors, project managers, and contact center leads.
	•	Credentials that travel
Nationally recognized badges for Prompting Essentials, AI Safety Steward, and Data Privacy Practitioner.
	•	Public awareness campaigns
Partner with libraries, chambers, and sector councils for short sessions that teach how to spot deepfakes and verify sources.
	•	International best practices
Align to NIST AI RMF for risk framing, ISO 42001 for AI management systems, and C2PA for content provenance.
column4:
Access principles
	•	Learning should be local, job relevant, and available in short modules that do not require leaving work or caregiving for long periods.
	•	Materials should be bilingual where appropriate and accessible by mobile with low bandwidth options.
	•	Programs should include targeted supports for Indigenous communities, newcomers, rural learners, and people with disabilities.

How to operationalize
	•	Regional learning hubs
Use colleges, libraries, and community centers as delivery points. In Atlantic Canada, pair universities with TechImpact, sector councils, and local chambers.
	•	Digital access and tools
Device lending, connectivity stipends, and public computer labs with secure environments for hands on exercises.
	•	Earn while you learn
Wage subsidies and paid apprenticeships so adult learners can train without losing income.
	•	Trusted intermediaries
Partner with Indigenous organizations, settlement services, and nonprofit employment centers to co design content and recruit learners.
	•	Employer consortia
Pool demand from small firms to run shared cohorts and rotate learners through real projects.
	•	Open curriculum and shared assets
Publish reusable modules, labs, and checklists under open licenses so anyone can teach with local context.

Measure equity and improve
	•	Track enrollment, completion, and placement by region and demographic.
	•	Publish annual access and outcomes dashboards.
	•	Fund what works based on verified job placement, wage gains, and employer reported performance.

column2:
This survey is being lambasted on social media for its inherent biases, overuse of jargon, and inability to define what AI is in the context of this survey. The questions assume that the public wants AI, and gives nowhere to voice an opinion to the contrary without using the space given to another question.

The language used is inaccessible, and does not follow Canada.ca guidelines for web style or plain language, putting it in contradiction of the Directive on the Management of Communications and Federal Identity. This directive explicitly states that it covers public opinion research: https://www.tbs-sct.canada.ca/pol/doc-eng.aspx?id=30682.

The framing of this survey, and its inaccessible language, ensures that the average member of the Canadian public will be unable to give their opinion, leaving only stakeholders in AI who understand the jargon and can offer answers that help to steer policy in a direction beneficial to themselves, rather than one which might take public concerns into account (for example, AI's negative impacts on the environment, the studies into how it causes brain drain, the loss of jobs that have already taken place in and out of government because of it, its negative impacts on child development, and the way that it threatens the decimation of the arts sector).

The framing of this survey, and its inaccessible language, ensures that the average member of the Canadian public will be unable to give their opinion, leaving only stakeholders in AI who understand the jargon and can offer answers that help to steer policy in a direction beneficial to themselves, rather than one which might take public concerns into account (for example, AI's negative impacts on the environment, the studies into how it causes brain drain, the loss of jobs that have already taken place in and out of government because of it, its negative impacts on child development, and the way that it threatens the decimation of the arts sector).

column2:
Strong critical thinking skills and media literacy (not only AI literacy), as well as strong historical and economic education, are all necessary for workers to help contextualize AI and the role it can play in their work and their lives.

Furthermore, up-to-date resources on widespread AI models is important, and an ecosystem of public, transparent, open datasets and algorithms will allow private individuals to experiment with AI themselves more easily, which in turn will create more social knowledge about AI's strengths and limitations.
column3:
Basic critical thinking education is one of the most powerful tools for this and many other issues in today's information economy. Canada's core critical thinking teaching in schools is exceptionally poor, and needs massive support. Many Canadians cannot distinguish between roughly similar but logically different statements, and don't approach fact-checking or hypothesizing with a strong sense of logic. Critical thinking training in schools should be mandatory, and should not only cover AI.

Beyond regular schooling, Canada needs to do a better job of ensuring availability of factual information for Canadians. Low technological and media literacy is connected with low information about science, history, and society. A robust information ecosystem and easily accessible public data on Canada's society and government can help to some degree here. Canada also needs to start acting to fight misinformation and disinformation, as these create a fractured and muddied sense of reality among the Canadian population that prevents AI literacy from effectively spreading through culture.
column4: Canada needs to recognize the amount of learning that happens through social media, and regulate social media accordingly. As long as social media remains an unregulated morass of culture war and misinformation, it will be difficult for Canadians to identify and practice reliable AI literacy skills, and indeed there may be significant resistance to the basic concept of AI literacy. Social media prevents AI literacy skills that are acquired in targeted government or private programs from effectively spreading through society.

column2: Focus on supporting lifelong learning, skills will stagnate

The skills required for the modern digital economy begin with understanding change itself. AI is not a disruption from the outside—it is part of humanity’s ongoing evolution, as transformative to this century as electricity or the printing press were to theirs. To thrive in this environment, people must be taught to adapt, to see change not as a threat but as a natural process that education prepares them for. Education is the foundation of life, and from that foundation AI should emerge as a partner to human progress, not a replacement for it.

This same principle of adaptability must shape how Canada governs AI. Traditional policymaking moves too slowly for a technology that evolves by the month. In Parliament, bills can take years to pass, but AI cannot wait that long. Canada needs a dedicated AI policy mechanism—a standing review system that continuously updates regulations every few months based on new discoveries, ethical concerns, and global developments. Such an adaptive policy loop would allow the country to stay current, responsive, and globally competitive while ensuring that innovation never outpaces accountability. Building a living policy framework for AI is not just good governance—it’s the only way to govern in an era defined by constant transformation.
column3:
AI literacy should not be treated as a technical skill but as a form of critical consciousness. Children must grow up understanding not just how to use AI tools, but how to think alongside them—how to question, interpret, and guide them. In schools, AI education should begin with teaching cognitive biases and how they shape both human judgment and algorithmic outputs. Students should learn that AI can “hallucinate,” that digital spaces often mirror personal interests rather than objective reality, and that truth online requires triangulation from multiple sources.

Equally important is cultivating imagination and agency. Young people should see AI as an extension of their creative and analytical mind, not a substitute for it. They should learn to lead creation rather than be led by algorithms, to design with intention instead of passively consuming. When students learn to analyze abundant information, detect bias, cross-check perspectives, and create with awareness, AI becomes a tool of empowerment rather than manipulation. Teaching these principles early would produce a generation that sees AI not as a mystery, but as a partner in thought—one they can guide with wisdom, imagination, and integrity.
column4:
To ensure equitable access to AI literacy, Canada must first make AI tools themselves accessible. Many citizens simply can’t justify paying for advanced AI subscriptions, and yet these tools are quickly becoming as essential as search engines once were. The government should provide free or subsidized AI access—especially for students, small businesses, researchers, and underserved communities—so that cost never becomes a barrier to learning or creativity. Equal access to the tools of intelligence is the foundation of equal opportunity in the digital age.

Beyond access, AI education should be contextual and relevant. Government bodies, research institutions, and private partners should organize tailored seminars and workshops designed for specific socioeconomic groups. For agricultural communities, AI literacy should focus on how intelligent systems can enhance crop monitoring, automate irrigation, or predict weather patterns. For healthcare workers, training should explore AI in diagnostics, data management, and patient care. The goal is to make AI tangible—something that improves daily life rather than a distant abstraction.

Finally, Canada should create open channels for idea-sharing. Every citizen, regardless of field, should be encouraged to contribute their thoughts, innovations, and suggestions on how AI could be applied in practice. Even if someone’s idea lies outside their expertise, connecting it with those in relevant sectors could spark collaboration and breakthrough. True AI literacy isn’t just about understanding machines—it’s about empowering people to imagine, connect, and build together.

column2:
Canada’s education and skills model is an artifact of a slower era. Top-down government programs cannot keep pace with the exponential velocity of AI.
The core problem is not a lack of skills supply, but a catastrophic lack of domestic demand. We train world-class talent only to see them leave for opportunities in the US. The government's primary role, therefore, is not to be the teacher, but to create a winning economy that demands talent.
First, execute the "Grand Bargain" to build a domestic demand engine for AI jobs.
Second, enable a new, high-speed ecosystem of learning built on three pillars:
1. The Living Curriculum: The static textbook is dead. Unlock open-source knowledge (e.g., MIT's OCW) and use AI as a real-time curator to ensure students learn from yesterday's breakthroughs, not last decade's theories.
2. The AI Co-Pilot: AI is the most powerful teaching tool ever invented. We must deploy AI tutors at scale and provide subsidized access to cloud compute and robotics "digital sandboxes." This allows learners to move from theory to building instantly, accelerating mastery.
3. The Nimble Incubator: Skills are wasted if ideas cannot reach the market. Create "innovation sandboxes" with minimal bureaucratic friction for AI and robotics startups, and ensure the best ideas are matched swiftly with venture capital.
This is not a slow, top-down program—it is a national engine built on creating economic demand, then unleashing our innovators to meet it with speed and agility.
column3:
Traditional "AI literacy" campaigns are a flawed and patronizing approach. The public does not need government-led PowerPoints to understand AI; they need direct, hands-on experience in an environment that naturally exposes AI's strengths and weaknesses. True literacy is a byproduct of a competitive, transparent ecosystem, not a top-down educational program.
The most effective way to enhance AI literacy is to create this ecosystem:
Literacy Through Use, Not Lectures: The fastest way to understand an AI's limitations is to use a bad one. By fostering a fiercely competitive domestic market for AI tools—spurred by the "Grand Bargain" and a pro-innovation regulatory environment—we ensure that Canadians are exposed to a wide variety of AI products. The free market is the most effective teacher: users will quickly learn to identify biased, unreliable, or "hallucinating" models when they can freely choose superior alternatives.
Literacy Through Radical Transparency: Mandating that AI products used in the public sector are "open-source by default" is the most powerful literacy tool we can deploy. When citizens, journalists, and researchers can directly inspect the code and audit the data of the AI systems that affect their lives, they develop a deep, authentic understanding of how these systems work, where their biases lie, and how they can fail. This is far more effective than any government awareness campaign.
Literacy Through Accountability: True awareness of risk comes from seeing consequences. By establishing a clear norm of absolute corporate liability for AI-induced harm, we force companies to be radically transparent about their products' limitations and biases as a matter of self-preservation. When companies are incentivized to educate their users on the risks, the public's practical literacy will skyrocket.
We don't need to teach Canadians about AI. We need to create an environment where they can learn for themselves.
column4:
Centralized government programs are an inherently slow and inequitable way to deliver education in the AI age. They create bureaucracy and geographic disparities. The most effective path to universal AI literacy is to radically decentralize access to knowledge and tools.
Embrace the Great Equalizer: The AI Tutor.
The AI tutor is the single most powerful tool for equity in education. It is infinitely patient, available 24/7, adapts to any learning style, and scales at near-zero marginal cost. The government's primary role is to ensure every Canadian has high-speed internet access, then get out of the way and let the market build and deploy these revolutionary tools.
Democratize the Tools, Not Just the Lessons.
True equity is about the power to build, not just to learn. The government's most effective equity program would be to provide universal, subsidized access to the core tools of creation: cloud compute and robotics simulation environments. Treating these "digital sandboxes" as a public utility gives every Canadian, regardless of background, the means to innovate.
Foster a Competitive Market.
A vibrant, competitive market is the ultimate engine of accessibility. The government must create an environment where companies compete to build the cheapest, most effective, and most user-friendly AI tools. This market-driven race to the bottom on price and complexity will deliver equitable access far more effectively than any top-down government program.
Equity is not achieved by creating more government gatekeepers. It is achieved by removing them and empowering individuals directly.

column2:
The modern digital economy does not require a workforce trained in specific tools, which will be obsolete in months. It requires a workforce forged in two timeless, foundational capabilities:

1. The Universal Skill: Cognitive Sovereignty.
In a world saturated by the "Asch Machine"—AI-driven propaganda and sophisticated falsehoods—the single most critical skill for every citizen is the ability to think critically, discern truth from manipulation, and verify information. This is the bedrock of a resilient society.

Canada can best support this by creating a "National Crucible for Critical Thought." This is a public, digital gymnasium where citizens can safely interact with and learn to identify deepfakes, biased algorithms, and deceptive AI-generated content. This moves digital literacy from a passive classroom exercise to an active, national self-defence training.

2. The Specialist Skill: Agentic Collaboration.
For the AI-focused workforce, the key skill is shifting from being a "user" of AI to being a "Steward" of it. This is the art of directing, guiding, and auditing autonomous AI systems to solve complex problems. It is the skill of partnership, not just programming.

Canada can support this by establishing "Sovereign Forges"—public-private partnerships where the next generation of AI architects learn their craft by building transparent, "Glass Box" AI systems for national-scale projects. This provides a real-world apprenticeship in building trustworthy AI, creating a class of builders who are not just technically proficient but doctrinally aligned with the principles of accountability and transparency.
column3:
To enhance AI literacy, Canada must abandon passive awareness campaigns and forge a national, experiential learning ecosystem. Literacy is not achieved by telling citizens AI is biased; it is achieved by giving them the tools to see the bias for themselves.

This requires a three-pillar strategy:

1. The National Crucible: A Digital Flight Simulator for the Mind.
The government must build a public, interactive digital environment where citizens can safely engage with real-world AI failure modes. In this "flight simulator," they can learn to spot deepfakes, interact with biased algorithms, and identify AI-generated propaganda. This is not a course; it is an inoculation. It builds a national "epistemic immune system" by training citizens to recognize manipulation through direct, hands-on experience.

2. "Glass Box" Certification: A Nutrition Label for AI.
 The government must mandate a "Glass Box" certification for all public-facing AI. This is a simple, clear "nutrition label" that discloses an AI's training data, known limitations, and potential biases. This empowers consumers to make informed choices and holds developers accountable for the integrity of their products.
The Steward's Mandate: Forging Internal Guardians.
For the workplace, Canada must support the development of a new professional credential: the 

3. "Sovereign AI Steward." These are not data scientists, but internal auditors trained to be the ethical and operational guardians of AI within their organizations. They are the human-on-the-loop, ensuring that AI is deployed responsibly from the inside out.
column4:
To ensure equitable access to AI literacy, Canada must treat it as essential public infrastructure, available to all citizens regardless of location or socioeconomic status. This is achieved through three pillars of democratic access:

1.Government's Role: Build the Public Utility.
The government must fund and build the "National Crucible" as a free, sovereign, and open-source digital public utility. It must be as accessible as a national park. This is the government's non-delegable duty—to provide the common ground where all citizens can forge the skills of cognitive self-defence. By making it a sovereign utility, Canada ensures that access is never controlled by foreign corporate interests.

2. The Library as the Local Forge.
This national utility must be distributed locally. The most effective and equitable delivery system is Canada's public library network. Libraries are the most trusted institutions in every community, serving all demographics. They must be equipped and funded to become the "local forges" for AI literacy, providing free public terminals and guided access to the National Crucible.

3. Industry's Role: Fuel the Open-Source Commons.
The private sector's role is not charity, but contribution. Any company that benefits from the national AI ecosystem—through government contracts or liability protections—must be required to contribute their foundational tools for bias detection and verification back to the open-source national toolchain. This creates a virtuous cycle, where the public utility is perpetually improved by the very industry it supports.

This is not about teaching a class; it is about issuing every citizen a key to the digital forge.

column3: Public awareness campaigns about the hazards of genAI. Making sure that people know that it absolutely cannot think. It is a Chinese Room scenario and has no clue what the input means nor what its output means, just that some outputs for some inputs get affirmative responses. Treating it as though it has knowledge and insights that it can impart engages our very human instinct to pack bond with anything that we can reflect a personality onto. Whether this be a roomba with googly eyes or a chatbot that uses personal pronouns, it's a very human instinct to see it as a creature we can love. But this is dangerous with genAI because it does not have a moral guide or even the ability to understand that it has said something untrue or harmful. We need to teach people that it cannot even know language and therefore cannot be trusted to tell truthful things.

column2:
As much as we need to talk about advanced skills for a modern, digital economy, most people are simply not there yet. I work in information/data management and can confidently tell you that most people barely have basic tech literacy (i.e., using a computer, MS office, Google, doing their own research/information literacy). Many of these people are actively in the workforce and the main tool they use everyday is a computer, and they barely know how to use it. Our schools are not doing a god enough job at teaching basic tech literacy skills. I see people of both older and younger generations who have no clue how to bookmark a web page or bcc someone on an email. It's pretty scary to be honest, so I highly doubt that most people (unless they are tech literate or have IT backgrounds) will be able to learn these advanced skills quickly, and they definitely don't have the ability to manage a plethora of AI generated data. 

For those who are tech literate, skill development can include intro to AI courses, database management and design, Canadian information policy and ethics, AI prompt engineering basics, cybersecurity, information governance, data sovereignty, cultural considerations for AI use, etc. 

I would personally like to see a Canadian government developed AI training program that can provide credible, factual information free of bias. I am trying to learn as much as I can about AI and this is already a challenge for someone who is information and tech literate (there is so much out there, hence a lot of sifting through inaccurate, misleading, or biased information). I feel that such a program would be invaluable for those of us already in AI adjacent sectors so that we can champion responsible AI use in our existing work.
column3: Targeted public education campaign, online open educational resources (one-page info sheets, online course modules, video series, etc.), and best practices guides. I think it's also important to produce resources targeted at certain populations (i.e., youth, elders, Indigenous Peoples, etc.) as this would provide tailored information and real life examples of how they would interact with AI. Many folks on social media have no idea that up to 30% of what they see on their feeds is AI generated, nor do they know how to tell the difference between something real and something AI.
column4: Definitely collaborate with other levels of government (or at least keep them in the loop and let them know what resources are available), incentivize public and private sectors to provide staff training on AI and develop company policies on AI use (having templates available would help), develop MOOC courses in partnership with Canadian universities so that anyone may educate themselves on AI. I also think it is crucial that anyone working in a public-facing role (i.e., nurses, doctors, social workers, teachers, etc.) be given this training for free as they often have to educate their clients as part of their work.

column2:
In a modern digital economy, Canada’s workforce needs a combination of technical, analytical, and human-centered skills. Core technical competencies include AI and machine learning literacy, data science, software engineering, cloud computing, cybersecurity, and digital infrastructure management. Complementing these are critical soft skills: problem-solving, ethical reasoning, digital literacy, adaptability, and collaboration across interdisciplinary teams. Workers must also understand privacy, safety, and bias implications of AI systems to deploy them responsibly, ensuring technology enhances rather than undermines public trust.

Canada can support skill development through multi-tiered, flexible programs that address both AI-specialized careers and broader workforce readiness. For AI-focused roles, expand funded post-secondary programs, co-op placements, and industry-academia partnerships that provide hands-on experience with privacy-safe, ethical AI systems. Mid-career reskilling initiatives should target professionals in traditional sectors (healthcare, finance, energy) with short courses, certifications, and mentorship on AI integration, cybersecurity, and responsible data use. For the wider workforce, national digital-literacy campaigns, online learning platforms, and community-based programs can build foundational skills and ethical awareness. Government incentives—such as subsidies for private-sector training, tax credits for employer-led reskilling, and publicly funded AI labs—can accelerate adoption. Embedding lifelong learning and ethical AI awareness into every stage of career development ensures Canadians can adapt rapidly to technological change while deploying AI responsibly, supporting both economic competitiveness and public trust.
column3:
Enhancing AI literacy in Canada requires a multi-layered approach that reaches workplaces, educational institutions, and the general public. Programs should teach not only how AI works, but also its limitations, biases, and ethical implications, including risks to privacy and manipulative uses. In workplaces, targeted training and credentialing can upskill employees in data literacy, AI ethics, and responsible deployment practices, particularly in regulated sectors like healthcare, finance, and public services. Post-secondary institutions should integrate AI literacy into broader curricula, offering modules on algorithmic bias, interpretability, and privacy-by-design principles.

Public awareness campaigns can use interactive tools, workshops, and media to explain AI’s strengths and weaknesses in plain language, empowering citizens to critically engage with AI-driven services. Canada can draw on international best practices such as the EU’s AI education initiatives, Singapore’s national AI literacy framework, and the U.K.’s public-facing AI explainability programs, adapting them to Canadian contexts with multilingual, inclusive outreach. Partnerships with civil society, libraries, and community organizations can ensure accessibility across age groups and regions. By combining formal credentials, workplace programs, and broad public campaigns, Canada can foster a population and workforce that understands AI’s capabilities and limits, promoting responsible use and informed trust.
column4:
Canada can ensure equitable AI literacy by combining federal leadership with provincial, municipal, and community partnerships to reach diverse regions, populations, and socioeconomic groups. The federal government can provide funding and guidance for inclusive digital literacy programs, while provinces and municipalities implement locally tailored initiatives through schools, libraries, and community centers. Programs should prioritize underserved and rural communities, Indigenous populations, and groups historically underrepresented in tech, offering free or subsidized access to training, mentorship, and AI learning resources.

Industry and the private sector have a complementary role by sponsoring workforce upskilling, providing hands-on learning platforms, and creating mentorship networks that expose participants to real-world AI applications while reinforcing privacy, ethics, and bias awareness. Educational strategies should include age-appropriate curricula, online and in-person workshops, and gamified tools that teach AI’s capabilities, limitations, and social impact. National campaigns can raise awareness of responsible AI use, while partnerships with civil society ensure materials are culturally relevant and linguistically accessible. Coordinated federal, provincial, and private efforts can reduce regional and demographic gaps, empowering all Canadians to participate knowledgeably in a digital economy shaped by AI.

Answering this question requires us to consider how to increase access to information across all of Canada for any topic. Libraries are always a great means for ensuring access to information across all groups in society, but some areas will have greater challenges than others.

For example, there are more than likely current issues with broadband internet access in Canada's rural areas and Northern Territories - if Canada has yet to improve internet access in these areas, then it should do so now using home-grown technological solutions (such as Satellites, fiberoptic cables, etc.). So too are there issues with Canadian's ability to access news as small town newspapers close down - a role that a well-funded CBC backed by strong coast-to-coast Radio and Television Broadcast coverage can fill.

In other words, to ensure equitable access to AI literacy across all groups in Canada, current gaps in Canada's communications and information systems must be identified and addressed.

column2: A modern digital economy depends on two kinds of skills: deep technical expertise and adaptable digital fluency. Canada needs more engineers, data scientists, and AI specialists, but also a much broader base of workers who can understand and apply technology in daily work. The focus should be on critical thinking, problem-solving, and data literacy rather than just coding. Government and industry should co-fund continuous learning programs that let people reskill quickly as tools evolve, including micro-credentials and employer-led apprenticeships. The goal is a workforce that can adapt as fast as technology changes, with training designed around real market needs rather than academic silos.
column3: AI literacy should become part of every Canadian’s learning path, not limited to the tech sector. Workplace programs, public education campaigns, and partnerships with colleges can teach how AI systems make decisions, where bias can creep in, and how to interpret outputs responsibly. People don’t need to become engineers to use AI effectively, but they do need to understand its boundaries and risks. Sharing international best practices from countries like Finland, which introduced nationwide AI education, could help Canada build practical, scalable programs that reach workers, students, and public servants alike. When people understand both the potential and the pitfalls of AI, they can use it more confidently and responsibly.
column4: Equitable access to AI literacy starts with recognizing that not all Canadians have the same connectivity, resources, or educational opportunities. Rural and Indigenous communities, newcomers, and lower-income groups need targeted support through local training centers, community colleges, and online programs designed for low-bandwidth access. Governments at all levels should work with industry to provide free or low-cost digital literacy programs, using libraries, schools, and public service networks as entry points. Industry has a key role to play by funding scholarships, mentorships, and toolkits that make AI knowledge accessible outside major cities. True inclusion means every Canadian, regardless of geography or income, has the chance to understand and benefit from AI rather than being left behind by it.

column2:
The foundation of a modern, digital economy is technical competence paired with critical understanding. Canada’s workforce needs more than coders or data scientists—it needs digitally fluent citizens able to understand, question, and safely use AI and emerging technologies across every profession.

That begins with education reform, not short-term training. AI, data, and systems literacy should be taught from primary school through to professional development. Every graduate, regardless of discipline, should understand how digital systems make decisions, what data ethics mean in practice, and how to use evidence to navigate an algorithmic world.

Equally important is rapid skill adaptation. Canada should fund modular, stackable credentials in areas such as AI assurance, cybersecurity, data governance, and digital identity—skills that translate across sectors. Public servants should be required and supported to build technical literacy, ensuring that policy, procurement, and oversight are grounded in reality rather than abstraction.

Canada must move beyond “awareness” toward competence—creating a population that participates confidently in innovation rather than watching it happen elsewhere. A digitally literate nation is the surest safeguard of both competitiveness and trust.
column3:
Canada must approach AI literacy as a long-term civic and economic investment, not a short-term communications exercise. Public awareness campaigns tend to oversimplify; true literacy means understanding, not reassurance.

AI literacy should be woven through all levels of education and professional life—from early curricula to executive training—emphasizing how AI systems work, where they fail, and what responsible oversight looks like. This includes demystifying key concepts such as model bias, explainability, and evaluation, using examples from everyday systems like biometric verification or recommendation engines.

Programs should prioritize critical thinking over hype, enabling people to recognize both the potential and the limits of AI. This requires collaboration between academia, standards bodies, and government to ensure that training reflects international best practices and validated evidence—not public perception or selective media narratives.

Finally, AI literacy must extend to the public service itself. Policymakers and executives who cannot grasp AI’s mechanisms or boundaries cannot regulate or adopt it responsibly.

Canada can lead by building a society of informed, critical, and confident digital citizens, capable of using and governing AI wisely—an advantage far more durable than any single technology.
column4:
To ensure equitable access to AI literacy, Canada must treat digital and AI education as a national infrastructure priority, not a communications exercise. Confidence in AI stems from knowledge and understanding, not from being reassured.

First, Canada should commit to universal AI education—integrating digital and algorithmic literacy into curricula across provinces and territories, from early education to post-secondary and professional programs. AI literacy should evolve into a basic civic skill, enabling every Canadian to understand and question the systems that shape their digital lives.

Second, the federal government must work with provinces, municipalities, and Indigenous governments to ensure access across all regions and demographics, including rural and remote areas. Public libraries, community colleges, and online learning platforms can act as local hubs for accessible lifelong learning.

Third, industry and academia should collaborate to make open, standards-aligned educational resources available to learners of all ages, backed by certifications that translate into employable skills.

Equitable AI literacy means empowerment through competence, ensuring every Canadian—regardless of geography or background—can engage confidently and critically with the technologies that define our future.

column2:
The internet is rife with misinformation and disinformation that has been facilitated by AI. In many cases, this is simply because people can very easily set up "slop" websites which attract visitors with low-quality information so that they can turn a modest profit by displaying ads. This has affected almost every sector that relies on information from the internet in some way, and firms are not equipped to handle it.

Furthermore, the nature of these technologies are changing so rapidly that it is challenging for individuals to stay up to date on how such "slop" can be identified. In practice, pretty much everyone needs to become an amateur fact-checker just to keep from being fooled. Perhaps the government of Canada could develop free educational materials or programs for the CBC which promote media literacy. Honestly, I have seen AI researchers themselves get fooled by such content at an alarming frequency, so I'm skeptical about what efficacy rates could be expected from such programs.
column3:
I don't think it's sufficient to try and educate individuals about these harms while their main sources of information (large tech companies like Meta, Microsoft, and Google) have pivoted their entire companies to operationalize such technologies at the greatest scale possible.

Start by holding these companies accountable for the irresponsible promotion of such technologies, for instance, by requiring Google to provide clear disclaimers about their AI summaries' tendency to promote inaccurate and dangerous results. Make the inclusion of such summaries opt-in rather than opt-out. Issue prohibitively expensive fines to companies that do not comply. Stop going easy on big tech.
column4: Your use of the phrase "informed confidence" makes me think you are more concerned with people feeling good about using AI than about minimizing its harms. I believe we should be treating the inclusion of AI in most products like we do lead in paints, or asbestos in Canadian buildings. It is in many cases, quite simply, a dangerous technology that should not be integrated except in particular low-risk circumstances.

column3: AI literacy requires media literacy and critical thinking skills. There needs to be public awareness campaigns in every medium. People who don't even own a computer will be impacted.
column4: Teachers need to be trained properly in the risks and benefits of these tools, so provincial governments need to be on board as more than AI cheerleaders. The private sector has to have ethical guardrails instead of seeing increased profits that come from getting rid of workers.

column2: A modern, digital economy requires that the workforce first has access to the necessities. You cannot develop an industry without the workers having a stable living situation, access to food and a sense of safety and security. By providing AI learning centres throughout the country, you support the individuals hit hardest by the challenges and get valuable feedback. You also create a space where new talent can access the skills they need to enter the industry and add to its growth and development.
column3: By working through learning organizations already in communities, you gain access to their participants that already see the value in learning new skills. We frequently assist individuals that have limited understanding of technology, and a slow approach is needed for some to build them up to a point when they can use AI with confidence. column2:
Across Canada
Building trust in AI requires clear responsibility between those who govern AI and those who deploy it.
The government should focus on transparency, standards, and accountability, while industry demonstrates trust through auditable and privacy-preserving implementations.
T-RIZE Perspective
T-RIZE’s role is in enabling responsible adoption by giving institutions the infrastructure to use AI safely from day one.
Through Rizemind and the T-RIZE Labs × ÉTS Industrial Research Chair led by Professor Kaiwen Zhang, we’re developing frameworks that let organizations collaborate securely on shared intelligence while retaining data control.
This creates a model of “trust by architecture” — where confidence in AI comes from verifiable design, not marketing or regulation alone.
For broader citizen engagement or awareness programs, T-RIZE does not take a position. Our contribution is to equip regulated industries with the technical means to demonstrate compliance, transparency, and integrity in practice.
column3:
Across Canada
AI literacy must move beyond theoretical ethics toward applied understanding—how models behave, where bias originates, and how privacy and accountability can be built into systems.
Real literacy means giving professionals the tools to interpret, validate, and question AI outputs, not just consume them.
T-RIZE Perspective
T-RIZE advances AI literacy through practical, research-driven collaboration.
The T-RIZE Labs × ÉTS Industrial Research Chair (Prof. Kaiwen Zhang, funded by Mitacs and NSERC) develops federated learning and secure communication frameworks that serve as living examples of privacy-preserving AI in action.
These initiatives train graduate and industry participants to understand model limitations, data bias, and distributed governance through direct experimentation—not classroom abstraction.
We believe literacy improves when organizations can see, test, and verify responsible AI design themselves.
T-RIZE does not take a position on broad national awareness campaigns but supports applied-AI education programs that link academic research to institutional deployment, ensuring Canada’s workforce can build and maintain trustworthy, privacy-first systems.
column4:
T-RIZE contributes by making applied AI and privacy-preserving technologies accessible through research–industry collaboration.
The T-RIZE Labs × ÉTS Industrial Research Chair provides a model for how academic institutions outside the largest AI hubs can participate in cutting-edge, federally funded research.
By focusing on federated learning, T-RIZE’s work allows organizations of any size to engage in collective model training without sharing or exporting data, reducing barriers for institutions with limited resources or strict data-sovereignty constraints.
Canada can further this goal by supporting regional innovation hubs and industry-linked programs that help organizations gain direct experience with trustworthy, privacy-first AI systems.
T-RIZE does not take a position on national funding or voucher programs but supports initiatives that make hands-on, verifiable AI education accessible to institutions across all regions and sectors.

column2:
A modern workforce needs both technical fluency and creative problem-solving. AI and automation are changing jobs across every sector, not just tech. That means workers need data literacy, critical thinking, and an understanding of how to use AI tools safely and effectively in their field.

Canada should expand practical training programs that combine technical skills with domain knowledge. Programs in healthcare, media, logistics, and manufacturing should all include modules on AI use and ethics. Short, stackable credentials that can be earned while working would help people adapt without leaving the workforce.

Partnerships between colleges, universities, and industry can make these programs more relevant. Public funding should support on-the-job learning, not just traditional education. The goal is to create a workforce that understands AI well enough to guide it, not be replaced by it.
column3:
AI literacy should start early and continue through every stage of life. Schools can teach the basics of how algorithms work, how data shapes outcomes, and why bias can appear in automated systems. For adults, training should focus on how to use AI responsibly in their own work, with real examples that relate to their industry.

Government and public institutions can lead public awareness campaigns that show both the benefits and the limits of AI. Media literacy should also include AI literacy, helping people recognize synthetic content and understand how recommendation systems influence what they see online.

Partnerships with industry, libraries, and community organizations can make these lessons accessible to everyone. When people understand AI’s strengths and weaknesses, they can use it with confidence and question it when needed.
column4:
AI literacy must reach beyond major cities and universities. Rural communities, Indigenous populations, and lower-income Canadians all need opportunities to learn how AI affects their work and lives. Canada can achieve this by funding community-based training programs delivered through local schools, libraries, and digital learning hubs.

Public institutions should provide free or low-cost online courses and resources that explain AI in simple, practical terms. Regional colleges and local employers can partner to create training that fits local industries, whether that’s farming, forestry, media, or manufacturing.

Connectivity is also part of equity. Reliable internet access and affordable devices are prerequisites for digital learning. Closing that gap should be part of any AI education plan. True inclusion means everyone can understand, question, and benefit from AI, no matter where they live or what they do.

column2: In a modern, digital economy, skills such as data literacy, computational thinking, problem-solving, and adaptability are essential. Canada can support their development by integrating digital skills into primary, secondary, and post-secondary education, while also offering accessible upskilling programs for the current workforce. Public-private partnerships can facilitate AI-focused career pathways, offering certifications and apprenticeships that allow individuals to gain hands-on experience. Workforce readiness programs should focus on continuous learning, enabling rapid adaptation to technological changes by offering training in emerging fields like machine learning, data analysis, and automation. By aligning education with industry needs, Canada can prepare both AI specialists and workers in all sectors to thrive in the evolving digital economy.
column3: To enhance AI literacy, Canada must create a comprehensive strategy that includes workplace training programs, public awareness campaigns, and educational curricula addressing AI’s potential and limitations. Awareness of AI’s biases and ethical challenges should be woven into these efforts. Offering accessible credentials, such as certifications or micro-credentials, would enable professionals to gain a deeper understanding of AI’s practical applications and ethical concerns. Public awareness campaigns should aim to educate all Canadians on how AI affects their lives, addressing common misconceptions and helping people understand the implications of AI in areas like privacy, fairness, and decision-making. International best practices, like those from countries with advanced AI strategies, can inform these efforts and ensure they are globally competitive.
column4: To ensure equitable access to AI literacy, Canada must collaborate across all levels of government and with the private sector to make learning opportunities universally accessible. Targeted initiatives can address regional disparities by offering online courses, community workshops, and mobile learning programs that cater to underrepresented or economically disadvantaged groups. Industry involvement is crucial in providing resources and expertise to deliver these programs, as well as offering internships and apprenticeships to marginalized communities. By fostering partnerships between educational institutions, businesses, and government, Canada can create a more inclusive digital workforce, ensuring that all Canadians, regardless of background, have the opportunity to engage with AI in an informed and meaningful way.
