column2:
Canada must engage citizens and businesses through trusted public institutions that connect technology with accountability. Public libraries are central to this work because they combine access, neutrality, and education.

Libraries should lead national AI literacy programs that teach residents how AI works, how to protect data, and how to recognize bias. They can host public demonstrations, consultations, and dialogue sessions where communities question and assess AI in plain language. Libraries can explain the benefits and risks of AI through workshops, exhibitions, and online learning modules accessible to all ages and abilities.

The federal government should fund a coordinated AI Trust Program delivered through libraries, schools, and community centres. This would standardize educational materials, track public confidence, and ensure consistent outreach across provinces and territories. Municipal governments and library boards can partner with local colleges, start-ups, and technology firms to develop case studies that show transparent, ethical AI use in daily life.

Businesses must contribute by co-funding library-based programs and disclosing how their systems use data, automate processes, and maintain fairness. Universities can help libraries interpret and share AI research in clear, community-focused ways.

All engagement should reach rural and underserved communities with multilingual, accessible content. Every AI system used in public services should have a plain-language summary available through local libraries and online portals.

Trust grows when learning happens in familiar public spaces where people feel ownership. Public libraries connect research, business, and government with residents, ensuring that responsible AI governance is informed by lived experience. When libraries lead AI education and engagement, Canadians see transparency in practice, not theory, and trust follows through understanding, participation, and shared accountability.
column3: Canada strengthens AI literacy by embedding it in trusted public institutions, with libraries as the national delivery network. Libraries already serve as digital learning hubs, making them ideal to teach how AI works, where it fails, and how it affects daily life. Libraries should lead a coordinated AI Literacy Framework supported by federal and provincial funding. This framework would provide consistent public education on data bias, privacy, misinformation, and ethical use. Programs would focus on helping citizens understand AI’s purpose, limitations, and accountability.Workplace training should include short, credentialed modules developed with colleges, unions, and employers. These modules would cover responsible AI use, data ethics, and interpreting outputs. Sector examples should include health, education, and municipal government. Public engagement campaigns run through library systems would demystify AI. Interactive exhibits, community workshops, and bilingual outreach would reach youth, seniors, and newcomers. Libraries would partner with schools and colleges to integrate AI literacy into digital citizenship programs and support teachers with ready-to-use materials. Professional development for librarians, educators, and public servants is essential. Create national micro-credentials to recognize staff trained in explaining AI systems, bias, and governance. Internationally, Canada should adapt best practices from Finland’s “Elements of AI,” Singapore’s “AI for Everyone,” and the EU’s public AI literacy models. Libraries can localize these to Canadian communities, ensuring accessibility in both official languages and Indigenous contexts. Success should be measured by increased participation, higher AI literacy scores, and equitable reach across regions. Growth in confidence, ethical awareness, and informed decision-making will demonstrate progress. By positioning libraries as the public face of AI literacy, Canada builds understanding through access, dialog
column4:
anada ensures equitable AI literacy by using public libraries as the national access network. Libraries exist in every community and already deliver digital learning to all ages and abilities. Federal and provincial governments should fund a National AI Literacy Program delivered through libraries, schools, and community centres. This program would provide consistent, free access to AI education in both official languages and in accessible formats.

Libraries should partner with municipalities, colleges, and Indigenous communities to design locally relevant programs that reflect cultural and regional needs. Industry and the private sector must co-fund these programs and supply transparent case studies that show real-world AI use.

Libraries should host workshops, exhibitions, and online learning modules that explain AI’s purpose, benefits, and limits. Outreach must include remote and rural regions using mobile technology labs and broadband grants.

Educators, librarians, and public servants need national training and micro-credentials to guide residents in responsible AI use. AI materials should be bilingual, accessible, and inclusive of diverse learning styles.

Success depends on collaboration: federal coordination, provincial delivery, municipal support, and private-sector participation. Libraries connect these partners while ensuring every Canadian regardless of income, location, or background has the knowledge to understand and question AI systems confidently and safely.

column2:
Canada’s competitiveness depends on both hard and soft skills. The next decade will be defined not by who learns to code, but by who learns to lead through change.

Hard skills include data literacy, process automation, cybersecurity, and digital tool integration. Workers must understand how data flows through a business and how to use AI safely to improve accuracy, speed, and output. These are now essential for every role that touches information, scheduling, inventory, or customer service.

Soft skills keep transformation alive. Critical thinking, communication, and adaptability are as important as technical ability. Workers and leaders must manage uncertainty, collaborate across functions, and make ethical decisions when using new technology.

Education and training must reflect life stages.
In K–12, focus on curiosity, data awareness, and problem solving through real projects.
In postsecondary, shift from lectures to applied learning that places students in real organizations.
For the existing workforce, prioritize reskilling through short, targeted programs that fit work schedules and address immediate business needs.
For leaders, emphasize operational transformation, communication, and change management.

Mentorship should be two-way, helping both mentors and learners reach personal and business goals.

Reskilling is not optional. Some jobs will disappear while others expand. The goal is to help Canadians move into higher-value work faster.

Demographics make this urgent. As baby boomers retire, decades of knowledge and work ethic risk being lost. Capturing and transferring that experience through mentorship, documentation, and training must be a national priority. Canada cannot afford a knowledge drain while facing a digital skills gap.
column3:
AI literacy means understanding how tools make decisions, what data they use, and where bias or risk can appear. It is not about learning to program; it is about learning to think critically about technology.
Start early. Introduce AI and data awareness in middle school through simple, hands-on projects like mapping community data or building basic automation tasks that teach both problem solving and responsibility. By high school, students should be able to assess data sources, understand privacy concepts, and recognize bias in algorithms.
In postsecondary, AI literacy must be built into every discipline. Engineers, business students, healthcare professionals, and trades should all learn how AI applies to their field. Applied courses that combine ethics, analytics, and automation are essential to build confidence and trust.
For the existing workforce, expand practical upskilling programs that use Canadian-made open-source curricula. Focus on how to use AI in context whether that’s scheduling, forecasting, risk analysis, or workflow automation. Training should be accessible, modular, and affordable, delivered through community colleges, chambers of commerce, and trade associations.
Finally, include the human side of literacy. Workers must understand their rights, responsibilities, and the limits of automation. Building confidence and agency in how to use these tools is key to acceptance and adoption.
column4:
Equitable access starts with the basics: connectivity, power, and the ability to learn. Every Canadian, no matter where they live, should have reliable internet, affordable electricity, and the training to use digital tools safely and confidently.
Create regional learning hubs through community colleges, chambers of commerce, and libraries. These hubs should blend in-person and online learning and be designed around the real needs of local industries. Training should be modular and portable so that workers can build skills over time, moving from basic digital literacy to applied AI use.
Connectivity is not optional. Many rural and northern communities still face slow or unreliable broadband. Expand infrastructure investment to close these gaps permanently. Device lending programs can ensure that students and workers without access to technology are not excluded from opportunity.
Emergency preparedness must also be part of the national plan. Rural areas often face greater risks when power or internet access fails. Expanding regional energy and grid capacity will not only support economic participation but also strengthen resilience during extreme weather and emergencies. Every community needs dependable power and communication networks to stay connected and safe.
Address demographic gaps directly. Older workers may need more time and support to adapt to digital tools, while younger workers benefit from mentorship and structured guidance. Encourage intergenerational learning models that bring these strengths together. This builds skills and preserves the knowledge base of retiring workers before it disappears.
Connectivity and literacy together form the foundation of economic and social resilience. Canada cannot claim to be an AI leader if half the country lacks the means to connect, learn, or operate in a digital economy.

column2:
dentify High-Value Workers: The AI tool analyzes existing government data (like years of tax filings via CRA, consistent employment in key sectors via ESDC records, duration of stay via IRCC data) to identify temporary workers who have demonstrated long-term commitment, economic contribution, and integration, even if their formal points score (CRS) is low due to factors like age or original education level.

Flag for Immediate PR Consideration: Instead of just flagging for a positive review, the system could flag these demonstrably integrated and economically valuable individuals for a dedicated, expedited pathway to Permanent Residency. The AI identifies the candidates; IRCC officers make the final decision but through a fast-tracked process designed for retention.

Define "Talented" Practically: This approach defines "talented" based on proven, long-term contribution and integration within Canada, rather than solely on initial entry criteria. The AI helps quantify this contribution objectively.

Address Retention: This directly tackles the problem of losing experienced workers who have already built lives and careers in Canada but face hurdles in the standard PR system. Granting them immediate PR secures their talent for Canada's economy and society.

In essence: You'd suggest using AI not to replace human decision-making, but to accurately and efficiently identify long-term, contributing temporary workers who are strong candidates for immediate PR, thereby retaining proven talent already within Canada. This uses AI to overcome the limitations of a purely points-based system for experienced residents.

column2:
For a modern digital economy, Canada needs to develop both technical and adaptable workforce skills and provide programs that allow rapid deployment and upskilling:

1. Key Skills

AI & data literacy: Machine learning, data analytics, AI ethics, and model deployment.

Digital fluency: Cloud computing, cybersecurity, software development, and digital collaboration tools.

Adaptability & problem-solving: Critical thinking, creative problem-solving, and continuous learning mindset.

Cross-functional & soft skills: Communication, teamwork, leadership, and change management in tech-driven environments.

2. Workforce Development Strategies

Formal education: Update curricula in universities, colleges, and K-12 to include AI, coding, and digital skills.

Reskilling & upskilling programs: Short courses, micro-credentials, and bootcamps for AI-focused and digital roles.

Public–private partnerships: Industry-driven training programs that align skills with real market needs.

Work-integrated learning: Co-op programs, apprenticeships, and secondments between industry and academia.

Lifelong learning incentives: Tax credits, subsidies, and online platforms to encourage continuous skill development.
column3:
Canada can enhance AI literacy by combining education, workplace training, and public engagement:

1. Workplace Training & Credentials

AI-focused certifications: Micro-credentials in machine learning, AI ethics, and data literacy.

Upskilling programs: Company-led workshops on AI limitations, biases, and responsible use.

Professional standards: Encourage professional bodies to embed AI ethics and literacy in continuing education.

2. Public Awareness & Engagement

National campaigns: Explain AI capabilities, risks, and biases in plain language via media and online platforms.

Community programs: Workshops, hackathons, and citizen science projects that demonstrate AI in real-world contexts.

Schools & universities: Integrate AI literacy into K-12 and post-secondary curricula, covering both technical and ethical dimensions.

3. International Best Practices

OECD and EU AI literacy initiatives: Leverage open-source teaching materials, bias detection toolkits, and ethical guidelines.

Benchmark programs: Adopt global AI awareness standards to ensure consistency across workplaces and public services.

Cross-border collaborations: Exchange programs with AI hubs (US, EU, Singapore) to keep Canadians current with best practices.
column4:
Canada can ensure equitable AI literacy by combining government coordination, industry participation, and inclusive educational strategies:

1. Multi-Level Government Collaboration

Federal–provincial partnerships to fund AI literacy programs across urban, rural, and remote regions.

Integrate AI literacy into public libraries, community centers, and adult education programs.

Provide targeted support for Indigenous communities and underrepresented groups.

2. Industry & Private Sector Role

Partner with schools, colleges, and universities to deliver workplace-ready AI training.

Sponsor community workshops, online courses, and mentorship programs for diverse populations.

Support scholarships, bootcamps, and micro-credential programs for underrepresented groups in tech.

3. Educational & Literacy Strategies

Embed AI education in K-12 and post-secondary curricula, emphasizing ethics, bias, and societal impacts.

Develop multilingual, accessible, and low-cost digital learning resources.

Use interactive programs (hackathons, citizen science, gamified learning) to engage broader demographics.

column2:
In-demand skills combine technical capability, data literacy, cloud architecture and applied machine learning with human competencies such as problem-solving, creativity, adaptability and ethical reasoning.
AI Strategy Task Force Consultation 11
While Canada has focused on cultivating AI developers, we are behind when it comes to preparing the broader population to adopt and implement the technology. It is now important to pivot to ensure AI competency encompasses the application of AI to solve real-world problems rather than focusing solely on developing theoretical solutions in search of a purpose. As the potential for AI becomes better understood, there will be opportunities to integrate it to transform sectors including healthcare, finance, construction, manufacturing and transportation. Canada needs to develop a broader talent pipeline with both AI competencies and the pragmatic knowledge of the sectors in which it can be applied for economic benefit.This requires an AI-enabled workforce, one where AI literacy is built into every post-secondary program in a way that is sectorally and occupationally relevant. The hands-on application of AI skills will ensure access is democratized and broad adoption is possible, while arming the population with an understanding of the risks and limitations inherent in a technology that is evolving quickly.
Polytechnics are well positioned to support this process because they train highly skilled technicians, analysts and frontline workers in every sector. Their applied, hands-on approach and close collaboration with industry partners stand to ensure a strong talent pipeline that can apply and integrate AI in a mindful, attentive way.
column3:
To strengthen AI literacy nationally, Canada should:
1. Support and expand the network of AI-focused applied research centres at polytechnics to help employers, particularly SMEs, integrate AI tools, develop sector-specific applications and access rapid, customized training. These centres connect applied research with workforce development, ensuring that AI literacy and adoption advance together
2. Create matching funds, training vouchers or training tax credits that encourage employers to enrol staff in accredited AI upskilling programs
Enhancing AI literacy in Canada requires more than integrating AI content into academic programs. It demands continuous engagement with employers and workers across all sectors, including those just beginning to explore AI adoption. Polytechnics are already playing this role through continuing education, corporate training and applied research partnerships that build AI awareness and capability from the ground up.
column4:
We recommend:
1. Canada strengthen its Labour Market Development Agreements to ensure funding is available to deliver AI upskilling via public post-secondary institutions rather than leaving the impact of LMDAs to political whims
2. Incentivizing employer participation by expanding training tax credits, providing training vouchers or offering cost-sharing mechanisms for SMEs that send their workers to accredited AI training programs
3. Ensuring equitable access to digital and AI infrastructure, ensuring broadband access and affordable devices for all learners, particularly in northern and rural regions
4. Providing dedicated access to compute resources to polytechnics and colleges undertaking pragmatic research partnerships with industry, ensuring resources are better shared across the research ecosystem
Ensuring equitable access to AI literacy is essential if Canada’s digital transition is to be inclusive and productive. Without intentional action, the benefits of AI adoption risk concentrating in urban, technology-dense centres, leaving small communities, underrepresented learners and mid-career workers behind. Businesses that lack sufficient resources – financial or human – also face barriers to AI adoption, speaking to the need to derisk engagement by using innovation intermediaries like polytechnics.
Polytechnics have deep connections with industry and are well-connected to smaller institutions located in rural and remote areas. They work directly with Indigenous communities, immigrant-serving organizations, government agencies and each other to identify and distribute best practices. This approach creates a national delivery network for inclusive digital training that should be catalyzed to meet the country’s AI skills development goals.

column2: A modern digital economy requires three layers of capability: technical fluency, applied problem-solving, and adaptive leadership. Canada needs both deep AI specialists and broadly AI-literate workers who can integrate these tools responsibly. Core skills include data literacy, statistics, coding, and machine learning for technical roles, plus product management, ethics, cybersecurity, and change management for applied and leadership roles. To build and deploy these skills, Canada should expand short, credentialed training delivered through colleges and polytechnics, fund employer-led upskilling tied to measurable productivity outcomes, and embed AI literacy and prompt engineering into all postsecondary programs. Launch national “AI in Practice” residencies that pair engineers, operators, and public servants to co-deploy real systems in health, manufacturing, and energy. Create a lifelong learning credit that individuals and employers can use for certified digital programs, and align immigration and visa policy to attract experienced technical operators, not just researchers. Finally, measure success by the share of workers using AI-enabled tools productively, not just by program enrollment, tying public funding to verified gains in efficiency, quality, or safety across sectors.
column3: Canada should enhance AI literacy by building practical, tiered education that reaches citizens, workers, and leaders. At the foundational level, introduce mandatory digital and AI literacy modules in schools, teaching how data, algorithms, and bias work in real contexts. For the workforce, fund short, stackable credentials through colleges, industry associations, and unions that cover safe AI use, data handling, and accountability in decision-making. Create sector-specific training for managers and operators that uses real tools and includes exercises on bias detection, audit interpretation, and escalation paths. In the public sector, require annual AI ethics and risk training for policy, procurement, and service delivery teams. Launch a national awareness campaign, similar to cybersecurity programs, that explains AI’s benefits, limits, and human oversight requirements through accessible examples. Partner with trusted institutions like libraries, community colleges, and Indigenous innovation centers to deliver inclusive learning. Follow international best practices by adopting open educational resources from OECD and UNESCO, and align curricula with global AI literacy frameworks so Canadian credentials are portable and credible.
column4: Canada can ensure equitable access to AI literacy by embedding it into existing public education, workforce, and community systems rather than creating siloed programs. Federal and provincial governments should co-fund an “AI Basics for All” curriculum delivered through schools, colleges, and public libraries, with content tailored for Indigenous communities, newcomers, and rural learners. Partner with municipalities, broadband providers, and the private sector to provide digital access, devices, and localized training hubs so no region is excluded. Industry should sponsor applied learning modules, apprenticeships, and tool access for workers in manufacturing, health, and agriculture, ensuring training reflects real workplace use. Unions and professional associations can help certify AI safety and bias awareness as part of ongoing credentials. Community organizations and Indigenous innovation centers should co-design culturally relevant learning that reflects data sovereignty and local priorities. To sustain reach, fund a national digital inclusion fund tied to measurable participation and employment outcomes, and publicly track regional uptake. Equitable literacy means every Canadian, regardless of geography or income—can understand, question, and safely use AI tools in their daily work and civic life.

column2:
The skills Canada needs for a modern, digital economy aren’t just technical — they’re human. Yes, math and data literacy matter, but the real foundation is resilience and integrity. People need to know how to think critically, solve problems under pressure, and recover when things don’t go their way. Right now, we’re raising generations that have been overly protected from failure. That might feel compassionate in the short term, but it’s crippling in the long run.

A modern economy runs on people who can adapt — who can learn fast, fail fast, and keep moving. That starts early. Our education system needs to stop passing students who aren’t ready. Failure in school shouldn’t be shameful; it should be instructive. The lesson shouldn’t be “everyone moves forward together,” but “everyone earns their progress.” Kids who learn how to handle setbacks grow into adults who can handle disruption — and disruption is what defines the digital age.

For workforce programs, the focus shouldn’t just be coding or AI-specific skills. It should be learning how to learn — problem-solving, quantitative reasoning, and ethical decision-making under uncertainty. Teach people how to understand systems, not just operate them. Pair that with strong apprenticeship models and continuous learning incentives so people can reskill quickly as technology evolves.

If Canada wants a workforce ready for the AI era, it needs to build grit alongside skills. Teach math, yes. Teach computing, yes. But above all, teach people how to fail, learn, and try again — because that’s the only skill that never becomes obsolete.
column3:
If Canada truly wants to enhance AI literacy, it has to start in the classroom — not in government-funded ad campaigns or checkbox training programs. Real learning happens young. The younger the better. I have daughters who are eight and eleven, and I’m teaching them as much about AI as I can — and, frankly, AI is teaching them too. Kids learn fast, they experiment fearlessly, and they don’t carry the baggage that makes adults hesitant to try new tools.

Right now, there’s too much emphasis on AI’s “limitations” and “biases.” Those are temporary problems, not permanent truths. The technology is evolving daily. The biases are being engineered out because they’re not accurate, and accuracy always wins. Pretty soon, these concerns will sound as outdated as people worrying that the internet would ruin libraries.

Most workplace training programs miss the mark. They’re surface-level, outdated by the time they launch, and designed more to check boxes than build skills. Credentials are losing meaning in a world where tools change every few months, and public awareness campaigns are little more than expensive advertising buys. None of that builds literacy — it just burns money.

The real solution is to build AI literacy into core education, the same way we teach reading, writing, and math. Kids should be using AI tools early — not as a novelty, but as part of how they think, create, and problem-solve. Because here’s the truth: I can’t teach my mom how to use her cell phone, and I’m definitely not going to teach her how to use AI. If we want a truly AI-literate country, we need to start with the generation that’s still curious enough to learn.
column4:
Equitable access to AI literacy in Canada doesn’t need a complex rollout plan — it needs simplicity and focus. The only scalable distribution model is through schools and public libraries. That’s it. Those are the two places that reach everyone, regardless of region, income, or background. There’s no need for multi-level collaboration, advisory panels, or endless pilot programs. This technology is intuitive, accessible, and already available to anyone with an internet connection. Overcomplicating it would be an enormous waste of time and money.

AI isn’t hard to learn. The learning curve is short, and the tools are designed to teach you as you go. Anyone who opens ChatGPT and starts asking questions can figure it out within minutes. That’s the beauty of it — it’s self-teaching. The real challenge isn’t comprehension; it’s exposure. So the goal should be to put AI into the hands of students as early as possible and normalize it as part of how learning happens.

Teachers who teach anything beyond Grade 3 should be fluent in how to use AI — for planning lessons, grading work, and helping students explore ideas. If it’s not embedded by that stage, we’re already behind. The longer we wait to make AI part of basic education, the more we risk raising a generation that consumes AI products instead of creating them.

Equitable access to AI literacy doesn’t require more bureaucracy — it just requires access, curiosity, and early exposure. Put it in schools. Put it in libraries. Let people learn by doing. The rest will take care of itself.

column2:
The skills gap isn't about teaching everyone to code neural networks—it's about creating two parallel tracks where a small group goes deep on AI development while the broader workforce learns to work effectively alongside AI systems, which is a completely different skill set. For AI-focused careers, Canada needs intensive, practical programs that look more like apprenticeships than degrees: six-month bootcamps where you're building real models on real problems with industry mentors, followed by direct placement into companies that committed to hiring graduates, because the traditional CS degree pipeline is too slow and too theoretical when companies need people who can ship models next quarter.

For the broader workforce, the critical skill is "AI literacy" that's actually useful—not how transformers work, but how to critically evaluate AI outputs, when to override the system, how to prompt effectively, and how to identify when AI is confidently wrong. This should be embedded in every职业training program and community college curriculum, taught by people who actually use AI in their jobs, not academics theorizing about it. The biggest gap is middle management and supervisors who don't understand the tools their teams are using, so fund "manager AI bootcamps" that focus on what questions to ask, how to set performance metrics, and when AI is appropriate versus when you need human judgment.

Rapid adaptation means killing the model where you learn skills once and coast for 40 years—instead, create "learning accounts" that give every worker $2000/year for approved training they can use throughout their career without employer permission, because people stuck in dying industries won't retrain if it means quitting their job first.
column3:
Embed AI literacy into existing workplace training the way we did health and safety—mandatory but practical, focusing on "here's how the AI you actually use can fail" rather than abstract ethics lectures, with real examples of biased outputs and when to distrust confident predictions. For the public, fund libraries and community organizations to run hands-on sessions where people use AI tools themselves and see the limitations firsthand, because you can't explain hallucinations as effectively as letting someone watch ChatGPT confidently make up citations.

Estonia's approach works: they trained 5% of their population through free online courses with government backing, making it a civic competency like financial literacy, and they focused heavily on "when AI is wrong" scenarios rather than hype. The key is making it immediately relevant—show healthcare workers how to spot when a diagnostic AI might be biased by training data, teach loan officers what questions reveal if the credit model is discriminating, give teachers examples of AI-generated student work so they know what to look for. Skip the "how neural networks work" entirely and go straight to "here are the five ways AI fails that affect your job, and here's how to catch them."
column4:
Fund provinces and municipalities to deliver AI literacy through infrastructure people already trust and use—public libraries in rural areas, community centers in low-income neighborhoods, settlement services for newcomers—rather than creating new federal programs nobody knows about. The federal role is providing free, translated curriculum and train-the-trainer programs so local facilitators can adapt content for their communities, whether that's showing farmers how AI affects precision agriculture or teaching seniors how to spot AI-generated scams targeting them.

Industry should be required to contribute as part of doing business in Canada: tech companies deploying AI get tax benefits only if they fund community literacy programs in underserved regions, and major employers using AI tools must train their workforce including contract and part-time workers, not just salaried staff. The equity unlock is making it zero-barrier: free programs, available in multiple languages, offered at times that work for shift workers, with childcare and internet access provided, because a Tuesday afternoon workshop downtown excludes exactly the people who most need AI literacy. Partner with unions and Indigenous organizations to co-design and deliver programs for their members, since top-down government campaigns get ignored while peer-led training from trusted community voices actually changes behavior—the goal isn't perfect coverage, it's reaching the people most vulnerable to AI harms first, not last.

column3: As the CEO of North Vancouver City Library, serving the City of North Vancouver in BC, I am responding to highlight the essential role of public libraries in Canada's AI strategy. Enhancing AI literacy in Canada means equipping citizens to think critically about technology’s benefits, limitations, and biases. Public libraries, cornerstones of Canadian democracy, are uniquely positioned to lead this effort. At North Vancouver City Library (NVCL), digital learning and critical thinking are already embedded in practice: over 3,600 residents participated in technology programs in 2024, and initiatives such our digitization station, recording booth and initiatives like Take Home Tech, which distributed 400 tablets and smartphones to vulnerable residents during the earliest days of the COVID shutdown, demonstrate how we expand equitable access to digital tools. We are currently developing a micro-credentialing program in partnership with a local post-secondary institution to strengthen community digital skills, and we are rolling out workshops on misinformation and disinformation to directly support critical thinking and AI literacy. With 16,000 Canadians attending library tech programs each month, libraries provide trusted, judgment-free education for youth, seniors, newcomers, and other groups often excluded from emerging technology discussions. By investing in libraries as hubs for AI ethics dialogue, mis/disinformation awareness, deepfake detection and information verification, Canada can build a national AI literacy infrastructure that reflects and safeguards democratic values.
column4: As the CEO of North Vancouver City Library, serving the City of North Vancouver in BC, I am responding to highlight the essential role of public libraries in Canada's AI strategy. Ensuring equitable access to AI literacy requires infrastructure that reaches every Canadian no matter where they live. With over 3,300 public library locations nationwide, 30% of Canadians live within walking distance of a library branch, and millions more regularly access free, universal service online. Public libraries are open seven days a week, operate without cost barriers, and form a trusted, collaborative network that includes local service providers, schools, and social agencies. The North Vancouver City Library, as an example, partners with newcomer settlement services, housing supports, our local seniors centre and women's shelter, technology businesses in our community and a variety of community education providers in delivering technology training and digital access programs. Libraries can work with federal, provincial, and municipal partners - as well as industry - to develop multilingual, accessible AI literacy programs for all ages and build on the success of our digital literacy programs. By investing in public libraries as Canada’s coast-to-coast AI learning infrastructure, we can prevent a two-tier digital future where geography or income determine opportunity - and ensure that AI readiness reflects Canadian values of equity and inclusion.

And you will find that we are succeeding in serving community members of all ages, incomes and backgrounds, with Canadian libraries welcoming more than 100 million visits per year at over 3,300 locations (https://canurb.org/wp-content/uploads/CUI_Overdue_report_10.04.23.pdf). Canada can also support the skills needed for a modern, digital economy by implementing Universal Basic Income. Previous studies have shown it increases happiness, mental and physical health, and also, gives Canadian residents who might not otherwise have it the security and freedom to invest in their own education and become skilled workers to help Canada stay ahead in the modern digital economy.

(Search "AI psychosis", it's all there). Best practices should be to ALWAYS double check any work done with AI--NOT with AI-generated resources.

I don't know whether this is true of the Girl Guides, but the U.S. Girl Scouts have a Cybersecurity badge; you could look into that as a starting-point.

column2: The development and deployment of intelligent robotics requires interdisciplinary skills that bridge neuroscience, AI, and robotics rather than narrow technical specialization alone. Canada's workforce needs people who understand computational principles underlying intelligent systems, can deploy and maintain physical AI in real-world environments, and grasp how these systems interact safely with humans. This creates two distinct training imperatives. For AI-focused careers, we need programmes that explicitly teach the integration of neuroscience principles with AI and robotics engineering, moving beyond pure computer science to include sensorimotor control, human perception, and the computational principles of biological intelligence. These programmes should emphasize the meta-science of modeling: how to translate scientific questions into computational approaches, not just how specific tools work. For broader workforce readiness, we need training that prepares workers to collaborate effectively with intelligent robots in healthcare, construction, and other sectors. This includes understanding human-robot interaction, safety protocols, and system oversight. Canada best supports this development through coordinated programmes linking universities with industry partners, creating clear pathways from education to employment in neuro-AI and robotics. We should establish interdisciplinary degree programmes and certificates that explicitly combine these fields, fund industry placements where students work on real deployment challenges, and support rapid upskilling programmes for current workers in target sectors. The crucial insight is that successful adoption requires workforce development that matches our strategic focus on the convergence of neuroscience, AI, and physical robotics.

To harness its benefits while protecting Canadian values, advancing AI literacy is essential. Canadians must develop skills to critically evaluate AI-generated content, spot misinformation, and grasp ethical implications.

Public libraries are uniquely positioned to lead AI literacy efforts. As trusted, inclusive institutions, libraries excel at teaching information literacy and source verification—vital for identifying deepfakes and AI-generated misinformation. Librarians’ expertise in evaluating sources and detecting bias fosters critical thinking in accessible, judgment-free environments.

Public libraries serve as pillars of democracy and informed citizenship. Our commitment to privacy, accuracy, and community engagement ensures initiatives reflect Canadian values. Libraries support youth, seniors, newcomers, and vulnerable groups with targeted literacy programs, ensuring equitable access to AI education. By hosting AI ethics discussions and gathering community input on policy, we amplify diverse voices and foster social cohesion.

To enhance AI literacy, Canada should invest in library training programs that strengthen critical evaluation skills. Library-led awareness campaigns can inform citizens about AI’s capabilities, risks, and responsible use. Libraries’ infrastructure makes us ideal partners for delivering training as part of national and local initiatives.

Canada’s libraries can also draw on international best practices, collaborating with global peers to integrate effective strategies in information verification and ethical AI use, tailored to Canadian values of transparency and accountability.

By investing in public library readiness and a collaborative national infrastructure, Canada can ensure citizens have the skills and confidence to engage safely with AI. Library leadership is crucial in fostering critical evaluation, supporting community engagement, and upholding values that sustain Canada’s democracy.

Our sector’s collaborative network enables national reach while maintaining deep local connections. This infrastructure is critical for reaching youth, seniors, newcomers, and other vulnerable groups, ensuring no community is left behind.

Libraries’ platforms support online training and multilingual resources, making AI literacy accessible to Canadians wherever they live and whatever their language or background. As the only public institution working universally across generations, public libraries are best equipped to foster critical thinking about AI-generated content and promote informed confidence in navigating AI’s opportunities and risks.

To truly advance equitable access, Canada should invest in the public library network and collaborate with all levels of government, industry, and the private sector. Federal support is vital to prevent a two-tiered system where a person’s postal code determines their readiness to engage safely and responsibly with AI. By leveraging library partnerships and infrastructure, we can deliver comprehensive, inclusive AI literacy programming—including online training, targeted engagement, and multilingual resources—safeguarding Canadian values and strengthening the social fabric of our communities.

column2:
We believe Canada needs two categories of skills to thrive in the modern digital economy: AI-and-data fluency and everyday digital competence.

1. Core Digital and Human Skills
- Everyday digital skills include working with modern productivity tools, managing and interpreting data (e.g., spreadsheets, dashboards), writing clear AI prompts, basic process automation, and collaborating effectively in digital environments.
- Human and adaptive skills such as problem solving, communication, critical thinking, design thinking, and change leadership, are essential complements. These enable workers to adapt as technology and roles evolve.

2. Advanced AI and Data Skills
- For AI-focused careers, Canada needs stronger pathways in machine learning, data engineering, cybersecurity, cloud infrastructure, and responsible AI governance.
- Promote interdisciplinary programs that combine technical expertise with ethics, health sciences, and social impact, ensuring AI is both powerful and people-centred.

3. Building and Deploying Skills at Scale
- Fund short, stackable, job-linked credentials delivered through colleges, universities, and industry consortia.
- Expand co-ops, apprenticeships, and experiential placements in hospitals, utilities, manufacturing, government, and startups, so learners build applied experience in real workplaces.
- Support mid-career upskilling and reskilling through tax credits, wage subsidies, and portable training accounts that follow workers across employers.
- Prioritize training in the public sector and SMEs, providing national, bilingual courseware and ensuring mutual recognition of micro-credentials across provinces to enable mobility.
column3:
Canada should make AI literacy practical, plain-language, and hands-on, rooted in everyday understanding rather than abstract theory. Building trust and competence means equipping workers and citizens to use AI confidently, safely, and critically.

1. Build Practical AI Literacy in the Workplace
Offer short, stackable training modules explaining what AI can and cannot do, common biases, privacy and security basics, and safe-use practices like data redaction, model cards, and human oversight. Create role-specific modules for clinicians, educators, public servants, and small-business owners with practical checklists and templates. Recognize AI micro-credentials across provinces to make skills transferable. Require public-sector AI vendors to include staff training in implementation contracts.

2. Promote Public Understanding and Confidence
Use schools, libraries, and community centres for outreach through videos, workshops, and demos showing how AI works, its limits, and biases. Teach citizens to spot deepfakes and misinformation, and clearly explain how to raise concerns or opt out. Launch a national “AI Help” portal consolidating explainers, rights, and real-world examples.

3. Adopt Global Best Practices
Use regulatory sandboxes for safe experimentation, trust labels or certifications for reviewed systems, and public evaluation summaries written for non-experts. Partner with academia, industry, and media to promote balanced, myth-free dialogue about AI.

4. Measure and Report Progress
Track indicators such as: percentage of workers completing AI literacy programs, share of the public identifying AI-generated content, uptake of micro-credentials, and reductions in AI misuse, data breaches, or related harms.
column4:
Canada can ensure equitable AI literacy by meeting people where they are, removing cost and access barriers, and building programs that reflect regional, linguistic, and cultural realities.

1. Build Universal and Inclusive Access Points
- Use public schools, colleges, libraries, and community centres as universal hubs for AI learning.
- Fund free or low-cost courses in both official languages and locally relevant Indigenous languages, ensuring inclusion across all communities.
- Develop offline and low-bandwidth learning options such as print guides, radio content, and downloadable mobile modules for rural, remote, and northern regions with limited internet access.
- Provide device and connectivity supports (e.g., loaner laptops, subsidized broadband) so participation isn’t limited by income or geography.

2. Coordinate Across Governments and Sectors
- Provinces and territories should lead on curriculum standards and recognize AI micro-credentials across jurisdictions.
- The federal government should fund content development, maintain a national “AI Help” portal, and manage national literacy benchmarks and assessments.
- Industry partners should co-design job-aligned learning modules, donate compute resources and devices, and include staff training commitments in public-sector AI contracts.

3. Empower Trusted Local Delivery
- Deliver training through schools, libraries, Indigenous organizations, settlement agencies, and community groups that people already trust.
- Pair AI literacy efforts with community ambassadors or peer trainers who can contextualize material for local needs and help overcome skepticism or language barriers.
4. Measure and Close Gaps Transparently
- Track who enrolls, completes, and applies AI literacy skills at work or in daily life, and publish disaggregated results by region, gender, age, and socioeconomic group.

column2:
A modern, digital economy demands a workforce that is both technically fluent and adaptable, capable of applying AI responsibly across every sector. Canada’s success will depend on developing deep AI expertise while equipping all workers with digital literacy, problem-solving, and lifelong learning skills.

1. Core Competencies for the AI Era.
Canada needs talent in machine learning, data science, software engineering, cybersecurity, human-computer interaction, and AI ethics. Equally critical are soft skills—creativity, critical thinking, and interdisciplinary collaboration—that enable responsible application of technology.

2. Broader Workforce Readiness.
AI will reshape roles in health, manufacturing, logistics, education, and public service. Every worker should have access to digital-upskilling programs that teach how to use, interpret, and govern AI tools safely and ethically.

3. Education and Training Pathways.
Integrate AI literacy and computational thinking into K–12 curricula, expand college and university micro-credentials, and strengthen partnerships with industry for hands-on training. National initiatives such as CIFAR, the Pan-Canadian AI Institutes, and the AI Strategy Task Force should coordinate new learning models that bridge research and practice.

4. Reskilling and Inclusion.
Establish a National AI Skills Fund to support mid-career reskilling and ensure equitable access for under-represented groups, including Indigenous and rural communities.

5. Workforce Deployment and Adaptation.
Create AI Talent Exchange Programs linking academia, startups, and public institutions to accelerate deployment of expertise where it’s most needed.

By investing in lifelong learning, equitable access, and adaptable skills, Canada can build a workforce ready to harness AI for innovation, productivity, and inclusive prosperity.
column3:
1. A Lifelong, Whole-of-Society Learning Model.
AI and digital literacy should be treated like reading or math—essential for all ages. Begin in early education by teaching curiosity, logic, and ethical awareness around technology. Continue through post-secondary programs, adult learning, and senior education, making AI literacy accessible through schools, community centers, workplaces, and online platforms.

2. Empower the Current Workforce.
The majority of Canadians already in the workforce—especially those in medium- and lower-income jobs—must be equipped to adapt. Provide universal access to short, flexible training that helps every worker use AI tools safely, productively, and ethically. Governments and employers should co-fund reskilling grants, paid learning leave, and local digital learning hubs.

3. Inclusion Across Communities.
Ensure equal participation from Indigenous Peoples, newcomers, rural regions, seniors, youth, and people with disabilities. Connectivity, equipment, and culturally appropriate training are prerequisites for digital inclusion.

4. Building Advanced and Ethical Expertise.
Continue developing world-class talent in AI research, data science, cybersecurity, and safety, while embedding ethics and human-centered design in every technical curriculum.

5. National Leadership and Coordination.
Create a National AI Skills & Inclusion Framework that unites education ministries, industry, labour, and community organizations under one vision—AI skills for every Canadian.

When every Canadian—of every age, background, and skill level—can understand and harness AI, adoption becomes national, equitable, and unstoppable.
column4:
For Canada to thrive in the AI era, AI literacy must be a universal right, not a privilege. Equitable access means every person—regardless of geography, income, age, or background—has the opportunity to understand and benefit from AI safely and confidently.

1. National Coordination with Local Reach.
Establish a Pan-Canadian AI Literacy Framework co-led by federal, provincial, territorial, and Indigenous governments to align funding, curriculum, and delivery. Local school boards, colleges, and community organizations should adapt programs to reflect regional realities and cultural perspectives.

2. Inclusive Education at Every Level.
Integrate AI and digital literacy into K-12 curricula, expand vocational and college programs focused on applied AI skills, and provide free or low-cost adult learning and senior-friendly training through community centres and libraries. AI understanding should grow across generations, not stop at formal schooling.

3. Industry and Private-Sector Partnerships.
Businesses should fund community learning hubs, offer on-the-job training, and collaborate with the Canadian AI Safety Institute (CAISI) and CIFAR to create bilingual, open-access learning materials. Private-sector leadership can make AI literacy part of workplace safety, ethics, and innovation culture.

4. Bridging the Digital Divide.
Ensure universal broadband, affordable devices, and accessible online tools so rural, remote, and low-income communities can fully participate. Accessibility must also include accommodations for Canadians with disabilities and linguistic diversity.

5. Building Informed Confidence.
Public awareness campaigns should explain how AI works, how data is used, and where to seek help or challenge misuse. Transparency builds trust; knowledge builds empowerment.

When AI literacy reaches every region, income group, and community, Canada’s innovation economy becomes not only stronger—but truly inclusive.

column3: Public libraries are well positioned because we already teach Canadians information literacy, such as verifying the sources, identifying deepfakes.  By teaching Canadians information and digital literacy skills, public libraries are an existing training platform for teaching AI literacy.  Canadians already recognize public libraries as trusted source of training that can help then think critically about AI generated content.  Public libraries are in almost every community, and they are already community hubs that individuals and small businesses turn to.  Public libraries work with all demographics and people from all walks of life.  In my library, Strathcona County library, we serve urban residents from our library, but we also take technology training out to rural residents on our Bookmobile and rural community halls.  Thousands of Canadians attend technology training every day at public libraries, and libraries are working tirelessly to bridge the current digital divide.  We don't want that divide to increase, so it is crucial that AI literacy and training is available to all Canadians, delivered by librarians they already trust. Public libraries provide infrastructure in a social space where Canadians can gather and debate and diverse voices could discuss the development of AI to reflect the Canadian values..  Public libraries are a judgement-free space where Canadians can hold discussions about AI ethics.  Librarians are information literacy experts and this is exactly the background needed when helping all Canadians understand biases and limitations.  Federal investment in public libraries would create a national AI literacy infrastructure across Canada in institutions that are trusted by Canadians to provide unbiased information about technologies.
column4: There are 3,300 public libraries across Canada in urban, suburban, rural and remote areas (no geographic barriers) and are open seven days a week. Public libraries have a national reach and form a strong collaborative network across Canada by connecting with hundreds of service providers and academic institutions.  At Strathcona County Library, we serve 70,000 urban and 30,000 rural residents by taking technology training out on our bookmobile and to rural community halls.  Public libraries focus on reducing social barriers by working with community agencies who work with newcomers (we work with the Edmonton Immigrant Settlement Agency that is federally funded).  We work with youth, seniors, newcomers, people with insecure housing, and other vulnerable populations.  We welcome everyone, provide free services (no financial barriers) and strive for universal access. We provide multilingual services and are already community hubs that individuals and small businesses turn to.    Thousands of Canadians attend technology training every day at public libraries, and we work to bridge the current digital divide, so it is crucial that AI literacy and training is available to all Canadians.  Federal investment in public libraries would help prevent two-tiers of AI readiness by building on an existing platform for delivering in person and online training.  Public libraires are a judgement-free space where Canadians from all segments of society can can hold discussions about AI ethics, have facilitated community discussions, and learn essential AI skills. Librarians are information literacy experts and this is exactly what is needed when helping Canadians of all ages understand biases and limitations.  Federal investment in public libraries would create a national AI literacy infrastructure across Canada in institutions that are trusted by all Canadians to provide unbiased information about technologies.

column2:
Canada’s competitiveness in an AI-driven world depends on cultivating both specialized technical talent and broad digital fluency across the workforce. Success requires lifelong learning systems that are agile, inclusive, and industry-connected.

1. Core skill domains.

AI and data literacy: understanding how AI works, its limits, and ethical use across all occupations.

Technical expertise: data science, machine learning, cybersecurity, cloud computing, and human-computer interaction.

Human-centered capabilities: critical thinking, creativity, communication, collaboration, and adaptability—skills that complement automation.

Responsible innovation: ethics, privacy, and sustainability integrated into design and decision-making.

2. Education and training systems.

Embed AI and digital literacy across K–12, colleges, and universities, emphasizing applied, interdisciplinary learning.

Fund micro-credentials and short upskilling programs co-designed with industry for rapid reskilling in high-demand fields.

Support work-integrated learning—apprenticeships, residencies, and co-ops—that link students and mid-career workers to emerging sectors.

3. Inclusion and access.

Expand programs for underrepresented groups—women, newcomers, Indigenous peoples, and rural learners—through digital infrastructure, mentorship, and financial supports.

Invest in community colleges and adult learning hubs as local engines of re-skilling.

4. National coordination and incentives.

Establish a Digital Skills Council linking government, employers, labour, and educators to forecast demand and align programs.

Offer tax credits or wage subsidies for employers who invest in employee upskilling using certified training providers.

Outcome: a resilient, future-ready workforce where every Canadian can participate confidently and ethically in the digital and AI economy.
column3:
To ensure Canadians use and govern AI responsibly, literacy efforts must reach beyond specialists to include workers, educators, and the public. The goal is informed confidence—understanding both AI’s potential and its limitations.

1. National AI literacy strategy.
Develop a coordinated National AI Literacy Framework led by ISED, in partnership with provinces, colleges, and libraries, to define learning outcomes across education levels and sectors—covering what AI is, how it works, where it fails, and how bias and data quality affect outcomes.

2. Education and workforce programs.

Embed AI and data literacy into K–12 curricula, emphasizing ethics, critical thinking, and responsible use.

Support college micro-credentials and industry certifications for AI-adjacent roles (healthcare, finance, public service), focusing on bias recognition, explainability, and data stewardship.

Expand workplace training grants so employers can upskill teams on using AI safely and transparently.

3. Public engagement and awareness.

Launch a national “AI in Everyday Life” campaign with plain-language explainers, interactive exhibits, and public dialogues highlighting both opportunities and risks.

Partner with public libraries, community centres, and media to host accessible workshops on AI’s societal impacts.

4. Leverage international best practices.
Adapt proven models such as Finland’s Elements of AI course and Singapore’s AI for Everyone/AI for Industry programs, offering bilingual, open-access versions tailored to Canada’s diverse communities.

5. Inclusion and trust.
Co-develop materials with Indigenous and equity-seeking groups, ensuring culturally relevant approaches that address algorithmic bias and data sovereignty.

Outcome: a population that understands, questions, and shapes AI—equipped to use it responsibly and to hold it accountable.
column4:
To make AI literacy a public good—not a privilege—Canada must combine national coordination with local delivery, ensuring every region and demographic can participate in the digital economy.

1. A coordinated national approach.
Establish a Pan-Canadian AI Literacy Partnership led by ISED and provinces/territories, aligning curricula, funding, and evaluation. This framework should guarantee access to foundational AI and digital skills training for learners of all ages and backgrounds.

2. Local access and delivery.
Empower community colleges, libraries, Indigenous learning centres, and settlement agencies to serve as regional AI learning hubs. Provide sustained federal grants for equipment, connectivity, and instructor training so rural and remote communities can participate equally.

3. Inclusive program design.
Co-create materials with Indigenous, newcomer, disability, and low-income communities, ensuring cultural and linguistic relevance. Embed examples that reflect local realities—such as AI in agriculture, health, or small business—to make learning practical and trusted.

4. Industry and private-sector collaboration.
Encourage companies to provide free or low-cost learning platforms, micro-credentials, and mentorships, while contributing to community training funds. Corporate participation should be tied to clear equity outcomes, not only workforce pipelines.

5. Education system integration.
Integrate AI literacy into K–12, adult education, and vocational programs, emphasizing critical thinking, ethics, and data responsibility alongside technical skills.

6. Incentives and evaluation.
Offer training tax credits, digital literacy stipends, and national reporting on regional participation to track progress and close gaps.

Outcome: equitable, inclusive AI literacy that empowers every Canadian—urban or rural, newcomer or Indigenous—to engage confidently and critically in an AI-enabled society.

column2:
Canada’s digital economy needs a workforce fluent in both technology and creativity — people who can understand AI systems, apply them ethically, and adapt quickly as tools evolve. The most valuable skills now are AI literacy, data analysis, digital design, cybersecurity, entrepreneurship, and critical thinking.

To build this capacity, Canada should expand micro-credential programs, retraining initiatives, and apprenticeship models that combine technical learning with real-world application.

Government, industry, and education systems must collaborate to create national AI and digital-skills hubs — accessible to students, workers, and small businesses alike.

By treating lifelong digital upskilling as economic infrastructure, Canada ensures every sector can adapt, innovate, and thrive in an AI-driven world.
column3:
Enhancing AI literacy in Canada means helping people understand not just how to use AI — but how to question it. True literacy includes awareness of AI’s limits, biases, and data dependencies.

Canada should launch a national AI literacy strategy that blends education, industry training, and public awareness. This includes:
	•	Workplace upskilling programs for employees and entrepreneurs using AI tools.
	•	Micro-credentials that teach bias detection, data ethics, and responsible deployment.
	•	Public campaigns and online learning platforms explaining AI’s real capabilities and risks in plain language.
	•	Partnerships with creators, SMEs, and educators to share accessible, real-world examples.

By making AI literacy universal — not elitist — Canada empowers citizens and businesses to use AI confidently, responsibly, and competitively.
column4:
Equitable access to AI literacy means every Canadian — regardless of region, income, or background — can understand and benefit from AI. To achieve this, Canada must treat digital education as a public right, not a privilege.

Government should partner with provinces, schools, libraries, and local innovation hubs to deliver free or low-cost AI literacy programs, both online and in-person. Rural and remote areas need reliable internet, devices, and community training spaces to close the digital gap.

Industry can help by funding AI mentorships, internships, and micro-credential scholarships for underrepresented groups, ensuring inclusion in the future workforce.

By combining public infrastructure, local delivery, and private-sector support, Canada can make AI understanding — and opportunity — accessible to everyone.

column2:
Canada needs to focus on two categories of skills. The first is AI and digital fluency — data analysis, coding, cybersecurity, cloud management, and human-AI collaboration. The second is adaptive and strategic skills such as critical thinking, communication, design, and problem solving that help workers use technology effectively rather than compete with it.

To build these skills at scale, governments, universities, and employers should align on a national digital skills framework linked to real industry needs. This means updating college and apprenticeship programs, expanding short micro-credential courses, and embedding hands-on AI and data projects in every field from health to trades.

Create incentives for lifelong learning through tax credits, training accounts, and partnerships between employers and post-secondary institutions that certify skills quickly.

The public sector should model the change by retraining its own workforce and opening AI fellowships for civil servants.

Finally, ensure inclusion by funding digital access and training in rural, Indigenous, and underrepresented communities so the benefits of AI extend to the entire workforce.
column3:
Launch a national AI literacy initiative that combines public awareness, education, and workforce training. Use plain language campaigns to explain what AI can and cannot do, with real examples of both benefits and failures.

Integrate AI ethics and bias education into school and university curricula across disciplines, not just computer science. Include critical thinking, data awareness, and hands-on exposure to model limitations.

Support workplace upskilling through micro-credentials and modular training programs developed jointly by industry, post-secondary institutions, and government. Prioritize accessibility for small and medium enterprises that lack in-house training capacity.

Fund community-based AI education programs in libraries, employment centers, and Indigenous and rural communities, using trusted local organizations to bridge digital literacy gaps.

Encourage media and public broadcasters to produce explanatory content on AI impacts and biases to normalize informed discussion.

Adopt international best practices such as the UK’s “AI Standards Hub” and Finland’s “Elements of AI” open course to provide free, accredited training options for Canadians.

Require public sector employees involved in AI procurement or use to complete foundational AI literacy and ethics training to model responsible adoption.
column4:
Develop a national AI literacy strategy co-led by federal, provincial, and territorial governments that ensures consistent access to training, resources, and funding across all regions.

Partner with schools, libraries, and community colleges to deliver free or low-cost AI literacy programs tailored to local needs, including rural, northern, and Indigenous communities.

Provide grants for community-based organizations and nonprofits to run workshops in multiple languages and formats, ensuring accessibility for newcomers, seniors, and people with disabilities.

Involve industry through a national “AI for All” coalition where companies contribute funding, mentorship, and learning materials, particularly for small and medium enterprises that lack training capacity.

Integrate AI and digital literacy into K–12 education to build early familiarity with responsible technology use and awareness of bias, privacy, and safety.

Offer micro-credentials and short online programs through post-secondary institutions, supported by government training incentives and employer partnerships to encourage participation.

Expand internet access and device subsidies in underserved regions to remove the most basic barrier to AI literacy and participation in the digital economy.

Track participation and outcomes by region and demographic group, ensuring that women, Indigenous peoples, visible minorities, and low-income Canadians benefit proportionately.

column3:
-	Public libraries are trusted guides in information literacy. Librarians specialize in helping people assess accuracy, verify sources, and think critically—skills directly applicable to identifying deepfakes, algorithmic bias, and other forms of AI-generated misinformation that pose risks to individuals, organizations, and democratic systems.
•	AI literacy must be inclusive and multigenerational. Canadians of all ages need the ability to navigate and assess AI-generated content. Public libraries are uniquely positioned to deliver learning opportunities and instruction across generations—reaching youth, seniors, newcomers, and vulnerable populations in both urban and rural communities.
•	Libraries already deliver AI-relevant programming at scale. Over 16,000 people attend technology programs at public libraries every month. These programs build foundational digital skills and can be expanded to include AI content literacy, bias awareness, and ethical use.
•	Libraries offer welcoming spaces for dialogue and exploration. As community hubs, libraries provide infrastructure for gathering, ideating, and debating. They are ideal venues for hosting AI ethics discussions, facilitating community input on AI policy, and amplifying diverse voices in shaping Canada’s AI future.
•	Libraries support AI-enabled social innovation. As hubs for the curious and creative, libraries foster exploration and experimentation—essential for engaging citizens in the possibilities and limitations of AI technologies.
•	Libraries are ready to scale AI literacy nationally. With existing platforms for online training, deep community networks, and a trusted reputation, libraries are well-positioned to deliver public awareness campaigns, workplace instruction, and targeted engagements aligned with international best practices.
•	Investing in library readiness builds national AI literacy infrastructure. Supporting libraries with funding, training, and policy alignment ensures that AI education is acce
column4:
Public libraries are Canada’s most accessible and inclusive learning infrastructure. With over 3,300 locations in urban, suburban, rural, and remote communities, public libraries offer coast-to-coast coverage and a collaborative network that delivers national reach while staying deeply rooted in local contexts.
•	Libraries are essential community hubs—open seven days a week and free to everyone. They eliminate financial, geographic, and social barriers to access, making them uniquely positioned to deliver AI literacy to all Canadians, regardless of age, background, or postal code.
•	Libraries work hand-in-hand with local service providers to reach those most at risk of being left behind. From newcomer settlement agencies and housing support organizations to youth programs and social services, libraries are embedded in the social fabric of their communities.
o	For example, EPL partners with agencies serving newcomers, housing-insecure individuals, and other vulnerable populations to extend its reach and impact.
•	Libraries already deliver digital and AI-relevant training at scale. With existing platforms for online learning and in-person programming, libraries are ready to expand AI literacy initiatives that teach Canadians how to navigate, evaluate, and think critically about AI-generated content.
•	Equity in AI literacy requires infrastructure that reaches every community. Libraries provide that infrastructure—serving 30% of Canadians within walking distance and offering multilingual, multigenerational programming that reflects the diversity of Canada.
•	Federal investment in public libraries prevents a two-tier system where AI readiness depends on geography or income. By supporting library-led initiatives, Canada can ensure that AI literacy is not a privilege, but a public good—available to all, everywhere.
•	Libraries are trusted partners in fostering informed confidence. No other public institution works as universally across generations. Libraries can help!

column2: Canada should not be focusing its energy and resources on AI -- it is a harmful science that is bad for the environment and society. Much of current AI usage is nothing but a drain on resources, and is feeding harmful misinformation to millions of individuals around the world. Generative AI and LLM in particular are extremely harmful -- it is completely unethical, as all the information it "provides" is stolen and often fabricated. Far too many companies and individuals, as well as governments, are overly trusting of an unreliable system that can hallucinate information as it pleases and can steal other individuals' intellectual property without consequence. If you are to remain ethically grounded, we must get OUT of the AI race. It is a short term grab for money and will not be in any way beneficial in the long term. STRICT limits must be placed on the usage of AI, especially in business and academics, and very clear warnings should be placed on any publicly available AI programs regarding the unreliability and frequent inaccuracies of the AI. The common use of AI is dangerous, and must be treated as such.
