column2:
Core digital fluency: data literacy, AI-assisted productivity, cybersecurity hygiene, cloud tools, and prompt engineering basics.

Technical tracks: machine learning, data engineering, MLOps, model evaluation/assurance, human–computer interaction, and privacy/security-by-design.

Business skills: AI product management, value modeling, change management, procurement for digital systems, and responsible-innovation governance.

Human strengths: critical thinking, systems thinking, collaboration, communication, design, and ethics.

Support mechanisms: national micro-credential frameworks; stackable college/university certificates; paid apprenticeships and co-ops; rapid reskilling via Employment Insurance–linked training; portable skills passports recognized by employers; and subsidized compute/lab access for hands-on learning.
column3:
Workplace programs: short, role-based modules (frontline, manager, exec) on what AI can/can’t do, bias and failure modes, and safe daily use.

Credentials: national “AI Foundations” micro-credential plus advanced badges (evaluation, red-teaming, privacy, copyright) with renewal requirements.

Public awareness: bilingual campaigns with plain-language explainers, demos of common pitfalls, and guidance on spotting synthetic media.

Best practices adoption: model cards and decision factsheets embedded in tools; hands-on labs that compare human vs. AI performance; sandboxed environments to practice safe use.

Sector playbooks: regulated templates for health, finance, justice, and public services covering risk tiers, audit checklists, and escalation paths.
column4:
Access infrastructure: universal library- and campus-based AI labs with free baseline model/compute access; broadband upgrades for underserved communities.

Local delivery: fund colleges, adult-ed centers, Indigenous institutes, settlement agencies, and public libraries to run recurring AI basics workshops and office hours.

Inclusive design: bilingual and accessible materials; culturally relevant examples; childcare and stipends for learners; flexible schedules and hybrid formats.

Targeted programs: youth (K–12 curricula with teacher PD), newcomers (credential-bridging + language-integrated AI courses), rural and remote workers (mobile training units), and persons with disabilities (assistive-AI training).

Partnerships: cost-shared programs with provinces/territories; industry-provided courseware and mentors; union-led upskilling; and outcome-based funding tied to completion, job placement, and wage gains.

column3:
Public libraries excel at teaching information literacy and source verification—skills that are directly transferable to identifying deepfakes and other forms of AI-generated misinformation that pose risks to individuals, organizations, and democratic systems. As AI-generated content becomes more prevalent, Canadians of all ages need the skills to navigate and critically assess what they encounter online.

No other public institution works as broadly and inclusively across generations as public libraries. They are uniquely positioned to help Canadians—from youth and seniors to newcomers and other vulnerable populations—develop the critical thinking and digital literacy skills required to safeguard Canadian values and the social fabric that strengthens our communities. Each month, more than 16,000 people participate in technology programs at public libraries, demonstrating the sector’s deep community reach and capacity to deliver meaningful, accessible training.

Beyond education, libraries provide welcoming social spaces and the infrastructure for gathering, ideating, and debating. As cornerstones of Canada’s democracy, they are ideal venues for hosting discussions on AI ethics, facilitating community input on AI policy development, and ensuring diverse voices help shape the future of AI use in Canada. Public libraries are hubs for the inquisitive, the curious, and the creative—communities that drive AI-enabled social innovation. With existing online platforms and digital delivery systems, libraries are ready to expand their role in providing equitable access to AI literacy and engagement opportunities nationwide.
column4:
Canada’s public library sector encompasses more than 3,300 locations across urban, suburban, rural, and remote communities. This extensive, collaborative network provides national reach while maintaining a local, community-focused presence. Public libraries are essential hubs in every neighbourhood, offering free, seven-day-a-week access to information, learning, and connection. They serve as trusted spaces where all Canadians—regardless of background, income, or location—can participate in opportunities for growth and innovation.

Public libraries are deeply embedded in their communities, partnering with hundreds of local service providers and key institutions to expand their reach and impact. Many libraries collaborate with agencies supporting newcomers, people experiencing housing insecurity, and other vulnerable populations, ensuring that equitable access to resources and training extends to those who need it most. With existing online platforms, libraries can also deliver digital and AI literacy programs virtually, reaching Canadians wherever they live.

As AI technologies become increasingly integrated into daily life, Canadians of all ages require skills to navigate and critically assess AI-generated content. No other public institution works as significantly and universally across generations as public libraries. They are uniquely positioned to support the government in engaging youth, seniors, newcomers, and marginalized groups to strengthen Canadian values and maintain the social fabric that underpins our communities.

Equity requires infrastructure that reaches every community. Public libraries provide precisely that—more than 3,300 locations offering coast-to-coast access, including in rural and remote areas. With 30% of Canadians living within walking distance of a library, they eliminate financial, geographic, and social barriers to participation. column2: A modern digital economy demands both technical and adaptive skills, and Canada must ensure that all workers—including those displaced from traditional sectors like auto and steel due to automation or tariffs—are supported in this transition. Core digital skills such as data literacy, AI fluency, and cybersecurity awareness are essential, but so are transferable skills like problem-solving, ethical reasoning, and collaboration. Canada should invest in inclusive, accessible training programs that serve both AI-focused careers and broader workforce readiness. This includes reskilling initiatives tailored to displaced workers, offering micro-credentials, vocational retraining, and pathways into emerging sectors like advanced manufacturing, clean tech, and logistics. Public-private partnerships can help align training with real industry needs, while regional programs should ensure support reaches communities most affected by economic shifts. By embedding digital skills into lifelong learning and workforce development strategies, Canada can empower all workers—not just tech specialists—to thrive in the AI-driven economy.
column3: Enhancing AI literacy in Canada requires a national effort to build public understanding of both the capabilities and limitations of AI, including its potential for bias, misinformation, and misuse. Workplace training programs and micro-credentials should be expanded to help employees across sectors—from tech to trades—understand how AI affects their roles and how to use it responsibly. Public awareness campaigns, delivered through schools, libraries, media, and community organizations, can demystify AI and promote informed engagement. These efforts must be inclusive, culturally sensitive, and accessible to all demographics, including displaced workers from sectors like auto and steel. Canada can also adopt international best practices, such as Finland’s “Elements of AI” course or Singapore’s national AI literacy initiatives, which combine online education with real-world examples. By embedding AI literacy into lifelong learning and civic education, and by promoting transparency in how AI systems are used, Canada can foster a more informed, confident, and critically engaged public.
column4: To ensure equitable access to AI literacy across Canada’s regions, demographics, and socioeconomic groups, a coordinated national strategy is needed that combines federal leadership with local delivery. Collaboration with provincial, territorial, and Indigenous governments can help tailor programs to community needs, while municipalities and school boards can embed AI literacy into education and public services. Industry and the private sector should contribute by funding inclusive training programs, offering workplace upskilling, and supporting outreach in underserved areas. Educational strategies must include free or low-cost micro-credentials, community-based workshops, and multilingual resources that reflect Canada’s diversity. Special attention should be given to displaced workers—such as those from auto and steel sectors—who need accessible pathways into digital roles. Public libraries, colleges, and employment centers can serve as trusted hubs for AI literacy. By combining inclusive design, local partnerships, and sustained investment, Canada can ensure that everyone—not just tech professionals—has the opportunity to understand and engage with AI responsibly.

column2:
Canada’s digital future depends on a workforce that is technically skilled, ethically grounded, and inclusive. To remain competitive, Canada must prepare workers for both AI and robotics—two fields that will define the next generation of innovation, productivity, and sustainable growth.
1) Core digital, AI, and robotics skills
Build AI and robotics literacy across all sectors through national education and training programs that combine technical, ethical, and human-centered learning.


Prioritize data fluency, automation, machine learning, and robotics repair and maintenance as essential future of work competencies.


Create applied robotics training centers focused on building, maintaining, and repairing robots—positioning Canada as a global hub for robotics manufacturing and servicing.


2) Inclusive pathways for lifelong learning
Launch a National Inclusive Digital and Robotics Skills Strategy offering training, mentorship, and funding for underrepresented communities—including 2SLGBTQ+, Indigenous, racialized, and disabled workers.


Expand micro-credentials and modular training in AI safety, robotics engineering, and ethical technology design to ensure rapid adaptation to emerging tools.


Partner with organizations like QueerTech to provide mentorship and career transition support for diverse professionals entering the digital and robotics sectors.


3) Empower educators and employers
Support educators and employers in embedding ethics, inclusion, and accessibility into digital transformation and workforce programs.


Encourage continuous learning through industry-academia-community partnerships, connecting training directly to evolving market needs.


4) Build accessible innovation ecosystems
Invest in community-based AI and robotics labs that give small businesses, nonprofits, and local innovators hands-on experience with emerging technologies.
column3:
Building AI literacy is essential to ensuring Canadians can understand, question, and responsibly use the technologies shaping their lives. True AI literacy goes beyond technical training—it must include awareness of bias, accessibility, and social impact. Canada can lead globally by pairing digital education with inclusive engagement that reflects the diversity of its people.
1) Build inclusive, lifelong learning programs
Launch a National AI and Digital Literacy Strategy to integrate AI awareness, ethics, and data skills across education, workplaces, and public institutions.


Partner with organizations like QueerTech to deliver peer-led learning programs, mentorship, and workshops focused on ethical AI use, bias recognition, and accessibility.


Expand micro-credentials and short courses in responsible AI, equity-centered design, and algorithmic accountability for both tech and non-tech professionals.


2) Embed AI literacy in education and training
Integrate AI fundamentals and ethical awareness into K–12, vocational, and postsecondary curricula, ensuring students understand both the opportunities and risks of AI.


Develop workplace certification programs to help employees adapt to automation, improve productivity, and recognize AI’s limitations and biases.


3) Increase public awareness and engagement
Launch a public awareness campaign on AI safety, bias, and responsible innovation using inclusive and accessible formats, including multilingual and community-based content.


Establish AI literacy hubs—both physical and online—where Canadians can access hands-on demonstrations, training, and ethical use guides.


4) Learn from international best practices
Adapt global models like Finland’s Elements of AI program and Singapore’s AI for Everyone initiative, embedding Canada’s unique focus on inclusion, equity, and human rights.
column4:
Equitable access to AI literacy is critical to ensuring every Canadian—not just those in major cities or privileged sectors—can participate in and benefit from the digital economy. Building on QueerTech’s mission to create inclusive pathways into technology, Canada must embed equity, accessibility, and representation into every level of AI education and training.
1) Develop a National Inclusive AI Literacy Framework
Create a federally coordinated framework that aligns provinces, territories, municipalities, and Indigenous governments on AI education, ensuring consistent standards and equitable delivery.


Prioritize rural, remote, and underserved communities through regional AI learning hubs offering hybrid and multilingual access.


Ensure all publicly funded programs include universal accessibility standards (WCAG 2.2+, plain language, captioning, assistive tech compatibility).


2) Partner with community and equity organizations
Collaborate with organizations like QueerTech to co-design inclusive AI literacy programs that reflect diverse lived experiences, including 2SLGBTQ+, Indigenous, racialized, newcomer, and disability communities.


Fund community-based mentorship and peer-learning networks to make AI education approachable and relevant.


3) Leverage the role of industry and the private sector
Encourage private companies to invest in equitable digital upskilling and contribute learning resources to open-access national platforms.


Establish public–private partnerships that fund scholarships, internships, and micro-credential programs for underrepresented groups.


4) Integrate literacy into lifelong learning
Embed AI awareness, bias recognition, and ethics into school curricula, workplace training, and reskilling programs across all sectors.


Develop inclusive national campaigns to raise public understanding of AI, emphasizing human rights, safety, and digital empowerment.

Fund Regional AI Hubs
Inject substantial funding into underdeveloped regions to build AI training centers, research facilities, and tech infrastructure. Stop concentrating everything in Toronto and Vancouver—spread opportunity across Canada.
Link Immigration to Regional AI Development
Tie AI talent immigration to underserved regions. Offer incentives for AI professionals and companies to establish in areas needing economic growth. Create pathways that require initial years in these regions, building local capacity and jobs.
Industry-Region Partnerships
Require companies receiving federal AI funding to establish operations outside major metros. Match private sector investment dollar-for-dollar in underdeveloped areas. Make regional presence a condition of government support.
Local AI Literacy Programs
Partner with provincial and municipal governments to deliver AI training tailored to local industries—agriculture tech in rural areas, resource AI in mining regions, manufacturing automation in industrial towns. Make training free and accessible.
Ensure high-quality, affordable internet capable of supporting AI applications across all regions" - because slow, unreliable, or unaffordable connections create real barriers to AI literacy and participation, even if technical "access" exists.
Community Colleges as AI Centers
Transform regional colleges into AI training hubs with industry partnerships. Create certificates and diplomas that lead directly to local jobs, not just degrees that require moving to cities.
Relieve Urban Pressure, Build Regional Prosperity
Toronto's infrastructure is strained. Deliberately shifting AI development and jobs to other regions reduces urban crowding while creating prosperity where it's desperately needed. This is smart national development that makes AI opportunity accessible to all Canadians, regardless of postal code.

column2: Key skills for a modern digital economy include strong mathematical and algorithmic knowledge, advanced chip design and manufacturing expertise, and proficiency in high-performance computing. Canada should also focus on developing talent capable of optimizing AI processing for everyday technologies such as smartphones, electric vehicles, and industrial systems. By investing in education, technical training, and research programs that connect academia with industry, Canada can build a highly adaptable workforce ready to lead in both AI-driven innovation and broader digital transformation.
column3: Canada can enhance AI literacy by promoting education that emphasizes both the capabilities and limitations of AI systems. Public awareness campaigns and workplace training programs should teach citizens and professionals how AI works, where biases may arise, and how to use these tools responsibly. Government oversight of large AI platforms can ensure that critical or safety-related AI outputs are based on verified, unbiased information. By integrating AI literacy into schools, professional development, and public communication, Canada can build a society that understands AI’s strengths while remaining vigilant about its risks.
column4: Ensuring equitable access to AI literacy across Canada requires collaboration between federal, provincial, and local governments, as well as active participation from industry and educational institutions. While AI development will naturally concentrate in major cities with strong infrastructure, educational access should be nationwide. People in remote areas should have opportunities to participate in training programs, internships, and exchange initiatives in urban centers to experience and learn AI firsthand. This approach will help bridge regional and social gaps, ensuring that everyone can understand, use, and benefit from AI technologies with confidence.

column2:
Canada should create an "AI Cost Steward" micro-credential program for engineers, project managers, and finance professionals. This certification would teach reading cloud bills, calculating cost-per-AI-task, departmental cost allocation, detecting spending anomalies, and implementing automated controls.

These skills matter because AI projects often fail from unpredictable costs, not technical problems. The program should include funded bootcamps for SMEs to establish cost monitoring dashboards with spending controls in under two weeks.

This addresses the workforce gap where organizations can build AI but lack financial management skills to operate it sustainably. Making cost management a core competency ensures Canada's workforce can deploy AI profitably at scale, not just experiment.

Core stack: data literacy, prompt + critical thinking, software/product fundamentals, cybersecurity hygiene, AI-assisted workflows, and change management.

Role-specific depth: MLOps, data engineering, model evaluation, human-factors design, and sector domain knowledge (health, energy, agri, public).

How to build & deploy: expand co-ops/industrial MScs, micro-credentials recognized by employers, and paid upskilling during work hours; tie grants to training outcomes.

EzBuildaBiz role: modular tracks and readiness certifications that move workers/SMEs from “AI-curious” to “AI-productive,” with hands-on templates and playbooks.
column3:

Public awareness: plain-language campaigns, model cards at point of use, and demos that show failure modes and escalation paths.

Best practices: adopt ISO/IEC risk and governance basics in training; mandate red-team/incident drills for high-risk users.

EzBuildaBiz role: turnkey courses, checklists, and “trust badges” earned after bias, safety, and privacy modules.

Whole-of-ecosystem: co-funded programs across federal/provincial/municipal governments with industry-provided mentors, datasets, and capstone problems.

Measurement & trust: track completion, job transitions, wage gains, and regional uptake; publish results.

EzBuildaBiz role: localized cohorts with sector-specific playbooks and multilingual content, delivered via accelerators, EDCs, and community partners.

column2: A modern digital economy needs an adaptable workforce and adaptive leadership. Canada must cultivate three key skill groups: AI builders who design and deploy intelligent systems, AI translators who connect technical innovation to business strategy, and AI users across every sector who can leverage these tools effectively. To achieve this, Canada should tackle its unemployment and underemployment challenges head-on by investing in large-scale reskilling and education initiatives that prepare workers for digital and AI-driven roles. By combining short, practical training with industry partnerships and accessible upskilling programs, Canada can turn workforce displacement to something that grows through adaptability and technological leadership.
column3: Canada can enhance AI literacy by making it a core competency across the workforce, education, and public messaging. This means integrating practical AI awareness and ethics modules into schools, universities, professional training and everyday life. Including how AI works, and also on its limitations, biases, and responsible use. Governments and industry should co-fund short, stackable credentials for workers, especially in sectors adopting AI rapidly, and promote public awareness campaigns that explain how AI decisions affect everyday life. Learn from international best practices like Finland’s Elements of AI program or Singapore’s AI for Everyone initiative can guide implementation.

column2:
Canada needs a two-track skills agenda: AI makers and AI operators. Makers build models, sensors, and robots. Operators deploy, supervise, and maintain them in real settings.

Core skill families:

- Embodied/robotics skills: tele-operation, human–robot teaming, safety cases, mechatronics, edge computing, networking, cybersecurity for OT, and quality assurance.
- Data & model skills: data stewardship, prompt/programmatic interfaces, evaluation and red-teaming, reliability engineering, MLOps/RobOps, model provenance.
- Domain + systems skills: applied AI in health, energy, logistics, defence, and public administration; process mapping; change management; procurement literacy.
- Human factors: interface design, job/task redesign, ethical risk assessment, incident investigation, and communication.

How to build them:

- Create National AI & Robotics Academies across colleges and polytechnics with stackable micro-credentials (operator → technician → technologist), recognised by industry and the public sector.
- Fund tele-operation simulators and sandboxes so learners practise on realistic tasks before touching live equipment.
- Launch cross-sector fellowships rotating talent through university labs, Canadian SMEs, and public service units.
- Align apprenticeships for RobOps/AIOps with Red Seal and CSA standards. Treat “robot operator/maintainer” like any skilled trade.
- Tie federal adoption funding to training plans that upskill incumbent workers, not just hire net-new roles.
- Provide compute credits and shared datasets so schools and SMEs can actually train and test systems.
- Publish playbooks for job redesign and safety cases to help managers integrate AI without eroding trust or productivity.
column3:
AI literacy must be practical, not abstract. Canadians need to know what AI can do, where it fails, and how humans remain accountable.

Content priorities:

- The difference between digital AI (LLMs, analytics) and physical AI (robots, automation); where human control sits in each.
- Limitations and bias: data gaps, hallucinations, distribution shift, adversarial risk, sensor failure. Teach failure modes with real case studies.
- Safety and governance: model cards, audit trails, incident reporting, and “stop authority” for operators.
- Data and privacy basics: consent, minimisation, retention, and security for telemetry and health/service data.
- Prompting and oversight: how to specify tasks, verify outputs, and escalate to humans.

Delivery:

- Integrate AI literacy across grades 6–12 and all college programs, with hands-on modules using open tools and simulators.
- Offer workplace micro-credentials for supervisors and shop-floor staff: 10–20 hour courses that end with a safety drill or tabletop exercise.
- Produce public explainer sheets for government deployments (“what this system does, data used, how to opt out, how to complain”).
- Run community labs in libraries and maker spaces where people can try tele-op rigs, sensors, and safe demo robots.
- Require any publicly funded pilot to include a public briefing and short training for affected users.
- Share transparent incident summaries so Canadians see that the system learns from mistakes.
column4:
Equity requires meeting people where they live and work, with infrastructure and programs that are affordable and culturally relevant.

Access foundations:

- Fund regional compute hubs and device libraries so northern, rural, and low-income communities can run real workloads, not just watch videos.
- Provide connectivity grants for schools and clinics in remote areas, with cached training content for low-bandwidth settings.
- Translate materials into French and Indigenous languages and co-design modules with local educators and Elders.

Targeted programs:

- Trades-first AI literacy: free short courses on tele-operation, safety cases, and digital inspection for apprentices and journeypersons.
- Care economy modules: frontline AI literacy for long-term care and home-care workers, including privacy, escalation paths, and resident rights.
- Youth pipelines: robotics clubs, stipend-supported internships, and national competitions that include rural and northern teams.
- Second-chance and newcomer pathways: paid bridges into RobOps/AIOps technician roles with credential recognition and language support.
- Public service outreach: mobile “AI clinics” that visit communities to demo safe systems and answer questions with a focus on consent and accountability.

Guardrails:

- Tie federal funding to no-cost access for priority groups; require open materials and local train-the-trainer models.
- Track equity metrics: participation, completion, placement, and wage gains by region and demographic. Publish results and adjust funding to close gaps.

If we treat AI literacy like public health—universal, practical, and locally delivered—we’ll build confidence, capability, and opportunity across Canada.

column2:
We need skills at multiple levels:
Deep AI expertise: Researchers, engineers, data scientists who build and advance systems. Canada has strong graduate programs; we need to retain graduates through competitive opportunities.
Applied AI capacity: People who can adapt, implement, and maintain AI tools in specific contexts—healthcare, agriculture, manufacturing. This requires domain expertise plus technical literacy, and is often best served by mid-career upskilling rather than computer science degrees.
Critical AI assessment: Professionals who can evaluate whether AI is appropriate for a use case, whether a vendor's claims are credible, and whether deployment risks outweigh benefits. Every sector needs people who can ask good questions about AI, even if they can't build it.
Affected user competency: People who interact with AI systems need to understand their limitations, recognize when outputs are unreliable, and know their rights when AI affects consequential decisions.
Support mechanisms:
Formal education: Integrate critical AI literacy into K-12, not as coding but as information evaluation and algorithmic awareness. Support post-secondary programs that combine technical and domain expertise (health informatics, agricultural technology).
Workforce development: Subsidized upskilling for mid-career professionals in AI-adjacent roles. Micro-credentials for specific competencies. Employer incentives for training investments.
Public education: Accessible explanations of AI capabilities and limitations. Normalize critical questioning of AI systems rather than treating them as authoritative.
column3:
AI literacy shouldn't focus primarily on how to use AI tools—it should emphasize how to evaluate them. This includes:
Understanding provenance: Where did training data come from? Whose perspectives are included and excluded? A medical AI trained primarily on clinical trials that excluded certain demographics will have blind spots.
Recognizing shallow correctness: AI can provide plausible-sounding but contextually inappropriate answers. Users need to distinguish between "this sounds right" and "this is right for my situation."
Identifying embedded values: Every AI system encodes choices about what matters. A hiring algorithm optimized for "culture fit" might entrench homogeneity. A benefits allocation system optimized for fraud prevention might deny legitimate claims. Literacy means seeing these value choices.
Knowing limitations: AI works better in some contexts than others. It struggles with novel situations, rare events, and cases requiring contextual judgment. Users should know when to trust AI and when to escalate to human judgment.
Workplace programs should be sector-specific and validated by workers themselves. A training program for healthcare workers evaluating clinical AI should be co-designed with clinicians, not delivered by tech vendors. International best practices exist (EU AI literacy initiatives, Singapore's AI governance programs) but must be adapted to Canadian contexts and values.
column4:
Current AI literacy efforts risk reproducing existing disparities—urban versus rural, high-income versus low-income, tech sectors versus others. Equitable access requires:
Regional delivery: Partner with provincial and territorial governments, community colleges, libraries, and local organizations who have trusted relationships and contextual knowledge. Online-only programs miss populations with connectivity barriers or language needs.
Culturally appropriate approaches: Indigenous communities may prioritize AI literacy that connects to data sovereignty and traditional knowledge protection. Newcomer communities may need multilingual resources and navigation support. One curriculum won't serve all.
Universal design: Literacy programs should be accessible to people with varying education levels, technical backgrounds, and learning preferences. Avoid assuming university education or tech familiarity.
Employer responsibility: Mandate or incentivize employer-provided AI literacy for workers whose jobs involve AI systems. This is particularly important in frontline service roles where workers must explain AI decisions to the public.
Private sector role: Companies deploying AI should fund public literacy initiatives, particularly in communities affected by their systems. This is both corporate responsibility and enlightened self-interest—literate users provide better feedback and adoption.
Government's role is coordinating, funding, and ensuring coverage reaches underserved populations. Industry provides resources and real-world examples. Educational institutions deliver content. Civil society validates that approaches serve community needs.

column2:
Teach students what AI actually is before teaching them how to use it. Kids need to understand AI's fundamentals—that it's files on a computer processing patterns, not magic. Cover what it can and can't do reliably, its black box nature, limitations like hallucinations, and why it fails in certain contexts. This should be an entire foundational course, not a unit tacked onto existing classes.
Create a dedicated AI interaction course. Teach students what happens when they hit send—how platforms collect data, where that data goes, how it's used or sold, and which companies exploit it versus which respect privacy. Once students understand these mechanisms, they can use AI ethically and far more effectively. Understanding LLMs enables responsible use; without that foundation, they're just blindly trusting whatever tool is handed to them.
With privacy-first AI systems like MiigEngine where data isn't retained or exploited, students can actually experiment and learn without surveillance concerns. The current fear around AI in schools exists partly because mainstream tools harvest student data. Remove that risk and you can teach responsible collaboration with AI instead of banning it or letting students abuse it to write entire assignments.
column3:
Revive and update the House Hippo for the AI age. The original PSA taught an entire generation of Canadians to question what they see in media by showing them how convincing fake content could be. Do the same for AI. Create public awareness campaigns that demonstrate AI hallucinations, biases, and limitations in memorable, accessible ways. Show people AI confidently producing wrong answers, demonstrate how it can be manipulated, and teach them to verify outputs. Make it engaging and Canadian, not a boring corporate training module. Target these campaigns broadly—elderly Canadians are especially vulnerable because they often can't tell they're interacting with AI or viewing AI-generated content in the first place.
Demonstrate its limitations, not just explain them. People need to see AI fail in controlled examples to understand it's not infallible. Show deepfakes, hallucinated "facts," biased outputs, and incorrect reasoning in ways that stick. The House Hippo worked because it was experiential, you believed the hippo existed, then learned you'd been fooled. Apply that same principle to AI literacy. Give people tips and hints to help them determine if what they're seeing is even AI to begin with. Many Canadians, particularly older generations, lack the baseline ability to recognize AI-generated content, making them easy targets for manipulation and scams.

If an LLM can write 90% of code, the new, high-value human role isn’t writing the remaining 10%; it’s designing the governing protocol that ensures the 90% is actually true.

column2:
Canada needs two classes of capability to compete in a modern, AI-enabled economy: technical depth and decision quality.

The first is obvious: engineering talent (machine learning, data engineering, cybersecurity, model assurance, AI safety, human-computer interaction, compute infrastructure). We do need more builders.

The second is less obvious but just as critical: leaders and operators who can evaluate, govern, and safely deploy AI in real systems. That includes AI risk assessment, data governance, critical infrastructure resilience, auditability, compliance, and change management. Canada will fall behind not because we lack coders, but because we lack people who can responsibly integrate advanced systems into health, energy, finance, defence, and public service delivery.

Canada should invest in three things:

Mission-aligned technical training: Fund applied AI, cybersecurity, and systems engineering programs that are tied to national priorities (health capacity, clean energy, grid resilience, trusted finance, defence readiness). This keeps talent in Canada and ties training to real use.

Executive and managerial upskilling: Create credentials for “AI-responsible leadership” boards, public service executives, infrastructure operators. If decision-makers cannot judge AI risk, AI will either be blocked or deployed recklessly.

Rotational talent models: Allow high-skill professionals to rotate between government, critical infrastructure operators, and private AI firms without losing progression. This spreads capability faster than building each silo separately.

In short: Canada must train people to build AI, and train people to govern AI. Both are economic capacity.
column3:
AI literacy in Canada cannot just be “how to use AI tools.” It must include: where AI is reliable, where it breaks, who is accountable when it breaks, and what rights people have.

There are four levers:

Workplace literacy and credentialing. Every major sector, healthcare, energy/utilities, finance, logistics, public service should have role-specific guidance and micro-credentials covering: how AI is being used in that domain, what it can and cannot decide alone, and what to do when it produces a questionable or biased output. This should be part of onboarding, not optional training.

Plain-language disclosure of AI limitations. Institutions that deploy AI in decisions affecting access, eligibility, safety, or financial outcomes should be required to disclose: “AI was used to assist this decision. Here’s what it is good at. Here’s where it can fail. Here’s how to challenge it.” Normalizing fallibility prevents over-trust and under-trust.

National public awareness campaigns focused on harm prevention, not hype. The message should not be “AI is the future,” it should be “AI is already in critical systems, here’s how it’s governed, here’s how you’re protected.” People gain confidence when they see that someone is in charge.

Bias and safety testing as part of social license. Before large-scale deployment in sensitive contexts, organizations should be expected to demonstrate that they tested for bias, security risk, and misuse. That expectation itself builds public literacy: it signals that risk is real, and it is being managed.

AI literacy should be framed as civic literacy, not just digital literacy.
column4:
If AI literacy is only available to executives in Toronto or engineers in Montreal, we will harden inequality and we will also lose trust. Equity is not just a fairness issue; it is a stability issue.

Canada should act on three levels:

Community-anchored delivery. Partner with provinces, municipalities, Indigenous governments, libraries, settlement agencies, labour unions, and chambers of commerce to deliver AI literacy as public service infrastructure, not just as university programming. Rural and remote communities need access to the same “what AI is doing to my job / how to protect myself / how to benefit” training as major centres.

Sector-aware training for workers, not just leadership. Frontline workers in health administration, logistics, natural resources, manufacturing, and municipal services are already experiencing AI-enabled monitoring, scheduling, and triage tools. They deserve plain-language training on: what is being automated, what is being tracked, what rights they retain, and how to escalate concerns. This protects dignity and lowers resentment.

No-cost access. Publicly funded AI literacy materials should be free, multilingual, and designed for different levels of digital comfort. Otherwise “AI literacy” becomes something that only well-resourced employers and regions can access, which directly erodes national cohesion and public trust.

Bottom line: equitable AI literacy is not a “nice to have.” It is the difference between national adoption and national backlash.

column2: Canada's digital economy requires three types of skills. First, there are foundational skills necessary for everyone, which include basic data and AI literacy, privacy and security practices, introductory statistics, and effective communication. Second, we need applied builder skills. This encompasses data engineering, machine learning (ML) and machine learning operations (MLOps), model testing and safety, secure software development, and product/user experience (UX) skills that ensure AI can be effectively integrated into real workflows. Third, there is demand for domain experts with AI fluency across fields such as health, energy, finance, manufacturing, agriculture, and government. These experts should be able to identify valuable AI use cases and evaluate their results. To rapidly develop these skills, we should implement practical data and AI modules in K–12 education and colleges, expand cooperative education programs and capstone projects, and introduce short micro-credentials that align with real job opportunities. We can also assist small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) by providing boot camps, starter kits, and shared help desks through trusted organizations and sector councils. To facilitate hands-on practice, we need to provide affordable computing resources and tools. Finally, we should measure success based on job placements, the launch of first production projects, and real-world impact.

Les compétences nécessaires à une économie numérique moderne dépassent les savoir-faire techniques : elles doivent être créatives, critiques et humaines, en phase avec les enjeux éthiques, culturels et sociaux de l’intelligence artificielle.

Compétences clés à développer :
• Créatives et critiques : Capacité à innover, à résoudre des problèmes complexes de manière éthique et à exercer une pensée critique sur les impacts de l’IA.
• Linguistiques et de données : Maîtrise de la gouvernance des données et développement d’une main-d’œuvre capable de soutenir la souveraineté linguistique, notamment en français.
• Transférables : Compétences en adaptabilité, collaboration interdisciplinaire et tolérance à l’incertitude.

Mesures de soutien recommandées :
• Financement inclusif : Rendre les programmes fédéraux d’innovation et de formation (ISDE, EDSC) accessibles aux OBNL culturels et aux créateurs.
• Formation ciblée : Investir dans la formation continue en éthique et culture numérique, et dans la création d’expertises spécialisées pour le secteur culturel francophone.
• Reconnaissance des savoirs culturels : Intégrer les arts et la créativité numérique dans les parcours de formation pour valoriser la dimension humaine de l’IA.

Pour améliorer les connaissances sur l’IA, il est essentiel de sensibiliser aux biais linguistiques, culturels et sociaux que les systèmes d’IA peuvent reproduire, et de promouvoir une approche critique et éthique.

Stratégies recommandées :
• Déployer des programmes de formation axés sur l’éthique, la diversité et la critique culturelle de l’IA.
• Utiliser les organismes culturels comme vecteurs de sensibilisation, en abordant les enjeux de souveraineté numérique, de découvrabilité et de diversité.

« L’IA peut renforcer l’homogénéisation des contenus plutôt que de favoriser une diversité des expressions culturelles. »

Stratégies proposées :
• Formations décentralisées et adaptées : Financer des formations mobiles pour les communautés francophones en contexte minoritaire et les peuples autochtones.
• Collaboration territoriale : Travailler avec des associations comme la FCCF pour déployer l’expertise en IA directement dans les communautés.
• Soutien aux créateurs : Développer des programmes adaptés aux OBNL culturels et des ateliers pour les créateurs, souvent travailleurs autonomes.

column2: The most important skills for a modern, digital economy are a healthy dose of skepticism and critical thinking. column3: The best way to achieve this would be to help Canadians achieve greater computer literacy, so that they're able to understand how computers actually work, and thus will be less likely to be swayed by hype cycles that are built on taking advantage of the lack of understanding that many people have. column2:
A modern digital economy needs workers who can use, shape, and when required question how technology is applied. Technical skills like AI, data, and cybersecurity matter, but so does domain knowledge in sectors like health, education, and manufacturing. AI companies often have strong technical capability but limited understanding of how these industries actually work, which can lead to solutions that miss real-world needs. In fact, our firm in particular sees this balance as a big differentiator.

To bridge that gap, Canada can promote collaboration between AI firms and domain experts through applied research hubs, shared innovation labs, and funded pilot projects. Programs like these help AI developers learn context while giving industry partners access to new tools and talent. Governments can encourage this by linking funding and procurement to partnerships that combine both skill sets.

At Innogist.AI, we are already applying this model by combining technical research in AI governance and orchestration with deep experience in education, government, and enterprise transformation. This kind of partnership-driven approach helps ensure that AI solutions are not only advanced but relevant, trusted, and ready to deliver measurable results in the real economy.
column3:
Canada can build meaningful AI literacy by combining accessibility, real examples, and community engagement. The model used in Finland’s “Elements of AI” course is a strong example: it is free, available online, written in plain language, and designed for all citizens. It teaches what AI can and cannot do, includes real-life scenarios, and builds ethical awareness. Canada can adopt a similar approach by creating short, low-barrier courses that anyone can take, supported by colleges, libraries, and employers. These should include both online and offline options so people in rural and remote areas can participate, even with limited broadband.

AI literacy must also extend to the workplace. Governments and businesses can co-develop micro-credentials and practical training where employees learn to use AI responsibly in their daily work. Public awareness campaigns can highlight success stories while addressing misconceptions about bias, privacy, and job impacts. The goal is to help people understand its limits, question its outputs, and use it safely. If Canada invests in inclusive education and practical awareness - rooted in its own regional and social realities it can build a confident, informed population ready to guide AI’s growth responsibly.
column4:
Canada can close the AI literacy gap by making access local, practical, and culturally grounded. For rural and northern regions, that means mobile training units, community-based AI hubs, and offline learning kits that can run without fast internet. These can be hosted in libraries, schools, or local offices, equipped with basic computing resources and supported by trained local facilitators. Governments should fund low-bandwidth AI learning platforms and ensure that programs are available in both official languages, as well as Indigenous and heritage languages where possible. This approach brings learning to where people already are and removes the cost and connectivity barriers that prevent many Canadians from participating.

For Indigenous communities, AI can also be a tool for language and cultural preservation. Micro-grants and technical support could help record oral histories, build digital dictionaries, and create local datasets for speech and text tools that remain under community ownership. Partnering with Indigenous-led organizations ensures that training reflects data sovereignty principles and benefits flow directly to the community. I've written about this on LinkedIn. Industry in turn can help by donating cloud credits, offering mentorship, and co-developing learning materials while respecting local control and consent.

column3:
Enhancing AI literacy in Canada requires equipping citizens with the skills to critically evaluate AI-generated content, understand its limitations, and recognize biases. Public libraries are uniquely positioned to support this goal, as institutions that excel in teaching information literacy and source verification—skills directly transferable to identifying deepfakes, misinformation, and other AI-related threats to individuals, organizations, and democratic systems.

With roughly 16,000 participants attending technology programs each month, libraries provide multigenerational access to education, reaching youth, seniors, newcomers, and other vulnerable populations. Libraries offer both physical and digital platforms for social gathering, ideation, and debate, creating inclusive spaces where Canadians can explore AI ethically and collaboratively. They can host AI ethics discussions, facilitate community input on policy development, and ensure diverse voices are part of shaping AI use that aligns with Canadian values.

By investing in library readiness, the government can leverage an existing national infrastructure of trusted, judgment-free education. Librarians’ expertise in critical evaluation and information literacy ensures Canadians gain practical, transferable skills to navigate AI responsibly, supporting both social innovation and the safeguarding of Canada’s democratic and community foundations.
column4:
Ensuring equitable access to AI literacy across Canada requires leveraging institutions that are both universally accessible and deeply embedded in local communities. Public libraries, with more than 3,300 locations in urban, suburban, rural, and remote areas, provide coast-to-coast infrastructure capable of delivering inclusive AI education while maintaining local relevance. Roughly 30% of Canadians live within walking distance of a public library, and all services are free, removing financial and geographic barriers.

Libraries are essential community hubs connected to hundreds of local service providers, schools, and social institutions. For example, Invermere Public Library collaborates with CBAL newcomer services, Family Dynamix social services, and the local school district to extend learning opportunities and reach vulnerable populations. Libraries also provide online platforms that expand training access to Canadians unable to visit physically.

No other public institution works as broadly and consistently across generations as libraries, enabling youth, seniors, newcomers, and other groups to develop critical skills for navigating AI-generated content responsibly. Federal investment in library-led AI literacy initiatives ensures equitable infrastructure, prevents a two-tier system where postal code determines access, and safeguards Canadian values and the social cohesion that strengthens our communities.

column2:
As educational researchers and teacher-educators, we argue Canada must cultivate AI literacy as a multiliteracy—a blend of conceptual understanding (what AI is and does), ethical reasoning (bias, privacy, equity), critical data practices, and pedagogical design skills that let learners use AI to inquire, create, and reflect. Evidence shows AI’s impact spans administration, instruction, and learning; therefore, preparing educators to re-design assessment, feedback, and inquiry with AI—rather than simply adopting tools—is decisive for downstream workforce readiness. Our AIME model demonstrates that implementation fails without sustained capacity-building for faculty and teacher candidates, framed as ethical, culturally responsive pedagogy rather than tool training. National policy should therefore (a) embed AI literacy across K-12 curricula and teacher-education programs; (b) fund practicum-linked, design-based learning with school boards; and (c) provide protected time and resources for professional learning that moves beyond compliance to critical, learner-centred innovation.
(Mueller & Ewart, 2025)
Mueller, J., & Ewart, K. (2025). AIME: Advancing Ethical, Inclusive AI Integration in Teacher Education (Use case). In Integrating AI in Education: Transforming Learning — C21 Canada (pp. 1–3). https://c21canada.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/05/ON-WLU-PDF-AIUseCase-2025.pdf
column3:
Three mutually reinforcing actions:
(i) Educator-first capacity: Build teacher AI literacy as the lever for children’s literacy, with content that addresses limitations, bias, surveillance, and workload trade-offs, not only functionality. This is a named ethical priority in K-12 policy development and a practical lesson from AIME’s workshops (case-based, inquiry-driven, equity-centred). 
(ii) Classroom assessment redesign: Support districts to pilot AI-aware assessment (process portfolios, oral defenses, metacognitive reflections, disclosure norms) that make AI’s role visible and teachable. Evidence suggests AI can personalize and support feedback, while human judgment must remain central. 
(iii) Public-facing literacy: Commission open courses and student-produced explainers that demystify how models work, where they err, and how communities can contest harms—moving from passive exploration to intentional, ethical design.
Mueller, J., & Ewart, K. (2025). AIME: Advancing Ethical, Inclusive AI Integration in Teacher Education (Use case). In Integrating AI in Education: Transforming Learning — C21 Canada (pp. 1–3). https://c21canada.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/05/ON-WLU-PDF-AIUseCase-2025.pdf 
Adams, C., Pente, P., Lemermeyer, G., & Rockwell, G. (2023). Ethical principles for artificial intelligence in K-12 education. Computers and Education: Artificial Intelligence, 4, 100131.
column4:
Equity requires targeted supports and localized co-design. We propose (a) dedicated funding streams for rural, northern, and underserved urban schools to access devices, connectivity, and trusted Canadian AI tools; (b) a national educator micro-credential in AI, with paid release time; and (c) community-partnered learning hubs modelled on AIME’s “AI as learning context” approach, which centres cultural responsiveness and student voice. Policies should explicitly integrate children’s rights and teacher well-being into procurement and professional learning criteria, ensuring that AI reduces mechanical load and expands time for relationship-rich teaching.
Adams, C., Pente, P., Lemermeyer, G., & Rockwell, G. (2023). Ethical principles for artificial intelligence in K-12 education. Computers and Education: Artificial Intelligence, 4, 100131.

column2:
Canada’s workforce needs a blend of technical, human, and adaptive skills to thrive in a modern, digital economy. This includes AI and digital literacy, data skills, cybersecurity, alongside critical thinking, collaboration, communication, and cultural competence, plus the ability to adapt quickly to technological change.

To support this, Canada should:

Expand accessible training and upskilling programs that reach women, Indigenous Peoples, newcomers, neurodiverse individuals, and adults with literacy barriers.

Integrate workforce readiness initiatives that combine technical skills with human and adaptive skills.

Strengthen partnerships across academia, industry, government, and community organizations to align learning with labor market needs.

Offer incentives and supports such as mentorship, funding, and career transition programs to ensure broad participation.

By fostering inclusive, adaptable, and technologically proficient talent, Canada can sustain a workforce ready for rapid digital transformation while promoting equitable opportunities for all Canadians.
column3:
Canada can strengthen AI literacy by combining foundational skills, critical awareness, and workforce integration, ensuring all Canadians are equipped to understand, use, and question AI responsibly.

Key strategies include:

Foundational literacy and digital readiness: Provide accessible programs that develop reading, writing, and digital skills, particularly for adults with literacy barriers, newcomers, Indigenous Peoples, women, and neurodiverse individuals, so they can engage confidently with AI technologies.

Awareness of AI limitations and biases: Educate citizens and workers about how AI systems operate, where biases may arise, and how human choices influence outcomes. Public campaigns, community programs, and curriculum modules can promote understanding of ethical, inclusive, and responsible AI use.

Workforce integration and ongoing learning: Offer scalable workplace training to ensure employees in all sectors gain AI competency, including critical thinking, decision-making oversight, and bias mitigation.

Ethical and inclusive frameworks: Embed principles of fairness, transparency, and accountability in AI education to empower communities to participate in and shape AI deployment.

By combining literacy development, critical awareness, workforce training, and ethical education, Canada can create a society that trusts, understands, and benefits equitably from AI.
column4:
To build a truly inclusive AI‑literate society, Canada must break down barriers to access and participation and target outreach to demographic groups that are often excluded—such as women, Indigenous peoples, newcomers, adults with literacy or foundational skills barriers, and people living in rural or remote regions.

Key strategies include:

Target foundational literacy and digital access: Provide accessible digital‑and‑AI literacy programmes in adult‑learning centres, libraries, community hubs, and workplaces. For example, national programmes have offered digital skills workshops to under‑represented women, newcomers, Indigenous women and low‑income Canadians. 
MediaSmarts
+1

Design female‑ and equity‑focused intervention programmes: Research shows that women‑owned SMEs adopt AI at lower rates—and when tailored programmes are offered to women and gender‑diverse entrepreneurs (regionally distributed across Prairies, Northern and Atlantic Canada) uptake improves. 



Regional and demographic reach: Ensure training programs are delivered not just in large urban centres but across provinces, territories, remote communities and underserved neighbourhoods. Also ensure content is culturally and linguistically relevant (including for Indigenous learners and newcomers).

Wrap‑around support: Provide supports like mentorship, flexible scheduling (evenings/weekends), childcare or literacy supports, and community networks that help adults with barriers transition into AI‑related literacy/training opportunities.

Collaborative delivery and public‑private partnerships: Bring together government, adult literacy organizations, tech training providers and employers to co‑create programmes that strengthen both understanding of AI and critical awareness of its limitations, biases and societal impacts.

Measure and track equity outcomes: Collect disaggregated data on participation by gender, Indigenous status, newcomers, persons with disabilities, literacy level and region

column2: Success in a modern, digital economy requires more than technical expertise—it demands creativity, ethical judgment, collaboration, and adaptability. Engineering education in Canada is evolving to prepare graduates who can design, deploy, and govern technologies responsibly while working across disciplines. By combining hands-on problem solving with digital fluency, data literacy, and societal awareness, we are equipping students to lead in an AI-enabled world. Government can accelerate this shift by supporting experiential learning, interdisciplinary programs, and partnerships that link education directly to emerging industry and community needs.
