In shaping Canada’s future, we must teach the principles of intellectual self-defense as articulated by Noam Chomsky and Ursula Franklin. Their work offers essential frameworks for understanding technology not as a neutral force, but as a system embedded with choices—choices that must be made consciously, ethically, and democratically.

We are witnessing a troubling trend: society increasingly surrenders its agency to politicians, institutions, and influencers in the hope of being heard, seen, or reassured. This erosion of personal and collective autonomy is dangerous. It undermines the very foundation of democratic life.

Education must be our strongest safeguard. In the age of artificial intelligence, we must prioritize critical thinking, civic literacy, and technological awareness. Students should not only learn how AI works—they must learn how to question it, challenge it, and shape its use in ways that serve the public good.

Agency is not optional. It is the cornerstone of a free society. Canada must ensure that every citizen is equipped to think independently, act responsibly, and defend their intellectual freedom—especially as we navigate the profound transformations brought by AI.

Canada’s modern, digital economy depends on people who can adapt, learn, and apply technology with confidence. The skills needed go beyond coding, they include communication, problem-solving, ethical reasoning, data literacy, creativity, and the ability to work with and alongside AI.

To build this capacity, Canada must connect education, career development, and industry through a national Work-Integrated Learning (WIL) and career readiness strategy. We already lead globally in WIL and are the only country with an accreditation framework that ensures learners identify what they will learn, reflect while learning, and reflect after. These same wrap-around supports are what prepare adaptable, future-ready workers.

Companies like InStage, a Canadian AI firm recognized internationally, are foundational to this. Their simulation and reflection technology lets learners safely practice interviews, presentations, and workplace interactions, helping them articulate skills and build confidence. When deployed nationally, this becomes a scalable engine for reskilling, upskilling, and workforce readiness across every sector.

To act now:

Invest in scalable solutions. Fund national deployment of proven AI learning tools like InStage through education, workforce, and employer networks.

Integrate career development and reflection into every training or reskilling program.

Align incentives across federal, provincial, and industry programs to make WIL a default part of learning.

Measure outcomes—how well Canadians can identify and use their skills, not just how many credentials they earn.

Canada can lead the G7 in human-centred, AI-enabled workforce readiness—but only if we invest in solutions that can scale now. The future workforce is already here; it just needs the tools and systems to keep up.
column3:
AI literacy in Canada must go beyond technical understanding—it’s about helping people use, question, and trust AI responsibly. Canadians need to know both the potential and the limits: what AI can do, when to use it, and when human judgment matters most.

Start early and teach often. Introduce AI awareness in K-12 and deepen it through post-secondary programs, especially within co-op and Work-Integrated Learning (WIL). Learners should not only use AI but also reflect on bias, ethics, and the human impact of technology.

Embed reflection and ethics in training. Every AI course or credential should include real scenarios where learners practice identifying bias, verifying information, and describing how decisions are made. This builds the habit of mindful use.

Use scalable Canadian tools. Companies like InStage, already recognized internationally, enable simulated learning and guided reflection that help people build confidence and understand AI’s strengths and weaknesses. These platforms make literacy practical, accessible, and measurable at scale.

Train the current workforce. Offer micro-credentials and short courses through employers, unions, and professional bodies so people in every role—from teachers to technicians—understand how to use AI responsibly and spot its risks.

Run public awareness campaigns. Use plain language storytelling and community partnerships to explain how AI is tested, monitored, and used in daily life. Transparency builds confidence.

Learn from global best practice. Adopt models from Singapore and Finland that teach AI literacy as a civic skill—free, inclusive, and constantly updated with input from educators, industry, and government.

AI literacy is not optional—it’s a national competency. By blending reflection, ethics, and applied practice through education and work, Canada can create a population that doesn’t fear AI but uses it wisely and humanely.
column4:
Equitable AI literacy will not happen on its own. Today, those who can afford tools learn faster; those who cannot fall behind. Some campuses and employment centres can buy modern solutions, others cannot. A northern school just dropped a tool while a well-funded urban campus expands. This is not a sales pitch; it is a system gap that hurts learners, job seekers, and Canadian firms whose customers cannot adopt.
What to do now
National Access Fund
Create a federal program that co-buys vetted AI learning tools for public schools, colleges, universities, employment centres, libraries, and community groups. Use a single catalogue and 30-day payment so adoption is fast and fair.
Shared infrastructure
Invest in connectivity, secure cloud credits, and device access for regions with weak networks, including the North and rural communities. No connectivity, no literacy.
Provincial and municipal compacts
Strike cost-sharing agreements so provinces and cities can add seats locally without new procurement. Align with existing education and workforce budgets.
Community first delivery
Deliver AI literacy through libraries, settlement agencies, Indigenous learning centres, unions, and chambers. Provide ready-to-run kits and facilitator training in plain language and in both official languages.

Inclusive program design
Prioritize newcomers, Indigenous communities, persons with disabilities, youth, and mid-career workers. Include accessibility standards, child-care supports, and stipends where needed.
Adopt platforms already working in classrooms and job centres (for example, InStage for simulation and reflection) so learners can practice, get feedback, and articulate skills. Choose tools through a transparent vet-and-verify process, not pay-to-play.
Track access by region and income, practice hours completed, skills articulated, job placements, and wage gains. Publish results each quarter.
Create a federal steering group with provinces, territories to prevent duplication.

At this point, education across all of society is needed. From elementary school through high school, and across the business world. This can be met by an ecosystem of AI educators who target the various aspects of adoption – risk and responsible AI, design/redesign of business systems that use AI, and preparing students for the new age and adapting to change. 

Supporting AI educators through funding can be a solution but great care needs to be taken to ensure quality. Some level of vetting needs to be done to ensure that funding is given to those who have real knowledge. At the same time, many aspects of AI as it is today are not learned in degree programs so past education should not be a filter. 

The Victoria Data Society (VDS) is a non-profit organization that was established in August 2024. Its purpose was to convene professionals in the data and AI space for networking and professional development. Our membership and interest in our programs has significantly expanded in the past year and the board is in the process to develop a strategy that expands our mandate. Given the collective knowledge and talent around data and AI, we are situated to be able to evaluate and vet the quality of education programs focused on data and AI. We can then match organizations’ education needs with vetted resources. 

This is a program we intend to launch at the beginning of next year. Other similar grassroots organizations could use this model to enhance AI education while supporting the growth of small and medium sized businesses that can provide valuable education.

Design thinking, communication, ethics, problem-solving, creativity, and adaptability and many others will be crucial to optimizing decision-making that can effectively leverage the capabilities of AI. Advancing an ‘AI-first’ culture requires skills such as strategic foresight to help workers translate technical knowledge into business value and maintain a culture of learning amidst continued technological shifts.

Targeted SME upskilling supports: Provide funding, incentives, and shared platforms for business-to-business mentorship, retraining, and upskilling programs that help workers and small businesses adapt to emerging technologies.

Government can encourage Academia to ensure compensation for creators of Educational resources, in both K-12 and Post-Secondary sectors. column2:
Canada’s digital economy and particularly its health care system, relies on developing a workforce that combines technical expertise, operational skill, and ethical awareness. This includes the technical capabilities required for applied AI development, privacy-preserving data use, and secure deployment, as well as the practical ability to interpret AI outputs, understand their limitations, and integrate them responsibly into clinical workflows. 
To build and sustain these capabilities, Canada should invest in targeted training programs for health care providers, administrators, and technical teams. Education that explains AI’s role, benefits, and limitations, will strengthen understanding, improve confidence, and support responsible adoption within the health system.
Hands-on learning opportunities are essential for real-world readiness. Expanding sandbox pilots that allow teams to test and evaluate Canadian-built AI tools in operational settings will help participants gain practical experience and ensure that skill development remains aligned with system priorities and patient needs.
National standards should provide a consistent foundation for all AI-related education and credentialing. Embedding a Pan-Canadian Interoperability Standard for Health AI and Canadian privacy requirements into training will promote consistency across jurisdictions, ensuring that the health AI workforce is not only technically proficient but also deeply aligned with Canada’s values of safety, transparency, and accountability.
column3:
Canada must make transparency and informed use the cornerstone of AI adoption in health care. This requires plain-language disclosure whenever AI is used in care and proactive education for patients and providers about its purpose, limitations, and outcomes. Clear communication builds understanding and addresses mistrust at its source, ensuring that technology strengthens rather than undermines public confidence in the health system.
To ensure AI is applied safely and ethically in practice, fairness, explainability, and bias monitoring must be integrated into deployment. Health care professionals should be trained to interpret AI outputs, identify potential risks, and raise concerns when necessary, turning ethical principles into reliable, real-world safeguards that protect patients and enhance quality of care.
Strong institutional support is essential for long-term success. Sandbox pilots should be used to train teams on how to evaluate and govern AI tools safely in practical environments. Educational resources and policy guidance should be updated every two to three years to ensure that training remains relevant as technologies evolve and clinical needs change.
National AI educational standards should help Canadians learn to recognize what AI-generated content is likely authentic versus what is false/malicious, including practical tools for identifying manipulated media and verifying credible sources through curricula, training, and public education initiatives.
A foundation of privacy by design must guide all literacy and governance efforts. Deployments should comply fully with PIPEDA and provincial privacy legislation, and identifiable patient data should never be used for training without explicit authorization and transparent consent. Embedding these principles into every level of education and implementation will ensure that health professionals across Canada understand not only how to use AI, but how to use it responsibly and in ways that uphold public trust
column4:
Canada should standardize expectations for AI literacy and adoption while allowing local flexibility to reflect the diversity of health systems across the country. A Pan-Canadian Interoperability Standard for Health AI, supported by strong national privacy safeguards, should serve as a shared foundation for education and implementation. At the same time, provinces, territories, and health institutions should be empowered to adapt materials to their local contexts so that programs remain relevant, accessible, and culturally appropriate.
Clear and inclusive communication is essential. All AI-related materials should be written in plain-language English and French, upholding Canada’s commitment to bilingualism and accessibility while ensuring that every citizen understands how AI is being used in their health care.
A coordinated, multi-stakeholder approach is needed to deliver AI programs effectively. Federal, provincial, and territorial governments should convene and fund initiatives, update guidance every two to three years, and ensure equitable access for rural, indigenous and underserved communities. Health institutions and universities should integrate AI literacy into professional training and credentialing, while industry partners should help develop user-facing resources and embed explainability and bias monitoring directly into their products to strengthen understanding at the point of care.
Hands-on learning opportunities must be available across all regions. Regional sandbox pilots should give communities the chance to test and refine Canadian-built tools in real clinical environments before wider deployment. This approach promotes equal access to education, builds local expertise, and ensures that every Canadian, regardless of geography or background, can take part in the responsible governance and use of AI in health care.

column2:
A modern digital economy requires a workforce that combines technical ability with creativity, adaptability, and ethical understanding. As AI and automation reshape industries, Canada must prepare people not only to build and manage these systems but also to work effectively alongside them.

The essential skills include data literacy, coding, and AI system understanding, but also problem-solving, design thinking, and cross-disciplinary collaboration. Workers in every field—from health care to manufacturing to the creative industries—will need a baseline understanding of how digital systems make decisions and how to use them responsibly.

Canada can support this through integrated education and lifelong learning. Schools and universities should embed digital and AI literacy across all disciplines, not just computer science. Vocational colleges and professional training centers should expand applied programs in machine learning, cybersecurity, cloud infrastructure, and data governance. Micro-credentialing and online certification can help workers update skills quickly as technology evolves.

Partnerships between government, industry, and unions are vital for ensuring that training reflects real market needs. Programs like VoxID Canada show how new fields—such as AI voice authentication and digital identity management—can create jobs that combine technical and ethical expertise.

Employers should be encouraged to invest in workforce reskilling through tax credits and co-funded training initiatives. Public investment in community-based learning hubs can ensure access across regions and age groups.

By treating digital skills as a shared national resource, Canada can build a workforce that adapts quickly, drives innovation, and keeps the country competitive in a global economy increasingly shaped by AI.
column3:
Enhancing AI literacy in Canada requires a coordinated national effort that combines education, communication, and transparency. Canadians need to understand not only what AI can do but also where its limits and biases lie. True literacy means being able to question and evaluate AI systems rather than simply use them.

The foundation should begin in education and workforce training. Schools, colleges, and universities must include courses that explain how AI models work, what data they rely on, and how bias can appear in automated decision-making. Employers can build on this with short, practical training programs that help workers apply AI tools responsibly in their industries. Micro-credentials and online certifications should emphasize both technical literacy and ethical awareness.

Public awareness campaigns can further strengthen understanding. Governments, broadcasters, and trusted organizations should collaborate to share clear examples of both AI benefits and risks. Community workshops, open forums, and creative media can make these lessons accessible to people of all ages.

Canada can learn from international models such as Finland’s “Elements of AI” program, which made foundational AI education freely available to citizens. Adapting this approach through Canadian institutions would help reach a broad audience while reflecting local cultural and linguistic diversity.

Programs like VoxID Canada demonstrate how transparency and accountability can be taught through real applications, showing the public how digital verification and traceability address bias and misuse.

By embedding AI literacy into education, workplaces, and civic life, Canada can create a society that is informed, confident, and capable of guiding technology in a way that reflects shared national values.
column4:
Ensuring equitable access to AI literacy in Canada requires collaboration among governments, industry, educators, and community organizations so that no group or region is left behind. Digital knowledge must be treated as a public good, with the same importance as literacy or numeracy.

A national AI literacy strategy should provide consistent learning opportunities across provinces and territories, supported by federal funding and regional implementation. Local governments and school boards can adapt materials to reflect community needs, languages, and cultural contexts, including Indigenous and rural communities where access to technology and training is often limited.

Public libraries, colleges, and community centers can serve as digital learning hubs, offering free access to computers, online courses, and workshops that teach the basics of AI systems, data privacy, and responsible technology use. Programs should be available in both official languages and designed for different age groups and skill levels.

The private sector also plays a key role. Technology companies and AI startups can partner with schools and training institutions to provide mentorship, practical learning modules, and scholarships for underrepresented groups. Initiatives like VoxID Canada show how industry leadership can be combined with social purpose by creating tools and programs that educate users about transparency and digital identity protection.

Finally, the federal government should invest in digital infrastructure, especially broadband connectivity, to make online learning accessible nationwide. By ensuring that education, technology, and opportunity reach every Canadian, the country can build an inclusive foundation for AI literacy that strengthens both social equity and economic resilience.

column2: A modern, digital economy needs three layers of skills: (1) technical AI/data skills (data management, ML methods, evaluation), (2) deployment skills (including monitoring and oversight), (3) domain skills (health, public health, clinical ops ect.) and (4) Responsible AI (ethics, equity, regulation). We built an AI4PH training platform to address these skills across this skills matrix and support mixed teams that can define a problem, make or adapt a responsible AI solution, and then evaluate it in a real health or public health setting. Canada should scale that model: fund sector-specific AI upskilling and make public-sector employers eligible for training dollars.
column3: Building AI literacy in Canada must go beyond the usual “what is AI” slides and focus on helping people understand what AI can actually do, where it can go wrong, and how to critically question it. We can take a cue from public health communication,  such as how we discuss vaccines, by incorporating AI into everyday public conversations. In schools, students should begin learning the basics of AI and develop critical thinking skills about technology as early as high school. In the workplace, sector-specific training programs, such as the AI4PH model for public health, can demonstrate how AI is applied in real-world settings and how to identify and address bias. And just as important, governments and health systems should be transparent about when and how AI is used and report publicly. People trust AI more when they can see what it’s doing and how it’s tested and regulated. Again, we can learn from strategies that have been effective for vaccines or other public health interventions.
column4: Canada can make AI literacy more equitable by bringing learning to communities, rather than expecting people to come to it. A train-the-trainer model, equipping teachers, community health staff, and Indigenous leaders to teach AI in their own contexts, is also a strong way to reach diverse groups.

column3:
Public libraries are uniquely positioned to help Canadians navigate the growing skills needed to navigate AI-generated misinformation, including deepfakes and biased content. As experts in information literacy and source verification, librarians teach essential skills that directly translate to identifying and critically evaluating AI-generated content. 

With over 16,000 people attending technology programs at public libraries each month, we are already equipping Canadians with the tools they need to understand the limitations and biases of digital information. No other public institution engages so broadly and inclusively across generations—libraries reach youth, seniors, newcomers, and other vulnerable populations, ensuring that AI literacy is accessible to all.

As trusted, democratic institutions and vital social infrastructure, public libraries offer more than just education—they provide safe, inclusive spaces for dialogue, debate, and community engagement. We can host public discussions on AI ethics, facilitate community input into AI policy development, and serve as hubs where diverse voices shape the future of AI in Canada. With existing platforms for online training and a strong foundation in privacy protection and intellectual freedom, libraries are ready to support a national AI literacy strategy that reflects Canadian values and strengthens our democratic fabric.
column4:
With more than 3,300 public library locations across urban, suburban, rural, and remote communities, Canada’s public library sector offers an unparalleled national infrastructure for equitable access to information and learning. Public libraries are essential community hubs—open seven days a week and free to all—providing coast-to-coast service that eliminates financial, geographic, and social barriers. In fact, 30% of Canadians live within walking distance of a public library, making us one of the most accessible public institutions in the country.
Public libraries are deeply embedded in their local contexts and connected to hundreds of service providers and community organizations. For example, Pickering Public Library partners with agencies that support newcomers, people experiencing homelessness, teens, 2SLGBTQIA+ residents, and other equity-deserving groups. These partnerships allow libraries to extend their reach and impact, ensuring that no community is left behind. Our strong, collaborative network enables us to deliver national-scale initiatives while remaining responsive to local needs.
As AI-generated content becomes more prevalent, Canadians of all ages need the skills to navigate and think critically about this emerging technology. Public libraries are uniquely positioned to meet this need. No other public institution works as universally across generations, and our existing platforms allow us to deliver both in-person and online training programs. 
Public libraries are more than just places to borrow books—they are democratic spaces for gathering, ideating, and debating. Libraries are ideal venues for hosting community discussions on AI ethics, facilitating public input into policy development, and fostering inclusive dialogue that reflects Canada’s diverse values. Federal investment in public libraries is an investment in a national AI literacy infrastructure—one that ensures all Canadians are equipped to thrive in an AI-driven future.

1. Digital Literacy: Basic digital skills are foundational across all sectors—understanding how to use digital tools and technologies effectively. Key Skills: Productivity software, digital communication tools, remote collaboration platforms, and data management.

2. Responsible AI Literacy: As AI systems become more pervasive, understanding their ethical implications—such as fairness, accountability, and transparency—will be essential across all sectors. Key Skills: Knowledge of AI ethics, bias mitigation, privacy concerns, and social impact of technology.

3. AI and Data Science Expertise: With the rise of AI and big data, the ability to analyze data, build machine learning models, and deploy AI solutions is critical. Key Skills: Machine learning, deep learning, natural language processing (NLP), data visualization, statistical analysis, and programming (Python, R, etc.).

4. Analytical and Problem-Solving Skills: As AI and automation transform industries, the ability to analyze and interpret complex data is essential, not only for technical roles but also for decision-makers across sectors. Key Skills: Critical thinking, data-driven decision-making, systems thinking, and creative problem-solving.

5. Build Resilience and Future-Proof the Workforce: With the rapid pace of technological change, the ability to adapt to new tools and platforms will be essential. This requires ongoing learning and flexibility. Potential Government Actions:
 - Workforce Resilience Programs: Develop resilience programs that promote lifelong learning. These programs should incentivize workers to continuously upskill and remain adaptable to technological shifts.
 - Technology Adoption Support: Create programs that help traditional industries (e.g., manufacturing, construction, agriculture) adopt digital tools and automation solutions. This could involve grants for tech adoption, as well as training workers in new technologies that improve efficiency and productivity.
column3: Learn from Global Initiatives: Canada can align itself with international efforts in AI literacy and ethical development by adopting best practices from global initiatives and contributing to global AI governance. This can be done through additional investment into supporting the Global Partnership on AI.

2. Targeted Outreach to Marginalized and Underserved Groups: CEIMIA and the GPAI have produced supporting material that can help facilitate these initiatives, like the Towards Substantive Equality in AI: Transformative AI Policy for Gender Equality and Diversity report: https://wp.oecd.ai/app/uploads/2025/05/towards-substantive-equality-in-artificial-intelligence_Transformative-AI-policy-for-gender-equality-and-diversity.pdf
- Community-Led AI Awareness Campaigns: Engage community leaders and local organizations to run AI literacy programs in marginalized communities. These programs can build trust and encourage participation, making AI less intimidating and more accessible
- Indigenous-Led AI Initiatives: Develop AI literacy programs specifically for Indigenous communities, with culturally relevant content and local knowledge integration, like AI applications that support Indigenous language preservation, resource management, and community development
- Support for Women: Encourage programs that provide mentorship, networking, and job placement opportunities for women in AI and tech fields. Companies can partner with non-profit organizations and educational institutions to provide AI internships and job shadowing opportunities
- Access to Assistive Technologies: Provide AI-driven assistive technology training for persons with disabilities, allowing them to better access digital tools and services, promoting inclusion in the workforce, education, and society at large

3. Building Awareness Around Ethical AI: AI literacy programs should teach AI’s ethical dimensions and implications for AI systems. Similarly, public debates and discussions about AI’s social impact, particularly concerning its potential harms would improve the public’s awareness of these issues

column2:
The widespread adoption of AI will fundamentally change Canada’s workforce. Already, nearly 3 in 10 Canadians report using AI tools in the workplace and Statistics Canada estimates that around 60% of occupations have high exposure to AI.
A university education helps people secure good jobs, earn strong salaries and build better futures. In the age of AI, a university education also gives Canadians the skills to adapt to and integrate new AI technology into their workplaces. Examples of such skills include critical thinking and problem-solving, digital literacy, adaptability, communication and collaboration.
According to estimates by Statistics Canada, the majority of jobs that require a bachelor’s degree or higher, including doctors, nurses, teachers and electrical engineers, have high complementarity with AI. This means that AI will augment these jobs rather than replace them.  
 With over 50 AI-related programs across the country ranging from micro-credentials to doctorates, Canadian universities are preparing workers with the AI skills that can augment human decision-making and create a more productive economy.
column3:
Canada needs to use a multiprong approach to enhance AI literacy.
1)	Formal education: Across the country engineering and computer science departments at universities offer specialized streams in BEng and BSc programs so that Canada’s future engineers can use AI to power the economy. 
2)	Work integrated learning (WIL): universities and their industry partners provide students with WIL opportunities, benefiting both students and industry. Students get the opportunity to hone their technical expertise in a workplace environment while  WIL partners can use emerging AI talent to improve productivity.
3)	Micro credentials and Workplace training: Beyond providing formal education, universities across the country and the three major AI institutes offer micro-credential and certificate courses in AI, machine learning and data analytics. These programs give workers and companies cutting edge digital skills to help streamline business processes.  
Examples:
o	Vector’s FastLane program hosts a Privacy Enhancing Techniques bootcamp, which gave healthcare start-up A.I. Vali the tools to train an AI model for their cancer detection device without having to share patient data between hospitals. Support from Vector allowed A.I. Vali to innovate in the healthcare sector while ensuring that their AI model is used responsibly.vi Mila offers multiple public programs in AI, including Indigenous Pathfinders in AI, a career pathway program designed to inspire Indigenous talent to learn, develop and lead the evolution of AI and the AI Policy Compass which helps policy and industry stakeholders understand the technical and political landscape of AI. Amii has trained over 250,000 learners through its foundational courses in AI and machine learning. Amii also offers custom training to industry partners to help them integrate AI into their products and processes.
Leveraging on and expanding on these existing programs will be vital to expand AI literacy in Canada.
column4:
As centres of AI learning and expertise, universities are ready and willing to partner with government and industry to expand access to AI literacy across regions, demographics and socioeconomic groups. There are many actions that stakeholders, including government, businesses, and educational institutions, can take  to expand the   AI literacy and proficiency in Canada. 
Expanding Work Integrated Learning opportunities would create a beneficial feedback loop for students and businesses, especially for SMEs. Students would gain valuable work experience while also introducing AI knowledge and skills to businesses, especially in sectors that are less likely to use generative AI technology.  Industry would also be able to share with universities  the skills that are currently missing and how might they be applied in the workplace. 
For Canadians who are no longer in school, Canada  must lower barriers for businesses and individuals to make reskilling and upskilling more attractive and accessible. Universities across the country are already providing micro-credentials in AI, machine learning and data analytics. Providing workers across the country with cutting edge digital skills will make them more productive while providing businesses with more confidence in AI technology to make them more streamlined and innovative.
Finally, Canada must ensure equitable access across the country to the infrastructure and technology needed to leverage AI tools. This includes access to compute infrastructure for all researchers regardless of the size or location of their institution and access to AI tools for SMEs, nonprofits and industries. In addition to improving access technology and AI tools, the Government of Canada can also play a helpful role in expanding equity by ensuring that data sets from under-represented regions including the North and rural areas are input into AI tools to help improve AI tool outcomes.

column2:
Labour predictions are impossible.  Policy makers need to humbly recognize that nearly every time an occupation has been predicted to become irrelevant, the timeline and/or the technological impact was too optimistic.  This leads to a near term shortage and the adoption of less desirable secondary solutions.  We also need to recognize that some predications, especially those espoused by an industry, are self-fulfilling. 

The question seeks an answer in the modern, digital economy, but we’ve long had a shortage of trades in many areas in Canada.  I propose there is an appreciable percentage of white collar knowledge workers that may actually derive immense satisfaction from learning a trade.  We could perhaps learn something from the Swiss vocational system where people participate in both apprenticeships and classroom-based learning and an engineer is often someone who was initially trained as a machinist.

In more vague terms, we should encourage education paths that train students in ways to think and approach problems in a more general sense. These are usually education programs that have historically had unclear career prospects (other than academia).  Physics and philosophy are two that come to mind.

In the strictest sense, “AI-focused” careers will be a very small percentage of the workforce. It would be the equivalent of seeking to be ready for “cloud-focused” careers 15 years ago.  

There is a large group of occupations where AI cannot replace humans or that we may want to resist this replacement.  We should direct a great deal of effort to attract both the existing and future workforce to these roles, especially those that have a positive impact on human quality of life.

Canada is facing such a moment right now.  The situation with Canada Post is a result of technological shift and automation in the face of inflexible historical norms.
column3:
To begin, what do we mean by “AI literacy” will need to be defined. We leverage automobile for transportation and also on them.  Are all drivers “car literate” or only those that do their own oil changes or have read the owner’s manual?  Or do we speak of the mechanics who can actually troubleshoot and resolve problems?

I don’t believe AI literacy is required from the public to achieve our goals. As technology has improved, we have become less “car literate” - fewer people change their own oil than ever before and oil level checks are done electronically by the car’s system.  Nearly everyone uses the cloud in some way, understands the concept of it, but we haven’t done anything to increase “cloud literacy”, beyond the advertisement of cloud providers. 
This is not to say that there are not any real dangers in terms of limitations, biases, misinformation, fake content, etc.  But the guardrails and the safeguards are required of the systems that perform the functions, the systems that distribute them and the processes that use the outputs. 

In the end, literacy is a very individual trait - it’s unfair and misguided to pin this requirement on the public. Our politicians and policy makers, however, should absolutely be AI literate, but they have so far often fallen short of it.  Let’s start there.

• Core literacies: data & statistics, computational thinking, prompt design, model evaluation & monitoring, cybersecurity/privacy, product thinking, UX, plus deep domain knowledge.
• Human differentiators: critical thinking, clear writing/speaking, ethics & safety judgment, collaboration across disciplines, change management, entrepreneurial mindset.
• Role tracks: AI/ML engineering (incl. MLOps & data engineering), applied science, AI product management, AI safety/evaluation, data governance & compliance, and “AI-enabled operators” in health, energy, finance, government.
• Programs that work: stackable micro-credentials mapped to roles; paid apprenticeships/residencies (6–12 months) inside hospitals/utilities/ministries; co-op expansion; modular bootcamps aligned to national standards; compute & dataset credits for learners; recognition of prior learning; portable “learning accounts” that workers can spend on approved courses.
• Rapid adaptation: a 90-day curriculum update cadence with industry advisory boards; outcome-based funding tied to job placement, wage uplift, and on-the-job AI adoption.
column3:
• Workplace training (short, role-based): fundamentals; where AI fails; prompt hygiene; bias/fairness basics; data protection; human-in-the-loop and escalation; audit trails & incident reporting.
• Credentials that signal trust: a national baseline “AI Literacy” badge; sector modules (health/finance/public safety); an “AI Buyer” certificate for managers who procure or approve systems; an “AI Operator” cert for frontline users.
• Public awareness: deepfake/media authentication campaigns; free “AI 101” via colleges/libraries; toolkits for teachers and small businesses; K–12 age-appropriate projects that emphasize limitations, not hype.
• Borrow what works internationally (adapt to Canada): NIST AI RMF for risk language, ISO/IEC 42001 for AI management systems, “Elements of AI”–style open courses, Singapore’s verifiable testing mindset (e.g., AI Verify–like evaluations for high-risk use).

column3:
Create a multi-channel national AI literacy program that combines mandatory, role-specific training for regulated professions, employer incentives for workforce upskilling, and public-facing awareness campaigns that explain AI’s capabilities and limits in plain language. This reduces misuse, improves procurement, and increases responsible adoption.
Operational levers (how)
•	Mandatory short-certificates for regulated sectors (health, finance, public procurement) requiring demonstration of competency in interpreting AI outputs, understanding bias, and oversight responsibilities.
•	Employer training credits: refundable tax credits or wage subsidies for firms that fund accredited AI-literacy programs for employees (managers, procurement officers, analysts).
•	Micro-credential ecosystem: fund the development of stackable micro-credentials (data literacy, MLOps basics, model-risk oversight) delivered by colleges and online platforms with accreditation standards.
•	Public campaigns & toolkits: plain-language guides, interactive simulations, and “explain-an-AI” sandboxes for citizens to test how algorithmic decisions are made and contested.
•	Sectoral playbooks: downloadable templates showing typical failure modes, data requirements, and KPIs for safe AI deployment in industries (health, agriculture, energy, government services).
•	Embed in education by offering provinces funding to deliver programming to in schools to students of all ages.  Specific conditions and metrics would need to be negotiated with the provinces but this would show leadership in AI. 
Measures of success
% of procurement officers certified; number of accredited micro-credentials issued; employer uptake of training credits; public awareness survey improvements on AI limits/bias.
column4:
Recommendation (what & why)
Make AI literacy nationally accessible and targeted: fund mobile/regional training hubs, subsidize programs for underserved groups, and require inclusive design in training delivery to close geographic and socio-economic gaps.
Operational levers (how)
•	Regional AI learning hubs: fund local centers (post-secondary partnerships) in each province/territory and in Indigenous communities that deliver in-person and hybrid training.
•	Upskilling vouchers: targeted, income-adjusted vouchers for low-income, Indigenous, rural, and underrepresented groups to obtain accredited AI micro-credentials.
•	Employer–community partnerships: encourage large employers to sponsor cohorts from local communities with matching government funds.
•	Accessible formats: require course providers to offer materials in multiple languages, accessible formats, and flexible scheduling (evenings, modular).
•	Digital inclusion: pair literacy funding with broadband and device subsidies where connectivity inhibits participation.
Measures of success
Training completion rates by region/demographic; uptake of vouchers; post-training employment or retention metrics.

column3:
Enhancing AI literacy in Canada requires treating it as a public good—a foundation for civic participation and responsible innovation. Literacy must go beyond coding or workplace skills to include critical understanding, ethical awareness, and the ability to question how AI systems affect daily life.

1. Start with public understanding and equitable access. Canada ranks 44th of 47 countries in AI literacy and 42nd in public trust (KPMG Canada, 2025). These low scores show a gap between Canada’s AI research leadership and public understanding of its uses and risks. National literacy efforts should be part of a digital rights and inclusion agenda, ensuring access to reliable information across regions, languages, and socioeconomic groups. Programs should use plain-language communication to explain how AI works, its limitations, and how algorithmic bias shapes outcomes. Literacy is not just about “how to use AI,” but about how to interpret and question it.

2. Pair literacy with transparency and engagement. Literacy should be reinforced by visible transparency mechanisms so citizens can apply what they learn. Public AI registers, dashboards, and standards like Digital Trust for Places & Routines (DTPR) help residents connect learning to real-world AI deployments. When people can see how AI is used in their communities—and have ways to ask questions—they build informed confidence rather than fear.

3. Canada should align its literacy strategy with global frameworks that emphasize human-centred, rights-based education. The OECD AI Principles highlight fairness, transparency, and accountability in both regulation and learning (OECD, 2019). UNESCO’s AI Ethics Recommendation (2021) and AI Competency Framework (2023) call on states to teach bias awareness, accountability, and sustainability. Aligning with these frameworks links technical knowledge with civic responsibility, preparing Canadians to engage critically and confidently with AI across sectors.

column2:
Enable rapid adaptation to technological change.
The most critical skill is continuous adaptability. Support should focus on upskilling initiatives that emphasize devoted safety and knowledge in AI interaction, enhancing prototyping skills, and embedding this practical education across learning platforms. A lifelong learning tax credit remains a useful tool, empowering workers while creating a traceable investment in human capital adaptable to dynamic technological shifts.

Programs for both AI-focused careers and broader workforce readiness.
Canada needs a differentiated approach. Broader workforce readiness, focusing on essential AI literacy and safe usage, can largely be delegated to the private sector (for employee-specific training) and the general education system. To cultivate AI-focused careers, government should prioritize expanding post-graduate programs and consider using research funding credits tailored toward industry needs to shape specialized academic pathways and incentivise talent development in strategic areas.
column3: Canada can enhance AI literacy by embedding practical education on AI’s capabilities, limitations, and biases across workplaces, schools, and the public sphere. Employers can introduce short, credentialed training programs that teach how to interpret model outputs, recognize bias, and apply responsible AI principles in daily operations. Universities and colleges can expand micro-credentials and co-op placements focused on explainability, fairness, and auditing, ensuring students graduate with both technical fluency and ethical awareness. Improved support for co-op programs that expose students to applied AI applications will help bridge the gap between classroom theory and real-world use, giving future professionals firsthand experience in responsible AI deployment. Public campaigns and community workshops can complement this by helping Canadians understand where AI excels and where it fails.

If we think of an LLM like a web search 2.0, then unlike the list result of a simple Google search, the answers that come back from an LLM are convincing. This adds complexity to training because individuals may not be aware or agree that they need it.

Canada needs to acknowledge this challenge and, despite this challenge, provide education and skills that enable informed decision-making on opportunities associated with AI adoption, upskill the current workforce, and equip the next generation to benefit from AI technology to assist them in their work. This will result in a more robust workforce.

column2:
Rapid adaptation (and adoption) is valuable... if the technological change is positive. Financial incentives to switch to LED bulbs when they still cost a fortune was both effective and smart. Swapping to a transient or unreliable system usually isn't. 

You are talking about AI like a monolith. It's not. It's like electricity. Completely life- and society-altering in massive, unforeseen ways AND driving products ranging from effective and largely beneficial (LED lamps) or therapeutic (SAD lamps) to fairly neutral (battery-powered toys) to dangerous or actively harmful (tasers).

Differentiating among these would be a necessary life skill.

As I said before, lifelong "school" provides avenues for ongoing training and relevance of the adult workforce, while helping maintain mental agility and facility with fluid knowledge (rather than crystallized knowledge.)

The most urgent "rapid adaptation" that needs to happen is a societal shift towards a healthy skepticism regarding technological bubbles, and a promotion of foundational skills in the tech industry, like systems architecture, logic, math, how to interpret statistics, and programming best practices, with an eye to sustainability, longevity, and product life-cycle planning that includes user-serviceable, long-lasting products.
column4:
See my response about lifelong learning and adult 'school'. (Note, such programs should heavily promote distance learning, for logistical, practical and inclusion reasons.)

Digital literacy in general requires equitable access to proper computers (not just cheap consoles or phones) and internet access, as well as accurate information that is not proffered based upon ad spending, political expedience or its incendiary nature (i.e. social media algorithms). It also requires proper, pedagogically-sound training, to wade through mis- and disinformation.

Moreover, AI literacy requires awareness of technological tools and limitations, absolute transparency about AI-generated or ('assisted') content, and the evolving informational and legal landscape.

Treat all of these like utilities, e.g. basic human rights, and add competence with them to basic curricula for all ages. Offer incentives to adults who don't attend school, and to companies for including appropriate adult curricula in their ongoing/onboarding training.

As AI (media/digital literacy in general) require effective critical thinking skills, remove incentives or special status to groups that undermine critical thinking. (Religious groups that promote dogma based upon faith over explicit reason based upon empirical observation comes to mind, but certain political groups share this trait as well.)

column2: Canada needs a workforce skilled in AI, data science, machine learning, software engineering, and digital problem-solving, alongside broader skills like critical thinking, adaptability, and collaboration. Support can come through targeted education programs, reskilling initiatives, apprenticeships, and partnerships between academia, industry, and government to enable rapid adaptation to technological change. Programs should address both AI-focused careers and general digital literacy for the broader workforce.
column3: AI literacy can be enhanced through workplace training programs, certification and credentialing initiatives, public awareness campaigns, and integration of AI ethics and explainability into curricula. Learning from international best practices, Canada can provide structured educational pathways for both professionals and the general public, emphasizing the limitations, biases, and responsible use of AI systems.
column4: Equitable access requires collaboration across federal, provincial, and municipal governments, along with industry and private-sector partners. Strategies include funding for underserved regions, online and flexible learning programs, community-based training initiatives, and inclusive curricula that address diverse populations. Ensuring AI literacy is accessible to all Canadians helps foster informed confidence and supports inclusive participation in the digital economy.

column4: Collaboration and investment from government to community to business to academia across the country.

I know some of it has been worked on, but I don't think we have high-speed internet everywhere in Canada yet. So that's the first step. 
Next, knowledge in general has to come first. K-12 need understanding BEFORE they need computers thinking for them. So AI has no place with developmental education. 
After that, digital literacy and training are usually part of area studies (academic, trades, industry, etc) If you're going to replace those, you're going to have a lot of service industry and not a lot of tech leadership. 
More support for education in general will do a lot of this lifting for you.

column2: Roundtable participants emphasized the need for cross-disciplinary expertise that integrates neuroscience, AI, data science, and ethics. They called for interdisciplinary training programs and fellowships to build technical and translational skills, particularly for scientists and clinicians applying AI to health research. Participants also stressed that workforce development must include data stewardship and governance skills, not only algorithmic development, to ensure sustainable and responsible AI implementation.
column3: Roundtable participants noted that AI literacy among clinicians, researchers, and decision-makers is crucial for safe and effective deployment. They proposed targeted professional training, integration of bias-awareness modules into existing programs, and public-facing initiatives to explain how AI models make decisions and where their limits lie. They emphasized that understanding bias and interpretability is central to maintaining ethical and trustworthy use of AI in neuroscience and health.

column2: A modern digital economy requires more than coding and data science it needs inclusive, ethical, and adaptive skills that prepare workers to lead, regulate, and coexist with AI. Canada should invest in digital and financial literacy, data stewardship, applied AI ethics, and interdisciplinary education that bridges technology, social impact, and human rights. Workforce development must prioritize women, survivors, and gender-diverse people, whose economic participation strengthens resilience and innovation. This includes re-skilling programs for workers in precarious sectors, mentorship pipelines for underrepresented groups, and public-private partnerships that offer paid training, micro-credentials, and community-based digital upskilling.
column3: Canada must foster both technical understanding and critical awareness of AI’s limitations, biases, and risks. Public education should go beyond how AI works to include how it affects privacy, employment, and economic security. Embedding AI literacy across school curricula, professional certification, and workplace training can prevent harm and promote confidence. Public awareness campaigns co-led by academia, survivor-led organizations, and Indigenous communities can make AI transparent, human-centered, and responsive.
column4: Equitable AI literacy begins with equitable access. Federal, provincial, and industry collaboration can fund free or low-cost literacy programs delivered through libraries, community hubs, and gender equity organizations. For women and survivors of economic abuse, accessible AI literacy means safer participation in digital banking, entrepreneurship, and employment. Programs must be culturally relevant, multilingual, and trauma-informed, bridging divides that prevent equitable engagement in the digital economy.

column2:
Building a competitive, AI-ready workforce requires Canada to adopt a multi-faceted strategy that cultivates talent with the multi-disciplinary, applied, and professional skills the modern economy demands.
1) Cultivate multi-disciplinary talent fluent in both AI and industry applications: To bridge the gap between Canada's world-class research and its industrial application, the national strategy should focus on developing innovators fluent in both the language of cutting-edge AI and the language of a strategic sector. Innovation-focused internships are a critical mechanism for achieving this, as they provide top researchers with the opportunity to apply their knowledge and skills in a real-world industrial setting. At scale, this approach could mirror successful international models, such as AI Singapore's 100 Experiments (100E) program, which deploys elite AI specialists directly into industry to accelerate the commercialization of novel, high-value solutions in under a year.
2) Prioritize applied skills for AI commercialization: To close the commercialization gap, Canada needs to develop talent skilled in building, deploying, and scaling practical AI solutions. This requires a blend of technical proficiency with strong business acumen and expertise in responsible AI and governance, including AI ethics, human-centric design, and bias detection. 
3) Support lifelong learning and workforce upskilling: To support Canada's nation-building ambitions in areas like energy and defence, the national AI skills strategy must extend beyond post-secondary students to include the existing workforce. This requires a national effort to support accessible, industry-aligned upskilling and reskilling programs that provide pathways for current workers to gain the foundational AI literacy needed to boost productivity and adapt to a changing economy.
column3:
Canada should address its national weakness in AI literacy by launching a national mission to build practical, applied AI skills at scale, treating it with the same urgency as a major nation-building project. This requires a coordinated, multi-level strategy that learns from international best practices.
1) Launch a national mission to enhance AI literacy: To address Canada's persistent productivity gap and accelerate the slow pace of technology adoption by businesses, Canada must transform its national AI literacy deficit into a competitive strength by launching a formal national mission to promote widespread AI literacy. This mission must move beyond ad-hoc programs to create a cohesive, national framework for skills development that empowers every citizen, accelerates business adoption, and ensures that the economic benefits of AI are widely shared. 
2) Adopt a multi-level national curriculum, learning from international best practices: Canada should learn from successful international models to create a comprehensive curriculum tailored to different audiences, including K-12 education, the existing workforce, and the general public.
3) Prioritize experiential learning for Highly Qualified Personnel (HQP): The national strategy should prioritize scaling work-integrated learning to embed skilled talent directly into organizations to enhance AI adoption and commercialization. This approach can enhance the ambient AI literacy of the entire team and provide practical, contextual learning about AI's capabilities, limitations, and biases. This literacy must include a sophisticated understanding of responsible AI principles, including ethics, governance, and human-centric design.
column4:
A national AI literacy strategy must intentionally promote equitable access from the outset to ensure all Canadians share the benefits of the AI revolution. This requires a multi-pronged approach focused on coordinated governance, targeted programming, and national delivery.
1) Mandate equity as a core principle of the national AI literacy strategy: Equity in AI literacy cannot be an afterthought; it must be a non-negotiable design principle for all national AI initiatives. This requires formal, top-down collaboration between federal, provincial, and municipal governments to ensure equitable access to both AI technologies and training. 
2) Deliver the national AI literacy mission through a place-based and culturally responsive model: The mission must be focused on building workforce AI readiness for all Canadians. To achieve this, it must be delivered through a model that reaches every community. The government should therefore provide dedicated funding to scale existing, proven Canadian initiatives with a track record of reaching underserved communities and geographies. Organizations like Mitacs have demonstrated success in reaching equity-deserving groups through its programming and delivery. 
3) Leverage sovereign infrastructure to help bridge the digital divide: Equitable access to AI literacy training is meaningless without equitable access to the underlying technology. The national strategy must ensure that the rollout of the Canadian Sovereign AI Compute Strategy includes specific provisions for regional nodes, subsidized access for underserved communities, and partnerships with Indigenous communities.

column3: The federal government can support AI literacy through education, democracy forums, funding for nonprofits to conduct training and development. KHP is building our mental health literacy programs to include AI literacy. For example, KHP has developed a partnership with Google to support the expansion of our Counsellor in the Classroom (CITC) program. CITC is a free program for grades 4-12 to promote the discussion of mental health and well-being among young people. KHP will use Google's Be Internet Awesome curriculum to develop CITC sessions for youth on building safer digital worlds and strengthening communities. KHP recognizes that we must meet young people where they’re at, online and offline, and we pursue partnerships and collaboration opportunities to do so at every turn.

Enhancing AI literacy means deepening information literacy — the ability to evaluate, question, and contextualize information in all its forms. Librarians are experts in this work. At Ottawa Public Library, our staff teach residents how to consider information and data security, to assess credibility and validate, to identify misinformation, and understand how data systems shape what they see and believe.

Our services introduce emerging technologies through a lens of inquiry. By fostering curiosity alongside healthy skepticism, libraries help residents recognize information risk, bias, and limitation — in AI and beyond.

A national strategy for AI literacy should therefore invest in Canada’s library network as a cornerstone for lifelong, community-based education. Through accessible, inclusive, and trusted learning spaces, libraries ensure that Canadians of all ages develop the critical awareness required to navigate digital environments responsibly.
column4:
Equitable access to technology begins with equitable access to knowledge and learning. Public libraries form Canada’s most extensive educational network — more than 3,300 branches across cities, towns, rural and remote areas — free and open to everyone.

At Ottawa Public Library, we reach residents in every neighbourhood and offer multilingual programs, digital literacy training, and targeted support for newcomers, seniors, and those experiencing housing or financial insecurity. Our Bookmobile and Homebound Services extend learning opportunities to those unable to reach a branch.

Equity is about more than internet access; it is about having the skills and confidence to learn, question, and participate. Libraries deliver this through open learning, collaboration, and inclusive design. Federal collaboration with public libraries would prevent a two-tier digital literacy system and guarantee that every Canadian — regardless of geography or income — can develop the critical thinking and digital skills essential to thrive in a rapidly changing world.

column3:
Post-secondary institutions have a role to play in educating students about the appropriate uses of AI. That said, many young and sometimes most vulnerable populations will not be reached by post-secondary education, either at the college or university level. It is therefore essential to consider how elementary and secondary schools address the changing face of AI in preparing young people. This requires placing considerable resources into training the trainers. Teachers are a fundamental source of AI literacy and need ongoing professional development before they can impart a coherent AI strategy to youth. 

It is also important to look beyond educational institutions as sites of literacy. Lifelong learning is important for protecting vulnerable older adults and for reaching those who have not attained considerable formal education. Initiatives such as the Dais’ Democracy@Work program are innovative methods for delivering literacy from trusted sources outside of traditional educational settings.

column2: Critical skills include data literacy, model understanding, prompt engineering, ethics-aware design, and cross-functional product integration skills that connect technical and business knowledge. Development should be supported through scalable micro-credentials, employer-led apprenticeships, and incentives for continual upskilling tied to industry needs. We should expand public-private partnerships with shared training platforms and subsidized access for SMEs. Encouraging modular credentials recognized by employers will accelerate workforce redeployment.
column3: We need to implement national media and data literacy programs that include specific modules on AI limitations, bias, and responsible use, tailored for different audiences. Industry-supported workplace training and sector-specific playbooks will help businesses implement bias mitigation techniques. Funding public-facing tools and campaigns that demonstrate practical examples of AI strengths and risks is important. Promoting evaluation frameworks and independent audits will allow citizens to see evidence of bias mitigation in high-impact systems. We must foster partnerships with provinces and schools to incorporate AI literacy into curricula.
column4: We must deploy federally funded, regionally delivered training hubs and mobile learning units to reach underserved communities and ensure they have broadband-enabled access. Partnering with industry to provide subsidized tools, mentorship, and internships will connect learners to employers. Funding for community organizations and Indigenous partners should be prioritized to develop culturally relevant curricula. We need to measure reach and impact with transparent equity key performance indicators (KPIs) and adjust programs based on outcomes. Leveraging provincial education authorities will help scale curriculum integration and credential recognition.

column3:
To effectively enhance AI literacy in Canada, particularly addressing the crucial need for awareness of AI’s limitations and biases, the federal government must invest in the nation’s existing, trusted institutions: public libraries. Librarians are professional educators with decades of expertise in information literacy and source verification, skills directly transferable to identifying AI-generated misinformation, such as deepfakes, which pose threats to individuals and our political systems. Leveraging this expertise creates the national AI literacy infrastructure Canada needs.
Public libraries provide the necessary judgment-free spaces where Canadians of all ages can safely develop the critical evaluation skills required to navigate and think critically about AI content. Because libraries represent a cornerstone of Canada’s democracy, they are ideally suited to facilitate community input on AI policy and host AI ethics discussions, ensuring public understanding of bias and limitations reflects Canadian values. Furthermore, no other public institution works as significantly and universally across generations, allowing us to reach youth, seniors, newcomers, and vulnerable populations. This essential multigenerational training is already active, with 16,000 people attending technology programs at public libraries every month.
To maximize this impact and ensure equitable access, federal investment in library AI capacity—specifically librarian training and programming resources—is crucial. This investment will utilize our unparalleled reach of over 3,300 locations to deliver public literacy programs coast-to-coast, protecting the social fabric of our communities.
column4:
Achieving equitable access to AI literacy requires leveraging an infrastructure that is already coast-to-coast, universally trusted, and open to all demographics. Public libraries fulfill this need completely, operating 3,300+ locations in urban, suburban, rural and remote areas. This unparalleled reach ensures that training is accessible to all Canadians, regardless of income or background, thereby eliminating barriers and preventing the widening of the digital divide. Libraries are community hubs that serve as the primary public infrastructure, often open seven days a week, and are free to everyone.
Crucially, libraries connect with populations often missed by traditional programs. They are hubs connected to hundreds of service providers and key institutions in local contexts, such as agencies serving newcomers and social services. This allows libraries to deliver essential, multigenerational training to youth, seniors, and vulnerable populations, safeguarding Canadian values and the social fabric of communities. Furthermore, libraries are consistently ranked among Canada’s most trusted institutions, making them the ideal setting to foster informed confidence in AI technologies. To maximize this equitable impact, federal investment in library AI capacity—including librarian training and programming resources—is essential to support this existing network in delivering public literacy programs to every community.
