column2:
To build an inclusive, future-ready workforce, Canada needs a comprehensive, fully funded national AI skills strategy developed in partnership with provincial and territorial governments. This strategy will ensure that Canadians in all regions and at every stage of their careers can access the learning opportunities they need to build AI literacy and technical competency. 
This strategy should establish a coordinated approach across all levels of education, building from K–12 to applied training and professional application in postsecondary education. A whole-of-government, multi-jurisdictional commitment would ensure AI literacy is embedded across curricula, preparing Canadians of all ages and backgrounds to participate confidently in an AI-enabled world. We recommend a progressive multi-focus approach, modeled on successful international frameworks advanced by UNESCO and the OECD, that embeds AI literacy and ethical awareness into the K-12 system. 

At the postsecondary level, this strategy should ensure that learners can progress from foundational AI literacy to advanced, sector-specific applications that drive innovation. The college sector, with unparallelled national reach, will be a critical ally in ensuring that students across the country can further develop their tech literacy skills. In addition to AI specific training, colleges already lead efforts to integrate AI capabilities into all program areas, ensuring that every graduate is ready and able to engage with AI.  By combining colleges’ practical training and strong community focus, these graduates will be positioned to drive innovation and facilitate industry AI adoption in their regions.
A population-wide baseline of AI literacy will strengthen Canada’s agility and competitiveness, enabling workers to identify and lead AI-driven opportunities wherever they arise. Expanding current training efforts under a unified vision will enable the full integration of AI across sectors and regions.
column4:
Preventing a digital divide must be a central priority of the AI strategy. While building foundational AI literacy in early education is essential, it must be complemented by targeted supports for Canadians already in the workforce. To ensure meaningful and broad adoption of AI, the government must provide working Canadians with support to access AI training learning opportunities.
The government has existing mechanisms, such as the Canada Training Credit (CTC) and the Canada Student Financial Assistance Program (CSFAP), that can serve as a foundation for supporting adult learners. However, the structure and eligibility criteria of these programs often do not align with the short-term and flexible learning formats that are best suited for mid-career learners.
To make it possible for these Canadians to retrain and keep pace with an AI-integrated economy, the government should: 
•	Amend the CTC so that training funds flow to applicants before they start their studies, ensuring mid-career learners have access to the short training programs that are best suited for rapid upskilling and reskilling.
•	Modernize the CSFAP to include eligibility for microcredentials and other short-term training options, so Canadians can upskill and reskill without leaving the workforce. 
•	Make further adjustment to the Student Financial Assistance Program to better reflect the realities of older students. Building off the Skills Boost program, we recommend providing an additional $1,600 in non-repayable grants to students returning to study who have been out of high school for at least 10 years. In addition, we recommend ensuring the previous income and spousal income is not used to disadvantage mid-career workers for learners needs assessment and maintaining a reasonable income threshold for assistance repayment, so workers feel confident in their ability to return to the workforce after upskilling.

column2:
A modern digital economy requires a workforce that combines strong technical knowledge with adaptable, human-centered skills. Canada’s success will depend on developing workers who understand technology and can apply it responsibly in industries that remain vital to our economy, such as manufacturing, aerospace, transportation, energy, and health care.

The most valuable skills are not limited to coding or data science. Workers need digital literacy, problem-solving, and critical thinking abilities that allow them to work safely and effectively alongside new technologies. Skills in areas such as predictive maintenance, robotics, data analysis, cybersecurity, and AI system oversight are becoming essential. Just as important are communication, teamwork, and ethical reasoning - skills that ensure technology supports people rather than replaces them.

Canada should strengthen education and training at every level. Public investment in universities, colleges, and trade schools must be matched with new programs that integrate AI and digital tools into traditional technical training. Short, modular training and micro-credential programs can help workers in mid-career update their skills quickly. These initiatives should be developed in partnership with industry and labour so that training reflects real workplace needs and leads directly to employment opportunities.

Unions and employers can work together to deliver practical on-the-job learning that prepares workers to adapt to continuous technological change. Governments should also support retraining for those whose roles are being automated, ensuring no worker is left behind as industries modernize.
column3:
Improving AI literacy in Canada requires helping people understand how the technology works, where it can be useful, and where its limits and risks lie. Canadians need to be aware that AI systems are not neutral, that they can reflect the biases in their data, and that human judgment remains essential in every setting where AI is used.

Government, educators, employers, and unions should work together to make AI education accessible to everyone. Public awareness campaigns can explain AI in practical terms, showing how it affects daily life in areas such as health care, transportation, and employment. The goal should be to give people enough understanding to ask questions, recognize potential bias, and make informed choices when interacting with automated systems.

In the workplace, training programs should teach employees how to use AI tools responsibly and understand their limits. This includes knowing how to interpret AI-generated outputs, check accuracy, and maintain safety and fairness. Labour and industry partnerships can help design these programs so that they reflect real job requirements.

Schools, colleges, and universities should also include basic AI literacy in their curricula. This would prepare students to enter the workforce with a balanced understanding of both the benefits and the challenges of AI.

Canada can learn from countries that have successfully introduced national AI literacy strategies by combining education, workforce training, and public engagement. When Canadians understand AI’s strengths and its shortcomings, they are better equipped to use it responsibly, hold institutions accountable, and ensure that the technology continues to serve society’s broader interests.
column4:
Ensuring equitable access to AI literacy means making sure that every Canadian, regardless of region, income, or background, can understand and benefit from new technologies. Canada must treat AI education as a public good, not a privilege available only in major cities or to those working in high-tech industries.

The federal government should work with provinces, territories, municipalities, and Indigenous governments to build consistent national standards for AI education while allowing flexibility to meet local needs. Funding should support public schools, colleges, and community programs in both urban and rural areas so that all Canadians have access to the same level of digital and AI learning.

Industry has a responsibility to contribute as well. Companies that use or develop AI should help fund and deliver literacy programs through partnerships with unions, postsecondary institutions, and training centers. These programs can provide free or low-cost learning opportunities for workers and young people, including those in trades and manufacturing, where technology adoption is accelerating.

Community organizations, libraries, and public learning hubs can help reach groups that may not have regular access to formal education or digital infrastructure. Targeted outreach should focus on women, newcomers, Indigenous communities, and older workers, ensuring that all groups can participate in and benefit from Canada’s digital economy.

AI literacy must go beyond technical training. It should include understanding of ethics, privacy, and fairness, helping Canadians engage with technology confidently and critically. By combining government coordination, industry participation, and local delivery, Canada can create an inclusive foundation for AI learning that leaves no region or community behind.

column2:
Canada must ensure that its workforce is not only digitally capable — but ethically and civically prepared to operate alongside synthetic systems.
In addition to technical and data fluency, the following skill domains are essential:
1. AI Safety and Governance Literacy
-Understanding how synthetic systems are regulated, certified, and limited.
-Knowing how to challenge misuses, trigger redress, and identify risk behaviors like drift or behavioral nudging.
-Recognizing manipulative interface patterns like infinite scroll, autoplay, or trust simulation — and knowing how to opt out or report violations.
2. Ethical Deployment and Human-in-the-Loop Design
-Building systems that keep humans involved in decision-making loops.
-Designing interfaces with override, audit, and refusal functionality as defaults.
-Understanding the limits of delegation to automated or adaptive AI systems — and when critical decisions must remain human-led.
3. Public-Sector and Civil Society Collaboration
-Training for AI oversight roles, public service auditing, and safety-critical licensing.
-Supporting workers in journalism, law, education, and public policy to meaningfully engage with AI governance issues.
-Investing in regional training hubs and open-access programs that build literacy and trust beyond industry.
Canada can build a world-class workforce by prioritizing alignment, safety, and sovereignty alongside speed or scale. Let’s train people not just to use AI — but to govern it.
column3:
Canada can lead by embedding literacy in three core areas:
1. Formal Education (K–12 and Post-Secondary)
-Teach students to recognize when they are being manipulated, profiled, or emotionally nudged by synthetic systems.
-Include instruction on bias, hallucination, redress, system provenance, and refusal mechanisms.
-Emphasize that outputs from AI systems can appear confident while being entirely false or misaligned.
2. Public Awareness and Community Access
-Launch a national public literacy campaign, using trusted institutions like libraries, community centers, and media.
-Create programs similar to the Community Access Program to deliver free, community-based education on AI risks and rights.
-Provide special emphasis on algorithmic attention traps like infinite scroll, autoplay, and synthetic influencers.
3. Workplace Literacy and Targeted Training
Develop modular training programs for workers across sectors — with distinct tracks for:
-High-risk AI deployment roles (with licensing),
-General workplace literacy (understanding what systems are allowed to do),
-Redress procedures and escalation pathways.
International best practices should focus on empowering people to say no to AI systems, not just how to use them. A literate public is the first line of defense against harm.
column4:
Equitable access to AI literacy must be treated as a national priority — not just for innovation, but for protection, empowerment, and public rights awareness.
To achieve this, Canada should:
1. Build a Modern Public AI Literacy Network
-Create a federally supported public access network — modeled in spirit after the Community Access Program (CAP), which in the late 1990s provided free internet access and digital literacy training in underserved communities.
-Modernize this approach for the synthetic era by offering free, accessible education on AI safety, behavioral manipulation, redress, and refusal rights — especially in rural, remote, and lower-income regions.
2. Partner Across Levels of Government and Civil Society
-Collaborate with provincial governments, Indigenous communities, libraries, schools, and trusted nonprofits to co-develop and deliver localized content in multiple languages.
-Require industry to contribute funding and curriculum access for systems deployed in the public.
3. Center Marginalized and At-Risk Populations
Design targeted outreach programs for:
-Seniors, newcomers, and low-literacy individuals,
-Youth in under-resourced schools,
-Communities historically impacted by biased or extractive systems.

Canada must treat AI literacy the same way it once treated public internet access: as a foundational civic right. The ability to understand and resist synthetic manipulation is not optional — it's modern self-defense.

This level of early and widespread literacy — especially among young adults — may also inspire the next generation of ethical AI practitioners, encouraging innovation grounded in safety, rights, and public trust.

A workforce competent in technological skills is critical for the effective use of ARC systems and for translating those skills to broader industry applications.  Ongoing DRI training is necessary for both novice and experienced DRI users to maintain a workforce proficient in rapidly evolving areas such as AI. Digital literacy is more than being able to use current digital tools – it must include understanding how to approach problems and facilitate industrial solutions through digital means 

Leverage training for researchers delivered by the national DRI ecosystem. Training in effective use of continually evolving hardware and software is essential for AI practitioners and researchers. Skills related to optimizing software performance, designing complex computational workflows, running jobs across multiple GPUs, using supercomputers, and managing large amounts of data are generally not taught in universities or colleges. But these are among the skills taught by the regional DRI organizations.  Without training - only a very limited set of researchers will have the ability to exploit the hardware to anywhere near its true potential

With students trained in high-tech skills, Canadian industry benefit from a workforce which is currently proficient yet adaptable to continuing technology changes. Training must be continually updated to meet changes in research and industrial needs as well as those in the technology

Both new and experienced AI users require specialized training to make effective and efficient use of HPC systems and AI/ML applications. Experienced researchers specifically require training to enable them to scale up research problems and to make best use of the latest hardware and software which changes significantly on 1–2-year timescales.  

The regional organizations partnered with DRAC delivered 25,000 participant-hours of training last year covering topics from data preparation to machine learning and neural networks.

From the tech perspective, there will be a rapid shift away from traditional coding to agentic product engineering and context design & engineering. This will require a new breed of developers who can work alongside AI and understand organizational systems development. We are moving away from encoding rules in software to building context for systems of LLMs. From a human perspective, enablement programs (individual or at an organizational level) will be needed to help with this time of transition.
People need to learn how to actually use computers, how they work, how to directly engage with the systems involved rather than just letting an app do things for them under the hood.

column2: I think Canada needs two intertwined skill stacks: (1) AI/tech-core skills, data literacy, Python/SQL, cloud, API/MLOps basics, prompt+retrieval engineering, cybersecurity hygiene, product thinking, and evaluation/safety; and (2) human advantage skills, problem framing, statistics-for-decisions, UX, communication, ethical reasoning, and change leadership. To keep pace with fast tech shifts, I’d fund short, stackable micro-credentials that “snap into” college/university credit, pair them with paid work-integrated learning, and guarantee compute and datasets so learners build real projects. For mid-career workers and SMEs, I’d co-fund employer cohorts that upskill whole teams (not just one person), tie training to a live use case with a 90-day ROI target, and create a national skills wallet that stores credentials workers can carry job-to-job. Public service should mirror this with AI fellowships and rotations so capability spreads across departments.
column3: To build informed confidence, I want tiered AI literacy: a plain-language baseline for everyone (how systems work, common failure modes, privacy, bias, and when to escalate), a practitioner track for power users (evaluation, red-teaming checklists, prompt/RAG safety), and a leadership track (risk, governance, and procurement). Deliver it where people already are: inside workplaces, colleges, libraries, and newcomer programs, plus free, mobile-first micro-lessons that include hands-on labs with synthetic data. Every course should teach limits and bias by design (counterfactual tests, drift alarms, “nutrition labels,” and incident reporting). I’d recognize learning with portable badges, align content to international frameworks (so skills travel), and require vendors selling AI into Canada’s public sector to fund user training and publish clear model cards and usage guardrails.
column4: Equity starts with access to devices, connectivity, and compute: I’d expand community tech hubs (through libraries, colleges, and Friendship Centres) with free GPUs-in-the-cloud seats, evening childcare, and stipends so low-income learners can attend. Provinces and municipalities can co-fund local delivery; the feds can set standards, content, and credential portability. Industry’s role is to provide datasets, mentors, and paid projects, especially for rural and northern communities, Indigenous partners, newcomers, and people with disabilities, plus commit to skills-first hiring where credentials aren’t a barrier. Schools should embed AI literacy across K-12 with accessible materials (bilingual, plain language, and screen-reader friendly), while adult-learning programs offer flexible, bite-size modules that stack toward diplomas. Finally, we should publish open dashboards on uptake and outcomes by region and demographic so we can see gaps early and move resources to where they’ll matter most.

column3:
The goal of AI literacy in Canada should be to properly align Canadians’ trust of AI with the trustworthiness of these systems. Alignment involves giving people the tools and information they need to critically analyze the AI products that are being marketed or suddenly appearing in their existing apps. At the same time, adoption is not the necessary outcome of AI literacy programs. Rather, the goal should be to ensure Canadians can meaningfully assess the risks and benefits of adopting AI. This is central to ensuring Canadians’ constitutional rights are respected. 
We recommend standing up a research pillar at SAIRI, the AI research institute we have proposed elsewhere in our responses, focused on researching and developing AI literacy strategies. These strategies could include policies, a resource hub, curricula, and public awareness campaigns. All AI literacy strategies should educate Canadians about the risks and benefits of a wide range of AI systems, not just LLMs. 

We recommend incentivizing the development of K-12 curricula that critically examines AI. Children are increasingly exposed to AI in social media applications, education technologies, and other tools, and are particularly vulnerable to its effects. We also recommend incentivizing the development of undergraduate courses on the sociotechnical aspects of AI at all public Canadian universities and colleges. 

We recommend the establishment of workplace AI training programs to ensure that workers in all sectors are aware of AI-related risks and can critically engage with the deployment of AI tools to assist their work. 

AI literacy will support responsible Canadian AI adoption and commercialization. As we have discussed in our other responses, greater awareness of the risks and benefits of AI systems will ensure that Canadians properly align their trust in AI with its trustworthiness. This will ensure that Canadians adopt trustworthy AI technologies and promote ethical AI innovation.
column4:
Understanding what “AI literacy” looks like in different social and cultural contexts is key to ensuring equitable access across regions, demographics, and socioeconomic groups. Our team cannot define “AI literacy” for all groups, but we recommend further research to this end. 

We suggest that this research should be conducted at SAIRI, the fourth research institute we have discussed throughout our responses. This research would include a mandate to identify groups in need of AI education and the type of AI literacy that should be made accessible to each group. SAIRI could work with affected stakeholders to produce the required educational materials, tailor them to each audience, and ensure they are available in the language of each group’s choice through translation.

The modern economy demands hybrid technical, ethical, and human-centred skills. CEWIL Canada’s national WIL network directly equips youth to bridge these dimensions.

Core skill domains:
-Technical: Python, cloud computing, machine learning operations.
-Domain: AI-healthcare, AI-finance, AI-sustainability applications.
-Human: Communication, ethics, project management, and teamwork.

Implementation model:
-Integrate WIL into curricula as a credit-bearing bridge between study, work, and entrepreneurship.
-Support micro-credentials recognized across post-secondary and industry partners.
-Prioritize placements in SMEs adopting AI responsibly.

This approach strengthens the AI Strategy’s pillars on talent, adoption, and inclusion, ensuring skills development directly supports Canada’s innovation economy.

Students need the AI-WIL (credited work experiences) that the CEWIL Canada network provides to create our AI-ready workforce.
column3:
AI literacy must move beyond awareness to applied understanding through experience. CEWIL Canada recommends embedding literacy and ethics training directly within WIL placements.

Strategies:
-Require all AI-WIL students to complete bias detection and “failure analysis” modules documenting what AI cannot do.
-Support public communication components, such as student blogs or open presentations explaining AI limitations.
-Offer free workplace AI literacy sessions for employers hosting WIL participants.
-Stack micro-credentials toward nationally recognized qualifications.

This ensures both students and employers become critical, informed users of AI, reinforcing Canada’s leadership in responsible innovation and evidence-based governance.
column4:
Equity in AI literacy requires distributed access and inclusive participation. CEWIL Canada’s iHUB model is a proven model that emphasizes outreach and flexible delivery, while increasing funding to equity-seeking groups.

Actions:
-Expand remote WIL opportunities linking rural students to urban innovation projects.
-Develop an Indigenous AI stream co-designed with communities, offering % wage subsidies, similar to MILA Pathfinders Program, and similarly funded CEWIL Canada iHUB project
-Establish newcomer fast-track programs that combine credential recognition with WIL experience.
-Enforce accessibility standards (WCAG 2.1 AA) across all national WIL digital platforms.

These steps ensure regional and demographic equity in Canada’s AI transition—addressing both inclusion and competitiveness within the AI Strategy framework.

column3:
Public libraries are foundational to Canada’s democratic and social infrastructure, providing trusted spaces where people of all ages come to learn, question, and connect. Libraries excel in teaching information literacy and source evaluation—skills that are directly transferable to recognizing deepfakes, algorithmic bias, and other forms of AI-generated misinformation that pose risks to individuals, organizations, and our political systems. As AI increasingly shapes the information environment, Canadians require the ability to navigate and critically assess AI-generated content. No other public institution engages so consistently and universally across generations, from youth and seniors to newcomers and other vulnerable populations. Public libraries are uniquely positioned to help safeguard Canadian values and uphold the social fabric that strengthens our communities.

Every month, more than 16,000 people participate in technology programs at public libraries, demonstrating the scale of our existing public literacy efforts. Libraries also serve as social spaces for gathering, learning, and democratic dialogue. We can host community conversations on AI ethics, facilitate public input into policymaking, and create environments where diverse voices are included in shaping how AI is used in Canada. As hubs for the inquisitive, the curious, and the creative, libraries provide fertile ground for AI-enabled social innovation and exploration.

With established in-person infrastructure and digital platforms for delivering online training, public libraries can rapidly expand public AI education at scale. By investing in library readiness, Canada can create a national AI literacy infrastructure grounded in trust, critical thinking, equity, and democratic engagement—ensuring that Canadians are not just consumers of AI, but informed and empowered participants in shaping its future.
column4:
The public library sector includes more than 3,300 locations across urban, suburban, rural, and remote communities, forming a unique coast-to-coast network that provides national reach while maintaining deeply local impact. Public libraries are essential community hubs—free, open seven days a week, and accessible to everyone—ensuring that all Canadians, regardless of income, geography, background, or ability, have a place to learn, connect, and grow. This universal access is foundational to equity: nearly a third of Canadians live within walking distance of a public library, and many rely on these spaces to bridge digital, social, and economic divides.

The Guelph Public Library reflects this role within our own community. We work closely with newcomer settlement services, social service agencies, housing support organizations, and local partners across health, cultural, and educational sectors. These partnerships expand our reach far beyond our physical branches, allowing us to meet people where they are and respond to evolving community needs. Our collaborative networks ensure that we can support populations who may otherwise be excluded from emerging digital opportunities, including youth, seniors, newcomers, and residents experiencing housing or income insecurity.

As AI transforms the information landscape, Canadians need the skills to navigate and critically evaluate AI-generated content. No other public institution engages so consistently and effectively across generations as public libraries. The Guelph Public Library already provides technology training and supports digital literacy across all ages, and we have established platforms to deliver both in-person and online learning. With the right support, we can extend this capacity to build community-wide AI literacy—ensuring that every resident, regardless of their postal code or life circumstance, has the opportunity to understand, question, and benefit from the technologies shaping our future.

column2:
The Dangers of “Cognitive Offloading”: GenAI certainly has some legitimate uses in research, in some cases performing types of analysis that traditional methods cannot. This includes programs that use GenAI for transcription of historical records and for analysis of language patterns.  But, as John Warner remarks, however, “everyone I’ve seen using this technology productively” already has “a deep foundation in thinking and reasoning.”  How can a student learn how to craft effective prompts to obtain answers from a GenAI tool and to evaluate the answers, if they routinely come to rely on GenAI to do the difficult thinking for them?
At base, the question – especially for undergraduate students – is not so much what GenAI can do for us, but rather what it does *to* us. The value of undergraduate study does not lie in writing a better essay, in submitting a better product: it’s the process that leads to a finished piece of writing that is an assignment’s purpose. And that process involves cognitive tasks all the way along, from the initial consideration of a topic to the submission of the final product. This includes those steps where students are often encouraged to use GenAI as an aid: brainstorming, refining research questions, literature reviews, source analysis, as well as improving writing. It is important to emphasize the extent to which all these stages are the point of the exercise: that is where the student’s learning happens. Outsourcing any of this essential brain work to GenAI means means that the student’s learning experience is shortchanged.  It is like having a robot lift weights for one at the gym: it may do so efficiently and well, but one’s own strength and fitness do not grow. It’s not the elevation of the weight that is the goal.
column3:
Even when working as its champions promise, GenAI does not produce original conclusions. It instead flattens the interpretative landscape by providing predictable answers that settle on the middle. As Benjamin Breen comments, though he finds some impressive abilities of GenAI not only to read but to interpret historical evidence at a high level, “the architecture of these models, the data that feeds them and the human training that guides them, all converges on the median.”  The analyses are thus “well-informed, but predictable,” not the “boundary-pushing” results that are often touted. As John Warner points out, it’s often at the level of a Wikipedia article. 
Even more disquieting is LLMs’ bias towards certain interpretations and political stances. GenAI reflects the assumptions of its source base. As Ruha Benjamin of Princeton argues, the racial bias of the writings produced by western societies over the centuries are thus implicitly encoded in GenAI’s output.  And sometimes the bias is deliberately trained into the data: a Utah-based education AI company, for instance, has designed a chatbot that impersonates the Holocaust victim and diarist Anne Frank. The German historian Henrik Schönemann, on trying out the bot, found that it urged him “to avoid pinning blame for Frank’s death on the …Nazis.”
column4:
Recent scientific studies have clearly shown that reliance on GenAI tools has a significant negative effect on critical thinking skills. As Michael Gerlich recently argued in a large-scale study, frequent use of AI encourages “cognitive offloading,” a reliance on AI tools to complete problem-solving and decision-making tasks. Gerlich’s research concludes that this does indeed decrease higher-level thinking skills, just as we worry that it will.  Similar results have recently been published from within the world of AI technological research: a group of Microsoft AI researchers led by Hao-Ping Lee has recently concluded from their own study of how Gen AI has been used by workers that  “higher confidence in GenAI is associated with less critical thinking.” Citing a prescient 1982 study by Lisanne Bainbridge, Lee et al. note that  “a key irony of automation is that by mechanising routine tasks and leaving exception-handling to the human user, you deprive the user of the routine opportunities to practice their judgement and strengthen their cognitive musculature, leaving them atrophied and unprepared when the exceptions do arise.” Altogether, Lee et al. conclude that reliance on GenAI tools lessens “independent problem-solving.”  
These are significant dangers for our students and indeed for all Canadians. Especially when the media, governmen, and universities tout the wonders of AI as an “innovative” learning tool,  students without high confidence in their own abilities are likely to doubt that their own efforts could be as good.  This is a feedback loop: if young learners whose analytical capacities have not yet developed grow up using GenAI extensively, it is hard to see how they can acquire those well-honed critical thinking skills an undergraduate degree engenders.

column3: Canada can strengthen AI literacy by drawing on the successful model established within its quantum ecosystem. Leading quantum research institutions have implemented effective outreach programs that have elevated public understanding and engagement—for example, the Perimeter Institute’s initiatives that equip high school teachers with the tools to introduce quantum concepts in classrooms across the country. A comparable approach should be adopted for artificial intelligence. Canadian institutions at the forefront of AI research and development are well positioned to lead coordinated education and outreach efforts that clarify how AI systems function, where their limitations lie, and how biases can emerge. By fostering partnerships among academic institutions, industry, and government, Canada can promote informed public dialogue and build the technical and ethical literacy needed for the responsible integration of AI across society.

Enhancing AI literacy in Canada requires a trusted, inclusive, and accessible approach and public libraries are ideally positioned to lead this effort. As experts in information literacy and source verification, librarians already teach skills directly transferable to identifying deepfakes, understanding algorithmic bias, and critically evaluating AI-generated content. With 16,000 Canadians attending technology programs at public libraries every month, libraries are already serving as informal learning hubs for digital skills. Their reach across generations - from youth to seniors, newcomers to vulnerable populations -makes them uniquely capable of delivering public literacy programs that build awareness of AI’s limitations and risks while promoting responsible use.

Beyond education, libraries offer physical and digital infrastructure for community engagement, dialogue, and innovation. As democratic institutions rooted in public trust, libraries can host AI ethics discussions, facilitate input on policy development, and serve as platforms for online training programs. Their role as social spaces for ideation and debate makes them natural conveners for targeted public awareness campaigns and workplace training initiatives. By investing in library readiness and leveraging their national network, Canada can build a robust AI literacy infrastructure that reflects Canadian values and empowers citizens to navigate an increasingly AI-driven world with confidence and critical insight.
column4:
Ensuring equitable access to AI literacy across Canada requires leveraging existing infrastructure that reaches every region, demographic, and socioeconomic group. With over 3,300 public library locations in urban, suburban, rural, and remote communities, libraries offer coast-to-coast coverage and eliminate barriers to access through free, universally available services. As essential community hubs, libraries are open seven days a week and provide multilingual resources, online training platforms, and welcoming spaces for all. Their deep connections to local service providers, such as newcomer settlement agencies, mental health organizations like CAMH, and social services, enable them to reach vulnerable populations including youth, seniors, newcomers, and those experiencing housing insecurity.

Public libraries are uniquely positioned to support the federal government in delivering AI literacy programs that safeguard Canadian values and strengthen the social fabric of our communities. No other public institution works as significantly and universally across generations. By collaborating with all levels of government and engaging industry partners, Canada can prevent a two-tiered system where postal code or income level determines AI readiness. Libraries provide trusted guidance, safe spaces for exploration, and critical thinking support—ensuring that all Canadians, regardless of background or location, are equipped to navigate and contribute to an AI-enriched society.

column2:
Canada’s modern digital economy requires a combination of technical, digital, and transversal skills. Foundational digital skills such as platform proficiency and enterprise software use are essential across nearly all sectors. Advanced technical skills, including AI and machine learning, cybersecurity, and data analytics are increasingly in demand, particularly in healthcare, manufacturing, agriculture, and finance. Equally important are transversal skills like problem-solving, communication, collaboration, and adaptability, which enable workers to integrate technology effectively into complex, real-world contexts.

Current data shows that Canadian employers face significant shortages of digitally skilled workers: only ~30 % of Canadians feel “very prepared” with workplace digital skills, while 67 % of businesses report difficulty hiring digital talent. Programs like the DIGITAL Supercluster illustrate the value of targeted initiatives, training over 15,000 Canadians in AI, cybersecurity, and digital transformation
.
Canada can best support workforce development through early and inclusive education, embedding digital literacy and AI fundamentals into K 12 and post-secondary curricula, with special attention to underrepresented communities including Black, Indigenous, and racialized youth. Accessible pathways for advanced skills such as micro-credentials, bootcamps, and apprenticeships should complement traditional education, while upskilling and reskilling programs enable mid-career workers to pivot as technology evolves.

Employer-education partnerships can bridge gaps between skills supply and demand, particularly for SMEs, while labor-market tracking ensures training aligns with sectoral needs. Programs must also emphasize inclusion and equity, providing Black-owned businesses and Black citizens with mentorship, technology access, and culturally relevant opportunities.
column3:
Enhancing AI literacy requires targeted, accessible, and inclusive initiatives that reach youth, workers, and the public while addressing both AI capabilities and limitations. AI literacy not only is about understanding technology, but it includes recognizing biases in datasets and algorithms, ethical considerations, and the societal implications of AI adoption.

Workplace training programs and credentials should focus on practical skills in AI use and oversight, paired with ethics and bias awareness, for roles across industries like healthcare, finance, education, and manufacturing. Micro-credentials, short courses, and bootcamps that combine technical training with ethics can help employees and employers make responsible AI decisions.

Targeted engagement campaigns are needed to reach underrepresented communities, including Black youth and Black-owned businesses. Organizations like Black Boys Code are leading the way by delivering AI-focused programs that introduce Black youth to AI, robotics, and machine learning, ensuring early exposure to both technical skills and social considerations. Expanding such programs nationally would cultivate a generation that understands AI’s benefits and limitations, while empowering historically excluded communities to participate fully in the AI economy.

Public awareness campaigns can leverage online platforms, media, and community-based workshops to explain AI in plain language, highlight common pitfalls such as algorithmic bias, and show how AI impacts everyday life. International best practices, such as the EU’s AI Act guidelines, OECD AI Principles, and AI literacy frameworks demonstrate the value of combining regulation, education, and stakeholder engagement to build trust.

Finally, embedding AI literacy into school curricula, professional development, and public programs ensures that Canadians across all ages and sectors are prepared to interact with AI responsibly and challenge biased or unethical applications.
column4:
Ensuring equitable AI literacy requires targeted strategies that reach all Canadians, particularly underrepresented communities, rural regions, and lower-income populations. Collaboration between federal, provincial, and municipal governments is essential to create coordinated programs that integrate AI education into schools, libraries, community centres, and workforce training initiatives. Funding should prioritize regions and communities with historically limited access to technology and digital skills programs.

The private sector can play a critical role by supporting inclusive AI education through partnerships with community organizations, sponsoring mentorship programs, providing technology access, and offering internships or apprenticeships that expose participants to AI in real-world settings. Industry-led training programs should be complemented with public education campaigns that explain AI, its limitations, and its societal impacts in plain language.

Educational strategies should focus on multi-level engagement, from K–12 curricula that introduce AI concepts and ethics, to post-secondary programs, workplace micro-credentials, and public workshops. Programs must also explicitly address barriers for marginalized groups, including Black, Indigenous, and racialized communities, by incorporating culturally relevant materials, mentorship opportunities, and scholarships. Organizations such as Black Boys Code demonstrate how community-rooted initiatives can effectively cultivate AI literacy among Black youth, providing both technical skills and awareness of ethical implications.

Finally, equitable access requires investment in technology infrastructure, including high-speed internet, accessible devices, and digital learning platforms, to ensure no community is left behind.

Digital and media literacy in general are a weak point basically everywhere in global society right now. Improving that from an elementary-school level onwards is not only of great benefit in and of itself, but will also help create a society and workforce better able to evaluate and interpret newer digital technologies. 

While I understand the degree of under-the-hood understanding of computers common among those who grew up in, e.g., the 1970s and 1980s isn't reasonable to expect and has fallen the way of drivers literally understanding the under-the-hood function of their cars, I believe that we do need a basic level of understanding of how modern technologies operate as part of an informed workforce and electorate. People don't need to understand the specifics of how a GPU works, but people *should* understand what "the cloud" is or understand the basic fundamental structures of the internet and the tech industry in much the same way that we try to teach physical geography or basic civics.

column2: Listen to AI experts who are not boosters.

The skills required for the modern economy are twofold: technical (AI literacy, data analytics, cloud computing, cybersecurity) and human-centric (complex problem-solving, critical thinking, collaboration, creativity, and empathy).

Canada can best support their development by creating a "mission-driven" ecosystem, rather than just funding abstract training. A national focus on AI for the "silver economy" and accessibility is the perfect mission to create a pipeline of applied, high-value talent.

This approach directly fuels youth employment in high-skill sectors. For engineering and computer science students, it provides complex, real-world challenges for co-op terms and capstone projects—eg. designing smart wheelchairs, navigation aids for the visually impaired, or assistive robotics. This is far more valuable than generic training and builds a specialized workforce.

Crucially, this mission creates new, essential roles for persons with disabilities. They are not just beneficiaries; they are the expert users and co-designers of this new technology. This supports their employment in high-value roles like specialized quality assurance (QA), UX/UI testing, and product validation, ensuring the tech is practical and effective.

This strategy also builds skills in related professions. For example, by establishing a "National Assistive Technology IP Commons," we create a significant need for specialized legal work. Gov't can partner with law schools and firms to have articling students manage the pro-bono IP transfers and licensing agreements. This gives young lawyers hands-on, public-interest experience in the high-demand field of tech IP.

This model transforms skills development from a simple cost into a self-reinforcing cycle. We build a world-leading industry, solve a major social challenge, and in the process, create a new generation of experienced, purpose-driven Canadian engineers, legal experts, and testers who are rooted in a Canadian-owned ecosystem.
column4:
To ensure equitable access to AI literacy, Canada must move beyond a one-size-fits-all digital approach and focus on community-based, tailored education. A national mission focused on the "silver economy" and accessibility is the perfect vehicle for this.

First, we must mandate accessibility in all materials. Gov't-funded literacy programs must be required to be fully WCAG compliant, with versions available in ASL/LSQ and plain language. This ensures persons with disabilities are not left behind from the start.

Second, we must fund "last-mile" delivery. This means empowering public libraries, seniors' centers, and community hubs—especially in rural, remote, and Indigenous communities—to run free, in-person workshops. This directly targets seniors (a key silver economy demographic) and those with lower socioeconomic status who may lack reliable internet or digital hardware.

Third, the content must be relevant to these specific groups. A national literacy campaign can cover the basics (eg. what is AI, spotting deepfakes), but specialized modules are needed. For seniors, this means focusing on practical skills like identifying AI-driven financial scams and understanding AI in healthcare.

Fourth, we must engage these demographics as partners, not just pupils. We should fund programs that employ persons with disabilities to co-design and deliver AI literacy training. They are best placed to teach others about the specific risks and opportunities, such as how AI bias in hiring algorithms can create new barriers, or how AI tools can be used for assistive technology. This "nothing without us" approach builds trust and creates employment, turning literacy from a top-down mandate into a community-led initiative that reaches every group.

column2:
Développer la littératie numérique – Pour assurer l’accès aux modèles d’IA, le développement de compétences numériques est primordial au succès de la stratégie nationale. Cependant, la littératie numérique doit se développer en concomitance avec l’apprentissage de la langue et les compétences littéraires. Comme l’utilisation de l’IA repose sur l’ingénierie de requête, son utilisateur doit détenir des compétences linguistiques, littéraires et numériques adéquates pour rédiger une requête efficace. Le développement de ces compétences est nécessaire à l’accessibilité numérique.
Financer la création de programmes de formation et de recherche en IA – Pour enrichir le bassin de talents en recherche, la stratégie nationale doit investir dans la création de programmes d’enseignement et de formation accessibles et abordables. L’adoption d’approches transdisciplinaires est la voie à prendre pour assurer la compétitivité du Canada et stimuler l’innovation. Le développement de compétences en IA facilitera la collaboration interdisciplinaire, la création d’experts, et le perfectionnement de la main-d’œuvre.
Concevoir de la formation sensibilisant aux biais linguistiques – La conception et l’entrainement des modèles d’IA soulèvent une variété de biais quant aux données, aux corpus et aux algorithmes. Afin d’assurer sa mise en œuvre optimale, la stratégie doit concevoir une participation et une consultation équitables des CFSM, et de la formation de sensibilisation complémentaire sur les biais linguistiques. Cette formation doit informer sur les biais linguistiques de la conception à la mise en œuvre. 
Créer un réseau de champions universitaires, collégiaux et sectoriels en IA – La recherche en IA requiert une approche transdisciplinaire facilitant la collaboration interfacultaire, interordre et interinstitutionnelle.
column3:
Veuillez consulter le mémoire de l'Association des collèges et des universités de la francophonie canadienne
Renforcer la recherche en français – La valorisation de la recherche en français stimule l’innovation et l’épanouissement des CFSM. Une culture de données probantes est au cœur de la production, la diffusion et la mobilisation de savoirs innovateurs et vitaux à l’apprentissage. Pour entraîner des modèles d’IA bilingues efficaces, le financement de projets collaboratifs et transdisciplinaires valorisera la recherche dans tous les secteurs de la culture et l’économie. La répartition équitable du financement augmentera aussi les retombées sociales et économiques dans les régions rurales et éloignées.

Faciliter la collaboration interordre en recherche – Pour atteindre le plein potentiel de la recherche en français, la stratégie nationale doit faciliter la collaboration interordre en recherche. La recherche appliquée dans les collèges accroît la prospérité locale et agit de levier à l’innovation. La recherche fondamentale dans les universités stimule le développement de connaissances utiles tant à l’apprentissage qu’à la formation de la main-d’œuvre. La conception de modèles d’IA bilingues serait bonifiée avec une collaboration interordre. 

Recommandation 2 : Que la stratégie nationale valorise et renforce la recherche faite en français dans la production et la mobilisation de connaissances.

Recommandation 8 : Que la stratégie nationale prévoie l’élaboration de formation et d’ateliers de sensibilisation aux biais linguistiques en relation avec la conception et la mise en œuvre des modèles d’IA.
column4:
Assurer le respect des droits linguistiques et l’égalité réelle.
Recommandation 15 : Que la stratégie nationale pérennise et accroisse le réseau d’infrastructures de recherche en contexte francophone minoritaire.

Recommandation 16 : Que la stratégie nationale mette en place un système de protection et de surveillance des données linguistiques.

En l’absence de données en contexte francophone minoritaire, il est alors impossible d’établir un aperçu complet des populations, des secteurs, des enjeux, défis et obstacles, et des progrès au sein des CFSM. Comme plusieurs établissements ont soulevé n’avoir ni les infrastructures ni les capacités pour collecter et analyser les données, une meilleure répartition des investissements est primordiale à une participation équitable. Accroître les capacités de collecte de données renforcera les capacités de recherche, et permettra de mieux collaborer avec des partenaires externes.

En matière de recherche et d’infrastructure, la répartition des ressources est inéquitable à l’échelle pancanadienne. Pour participer équitablement à l’élaboration d’outils, de modèles d’IA et à la recherche, il faut renforcer et pérenniser le réseau d’infrastructures en contexte francophone minoritaire. Cela permettrait d’accroître les capacités des établissements, de renforcer la recherche en français, et d’assurer une offre de programmes pérenne.

La collecte, la gestion et l’exploitation des données linguistiques sont un enjeu pour les CFSM. Alors qu’on observe un manque de données sur nos populations, l’accroissement des capacités de collecte nécessitera un système de gestion, de protection et de surveillance pancanadien. Afin de protéger les données recueillies, et les infrastructures, il faut que la stratégie mette en place un système spécifique à la gestion des données linguistiques.

Une économie numérique moderne exige des compétences en littératie numérique, en compréhension critique de l’IA, en adaptation technologique et en détection des biais. 
Le Canada peut soutenir leur développement en finançant des formations accessibles, conçues avec les organismes communautaires comme le CIAFT, qui accompagnent les femmes et les groupes sous-représentés. En misant sur l’inclusion et la formation continue, le Canada peut bâtir une main-d’œuvre prête à relever les défis du numérique.
column3: Pour améliorer les connaissances sur l’IA, le Canada doit investir dans des programmes de formation accessibles et inclusifs, qui intègrent non seulement les compétences techniques, mais aussi une compréhension critique des biais algorithmiques et des limites de ces technologies. Les organismes communautaires comme le CIAFT sont bien placés pour concevoir des formations adaptées aux réalités des travailleuses et des groupes sous-représentés. En favorisant une culture numérique éclairée et responsable, le Canada peut renforcer la vigilance citoyenne et la confiance envers l’IA.
column4: Le Canada peut garantir un accès équitable aux connaissances en IA en soutenant des formations inclusives, accessibles et adaptées aux réalités régionales et sociales. Cela implique de financer des initiatives locales portées par des organismes communautaires comme le CIAFT, qui accompagnent les femmes et les groupes marginalisés. En misant sur des partenariats avec les milieux de l’éducation, de l’employabilité et du développement social, le Canada peut démocratiser l’apprentissage de l’IA et réduire les inégalités numériques.

column2:
To thrive in a modern digital economy, Canada’s workforce must be equipped with a blend of technical, analytical, and policy-oriented skills. Core competencies include software engineering, data science, cloud computing, cybersecurity, human-AI interaction design, and digital infrastructure management. Additionally, expertise in AI regulation, data governance, and digital policy is increasingly critical.

Canada can support the development and deployment of these skills by expanding access to education and training programs across all levels. This includes integrating AI and digital literacy into K–12 curricula, scaling post-secondary programs focused on emerging technologies, and offering micro-credentials and modular learning for rapid upskilling, and embedding AI literacy into all post-secondary courses not just in computer science. 

Ensure that training is aligned with market needs and accessible to diverse learners though partnerships between government, industry, and academia.

These efforts will help build a resilient, future-ready workforce capable of driving innovation, supporting AI adoption, and contributing to inclusive economic growth.
column3:
Enhancing AI literacy requires a national strategy that promotes understanding of both the capabilities and limitations of AI. Early education should focus on critical thinking, ethical reasoning, and the societal impacts of automation. Building on existing digital literacy programs, AI-specific modules should be introduced in schools to foster cognitive development and responsible technology use.

Career colleges and vocational institutions should expand AI-related programs to meet evolving workforce demands. Industries can support just-in-time training and workplace learning to ensure employees are equipped to work with AI tools responsibly.

Executive AI literacy programs for business leaders and policymakers are essential to improve strategic decision-making and governance. These programs should address AI risks, regulatory frameworks, and ethical deployment.

Together, these initiatives will foster a culture of informed engagement with AI across all sectors.
column4:
Canada must take deliberate steps to ensure AI literacy is accessible to all communities. This includes working with Indigenous organizations to co-develop culturally relevant training and capacity-building programs.

Expanding reliable connectivity and digital infrastructure to underserved regions is foundational. AI literacy should be embedded in school curricula nationwide, with inclusive content that reflects diverse perspectives and learning needs.

Canada should also invest in community-led initiatives that address barriers to digital access, such as affordability, language, and disability inclusion. Public libraries, community centres, and local organizations can serve as hubs for digital education and outreach.

These efforts will ensure that all Canadians—regardless of geography, income, or background—can participate in and benefit from the opportunities of the AI-driven economy.

column2:
From CIPPIC's public interest perspective, building a modern and resilient digital workforce requires a holistic skills strategy. This strategy must go beyond technical "AI-focused careers" and equally prioritize the development of critical, legal, and ethical governance skills. A "ready" workforce is not just one that can build AI, but one that can govern, audit, and hold AI accountable. A holistic skills plan should pair broad rights literacy with specialized assurance careers and a well-resourced oversight ecosystem.

Algorithmic & rights literacy for all workers. Scale programs that teach people to critically assess AI outputs, understand notice/explanation and appeal rights for automated decisions, and use clear channels for challenge and redress.

Professional auditing & assurance (a new career track). Seed interdisciplinary training that blends law, ethics, and computer science to produce independent algorithmic auditors/public-interest technologists capable of bias/safety/rights compliance audits and post-deployment monitoring.

Public-interest governance & legal expertise (the oversight layer). Invest in specialist training for lawyers, regulators, and civil-society advocates to strengthen technology law practice, regulatory capacity, and evidence-based interventions - including Indigenous-led governance where relevant.
column3:
Canada should redefine AI literacy as rights-based empowerment. Awareness programs must teach people how to exercise power - to know when AI is involved, to see basic documentation (AIA summaries, evaluations), and to challenge outcomes. Government can make this real by standing up a public AI system registry, mandating public-facing AIA summaries and incident reporting, and publishing clear appeal pathways wherever AI is used in services. In workplaces, literacy must pair skills with employer duties: prior notice, plain-language explanations, and access to human review. These steps are already legible within the Directive on Automated Decision-Making and OPC privacy guidance.

Limits & bias (when to escalate). Teach how data and deployment context drive error and bias, basic validation checks, and clear escalation triggers for human review in high-impact decisions.

Workplace governance basics. Introduce practical controls drawn from NIST AI RMF and ISO/IEC 42001 (risk registers, model cards, incident logs), paired with checklists/templates teams can actually use.

Privacy & consent in practice. Cover lawful bases, notice/explanation rights, opt-out/appeal pathways, and safe data-handling habits (minimization, retention, deletion/portability).

Data & IP origins. Explain provenance, copyright/fair-dealing basics, and why training data transparency matters; teach how to request or verify provenance.

Train-the-trainer kits for unions, libraries, and community orgs; micro-credentials/badges for workplace programs.

Accessible, multilingual materials (WCAG 2.1 AA or better) and scenario-based drills (table-tops, “mystery-shopper” audits of tools in use).

Plain-language notices and a local registry link so learners can see which high-impact systems operate in their community or workplace.
column4:
Real literacy means people can recognize limits, exercise rights, and get help. Close access gaps, design for disability from the start, and fund community institutions to teach critical, rights-centred skills in the places people already go.

Close access gaps; design for accessibility from day one. Tackle rural/remote/Northern connectivity and device affordability; require WCAG 2.1 AA (or better) for all publicly funded tools and materials.

Adopt a rights-based curriculum. Teach limits and risks (bias, reliability), data provenance, and how to challenge or appeal automated decisions (notice, explanation, redress).

Use public institutions as primary educators. Co-fund federal/provincial programs delivered through libraries, schools, colleges, community clinics, and public-interest organizations, with multilingual and plain-language materials.

Mandate transparency learners can use. Maintain a public registry of high-impact AI systems and publish plain-language audit/evaluation summaries so communities can see what’s in use locally.

Equip SMEs with practical supports. Provide templates, checklists, and micro-grants/vouchers for training, DPIAs, and safe deployment practices to reduce skills and cost barriers.

Center Indigenous data sovereignty and community delivery. Support Indigenous-led curricula and programs, aligned with OCAP/CARE and FPIC, in Indigenous languages where relevant, with community control over data and benefits.

Equitable access requires distributed, sovereign infrastructure that serves a broad range of users. A data centre must be designed to support researchers, startups, and SMEs across Canada – not just in major urban centres. Federal government participation as a primary leaseholder would ensure flexible access to compute power for Canadian organizations that need it most, fostering inclusive innovation.

column3: Enhancing AI literacy in Canada requires adopting ISO/IEC JTC1/SC42 standards that establish common terminology, risk management practices, and trustworthiness criteria for AI systems, as well as ISO/IEC JTC1/SC27 standards related to information security. Workplace training programs should emphasize understanding AI limitations, identifying potential biases, and implementing human oversight mechanisms based on SC42 frameworks. Public awareness campaigns must communicate AI's capabilities and constraints using standardized concepts that promote consistent understanding across sectors. Credentials should incorporate SC42-aligned and SC27-aligned assessment methodologies for evaluating AI transparency, fairness, and accountability, as well as information security. By aligning education initiatives with SC42 standards that define systematic approaches to AI governance, bias mitigation, and risk assessment, Canada can build a digitally literate workforce and citizenry capable of critically engaging with AI technologies while recognizing both their transformative potential and inherent limitations.

The required skill set for the modern digital economy moves beyond technical coding to emphasize cognitive abilities, creativity, and the capability to rapidly generate and distribute value in a highly complex and digital environment.

1. Core Cognitive and Analytical Skills
The foundation of the modern skill set is the ability to effectively filter and interpret massive amounts of data and information.

Filtering and Discernment: The critical skill is the ability to understand what is signal and what is noise, and to determine what is important and what is not. This involves high-level analytical and critical thinking skills.

Technological Enhancement: The ability to effectively use and enhance existing technological skills to produce higher degrees of good throughout an enterprise, society, or population.

2. Creative and Problem-Solving Skills
Future economic value will be driven by uniquely human, non-rote capabilities that accelerate problem-solving.

Creative Assets: Core skills include creativity, insight, innovation, and imagination. These are the human attributes that are ultimately going to foster a lot of rapid problem-solving.

Rapid Problem Solving: The ability to swiftly identify, analyze, and implement effective solutions in dynamic, technology-driven environments.

3. Value Creation and Global Distribution Skills
Economic success in the digital economy is defined by the ability to create value and ensure its global distribution.

Digital Value Creation: The ability to add value to a digital framework, such as a blockchain, and then effectively distribute that value globally for sale. This connects technological proficiency with market acumen and global strategy.
column3:
anada possesses a cohort of citizens with the necessary skill and sophistication to articulate an AI skill set that enables societal flourishing. This expertise is deeply rooted in domains that are critical for national resilience and human well-being.

These specialized areas represent where the Canadian capacity for discerning and applying AI for positive impact is most pronounced:

Financial Security and Financial Literacy: Individuals capable of leveraging AI to enhance personal and national financial resilience, moving beyond basic understanding to complex, AI-enabled risk management and wealth creation.

Health and Wellness: Experts who can apply AI ethically to improve public health outcomes, personal well-being, and preventive medicine, ensuring that technology serves the moral imperative of enhancing life quality.

Moral and Ethical Truth: A critical mass of Canadians equipped with a pure and clear ability to discern right from wrong in complex AI deployments. This expertise ensures that AI models are built on objective, values-based foundations, preventing bias and ensuring accountability.

Artistic Expression: Professionals who can harness AI as a tool for new forms of artistic expression and digital creativity, expanding cultural boundaries while exploring the human condition.

This collective skill set allows Canada not just to develop AI, but to apply it in a manner that maximizes human potential and fosters a society defined by high ethical standards and equitable outcomes.

ensure equitable access to AI literacy across all regions, demographics, and socioeconomic groups in Canada, a comprehensive strategy is needed that urgently addresses infrastructure deficits and actively incorporates marginalized communities into the future digital economy.

1. Resolve the Digital Infrastructure Deficit
Physical connectivity remains the primary barrier to equitable AI literacy, particularly in remote and Northern communities, disproportionately affecting Indigenous populations.

Satellite Connectivity Solutions: Government efforts must aggressively promote and invest in high-speed, high-capacity satellite internet solutions (e.g., leveraging services like those offered by Rogers Satellite or future Starlink-style services from Canadian AI champions like Cohere or others).

National Mandate for High-Speed Access: Treat high-speed, reliable internet as an essential public utility, establishing a clear national mandate and timeline for 100% connectivity coverage to ensure remote parts of the country can fully participate in the digital economy.

2. Prioritize and Engage Indigenous Communities
The urgency of global technological acceleration demands immediate and sustained action to prevent Indigenous populations from being further marginalized.

Culturally Relevant Training: Develop and fund AI literacy programs designed in collaboration with Indigenous leaders and educators. These programs must be culturally relevant, accessible, and respectful of Indigenous knowledge systems.

Bridging the Gap: Acknowledge that the Americans, Chinese, and Indians are not going to slow down or wait. Canada must act now to quickly integrate Indigenous peoples into the AI-enabled workforce, reversing the trend of dramatic technological exclusion.

3. Targeted Programs for All Demographics
Equitable access requires more than just infrastructure; it demands tailored educational content and delivery methods for all underserved groups.

column3: On a more personal note: I am a university student. I am enrolled in teacher's college at York university, and I am seeing the effects of AI tools both on my classmates and on my students. Problems or tasks that would once have fostered discussion and personal/collective problem-solving are now outsourced to chatbots like Cortana, ChatGPT, and DeepSeekAI. Let me be clear: students are outsourcing their thinking to chatbots - and this isn't just present in the education world: I have peers who cite concerns in medical and legal fields as well. We are witnessing a generation of otherwise promising academics who are paving their way to success using unethical technology and failing to learn in the process. Would you want to be operated on by a doctor who graduated solely thanks to ChatGPT? Would you want your children to be taught by someone who cared so little about their career that they submitted every assignment using generative AI? This is the reality in which we live. Make no mistake: the anti-AI policies of educational institutions and various companies looking to hire are categorically unable to identify when and where AI is being used by those submitting info to them. Mark Carney's government is making it easier for these issues to compound, and I want to illustrate how deeply I am against these actions. This is a deeply risky endeavor and we don't yet have the data to demonstrate the full scope of the consequences - I haven't even mentioned yet the effects that AI has on mental health and those vulnerable members of our society who are unaware of the dangers, such as children and the elderly who are not as technologically savvy to understand the ramifications of providing information to these sites.

Sourced from (Blit, Goldfarb, Samson, Tapp, 2025) (Policy paper is currently among the Finalists for the Hunter Prize)

To succeed in the coming age of intelligence, Canada must treat AI literacy as a basic civic skill, essential to participate in modern economic and democratic life.

Ottawa should launch a national AI literacy campaign to build awareness, trust, and hands-on competence across the population. These efforts would aim to replace anxiety with confidence, and hesitation with curiosity. Public lectures, community workshops, media partnerships, and national challenges can engage citizens at scale, while open learning platforms can make training accessible to everyone, from students and teachers to small-business owners and retirees. Embedding AI education in school curricula at every level will ensure that the next generation grows up fluent in this technology, rather than fearful of it.

A complementary set of workforce tools are also needed. AI education tax credits, stackable micro-credentials, and online programs can help workers at all skill levels learn to integrate AI into their professions. Executive-level courses can equip leaders with the ability to connect AI adoption to their business strategies and bottom lines. 

Canada’s ability to thrive in the AI era depends on unlocking the creativity of its people. In many businesses, adoption is happening from the bottom up, as workers experiment with new tools and share what they learn. By giving Canadians the skills and confidence to use AI, we can turn this informal experimentation into a national movement, speeding up adoption across sectors and embedding innovation into everyday work. And by empowering all Canadians to use AI, we help ensure that its benefits are widely shared and not just captured by a small technical elite.

Skills that may be useful for a modern, digital economy, as well as any information age economy, would be the ability to think critically and write clearly.

Granted, knowledge of programming skills would probably be useful, but it's hard to say how well some people may be able to adapt or not.

There may also be a case to be made for being proficient in using an operating system that is not proprietary to a company that is headquartered in the United States.  Given where trade talk has gone lately, it would be best to have knowledge of an operating system, such as Linux, that is not proprietary to a U.S.-based corporation.  A dependence on a U.S.-based operating system in order to pursue a modern, digital economy could be a key weakness, particularly in the event that Canada cannot renegotiate a good deal for Canada on CUSMA.

At this point in time, I don't know how well someone might be able to design some sort of programs towards broader worker readiness.  Should workers who fear that they may have their jobs phased out by AI in the next 3 to 5 years be seeking training now?  Yet, in 3-5 years, it's hard to say what such workers should be doing.  Each person would be unique too.
column3:
The Government of Canada could set up online training, perhaps in one-hour or two-hour increments.

It is possible to do a WHMIS course or a FoodSafe course online.  Perhaps it might be possible to do an AI literacy course online too.  Ideally, it would be nice if such a course were to involve no proprietary software -- or at least no proprietary software via a U.S.-based company.  Granted, as a web-based course, such might just depend on the web browser.  Hopefully, such could be compatible with multiple web browsers, whether Firefox, Safari or Chrome, or perhaps Norwegian-based Vivaldi.

As per training in literacy, one early area of AI might be social media, where governments around the world, including Canada, are generally doing a poor job of regulating.  On this front, it might be possible to create a social media literacy online course and then make it available at low-cost or zero-cost.  Then, teenagers might be encouraged to take the course prior to getting themselves a social media account.  (Maybe the social media account creation process should look for a completion code for an online course on social media.)

Perhaps the Ministry of Industry can set up a range of basic AI courses and/or announcements and/or keep track of stats in real-time.  There might be online trivia, with the bank of Q & A material expanding over time as more announcements and data is collected.

The Government of Canada does have an interesting campaign on disinformation at:
  www.canada.ca/disinformation

Would there be a case for setting up some sort of informational resource on AI akin to what is present for disinfo?

At present, the following vanity URLs would be available:
  www.canada.ca/ai
  www.canada.ca/artificialintelligence
  www.canada.ca/artificial-intelligence
  www.canada.ca/killerrobots
  www.canada.ca/ghostinthemachine
